## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Table-Driven Backtrack-free Parser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputBuf["Input Buffer\n(contains remaining tokens + end-marker)"] --> lookaheadCtrl
    lookaheadCtrl["Lookahead Controller\n(reads 1 token ahead)"] --> tableLookup
    stackTop["Parser Stack\n(top non-terminal exposed)"] --> tableLookup
    tableLookup["Predictive Parsing Table M[A, a]\n(L L 1 decision matrix)"] --> actionDecide
    actionDecide{"Action Selector"}
    actionDecide -->|"Production A -> alpha"| expand
    actionDecide -->|"Terminal match"| match
    actionDecide -->|"Empty cell"| errorHandler
    expand["Expansion Unit\n(push RHS onto stack in reverse)"] --> stackTop
    match["Match Unit\n(pop stack, advance input)"] --> stackTop
    match --> inputBuf
    errorHandler["Error Recovery Module\n(panic-mode / synch tokens)"] --> stackTop
    errorHandler --> inputBuf
    actionDecide -->|"A -> epsilon"| epsilonApply["Epsilon Application\n(apply FOLLOW-driven rule)"]
    epsilonApply --> stackTop
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Production Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Need to expand non-terminal A\nwith current lookahead 'a'"] --> q1{"A has\nmultiple productions?"}
    q1 -->|"No"| singleProd["Use the single production\nunconditionally"]
    q1 -->|"Yes"| q2{"Compute FIRST(alpha_i)\nfor each production A -> alpha_i"}
    q2 --> q3{"Does any FIRST(alpha_i)\ncontain 'a' ?"}
    q3 -->|"Yes, uniquely"| chooseProd["Select that production\nM[A, a] = A -> alpha_i"]
    q3 -->|"No, but epsilon in FIRST"| q4{"Is 'a' in FOLLOW(A) ?"}
    q4 -->|"Yes"| applyEps["Apply epsilon production\nM[A, a] = A -> epsilon"]
    q4 -->|"No"| errOut["Syntax Error\n(recover with synch tokens)"]
    q3 -->|"Yes, multiple match"| conflict["NOT Backtrack-Free\n(grammar is not LL 1)"]
    singleProd --> done["Action Selected"]
    chooseProd --> done
    applyEps --> done
    errOut --> done
    conflict --> done
```

---

### 4.3 Transformation Pipeline Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g0["Original Grammar G\n(may have left recursion\nor common prefixes)"] --> t1
    t1["Step 1: Left Recursion\nElimination"] --> g1
    g1["Intermediate G1\n(no left recursion)"] --> t2
    t2["Step 2: Left Factoring\n(if required)"] --> g2
    g2["Transformed G2\n(no common prefixes)"] --> t3
    t3["Step 3: Compute FIRST\nand FOLLOW sets"] --> sets
    sets["FIRST, FOLLOW,\nFIRST+ tables"] --> t4
    t4["Step 4: Build Predictive\nParsing Table M[A,a]"] --> m
    m["Predictive Table M\n(each cell has <= 1 entry)"] --> t5
    t5{"Every non-empty cell\nhas exactly one production?"}
    t5 -->|"Yes"| backtrackFree["GRAMMAR IS LL(1)\n= BACKTRACK-FREE"]
    t5 -->|"No"| conflict2["Not LL(1)\nRequires LR(1) or rewriting"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked to "show the predictive parsing table," examiners expect you to draw it as a **2-D grid with non-terminals on the rows and terminals (including $) on the columns**, with single productions in each populated cell.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define backtrack-free parsing. State the necessary conditions a grammar must satisfy to be parsed backtrack-free.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Backtrack-free parsing is a deterministic top-down parsing technique in which the parser can select the correct production rule for a non-terminal using only the current input symbol (lookahead) — without rescanning or rewinding the input.

> **Conditions (2 marks):** A grammar admitting backtrack-free parsing (i.e., an LL(1) grammar) must satisfy:
> 1. **No left recursion** — no production of the form $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$.
> 2. **No common prefix ambiguity** — for any non-terminal $A$ with alternatives $A \rightarrow \alpha_i$ and $A \rightarrow \alpha_j$ ($i \neq j$), we must have $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$.
> 3. **Disjoint predictive sets** — no cell of the predictive parsing table $M[A, a]$ contains more than one production.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is a predictive parsing table? How is the entry $M[A, a]$ computed when $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ for a production $A \rightarrow \alpha$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Predictive parsing table (1.5 marks):** A predictive parsing table $M$ is a 2-D matrix indexed by a non-terminal $A$ (row) and a terminal $a$ (column, including the end-marker $\$$). Each non-empty entry $M[A, a]$ stores the unique production to apply when $A$ is on top of the stack and $a$ is the lookahead.

> **Computation rule for $\varepsilon$ case (1.5 marks):** If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ for production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, then for **every** terminal $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, we set
> $$M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$$
> In particular, if $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, then $M[A, \$] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ as well. This is the **"$\varepsilon$-in-FIRST"** rule in table construction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q.A (a)** Consider the following grammar $G$:

$$
S \rightarrow a S b S \mid b S a S \mid \varepsilon
$$

**(i)** Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals. **(5 marks)**
**(ii)** Construct the predictive parsing table $M$. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify. **(2 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Demonstrate the parsing of the input string `a b a b $` using the table. Show each move of the stack and input. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)(i) — FIRST and FOLLOW Computation (5 marks)**

Applying the FIRST rules:

| Non-terminal | Productions | FIRST set |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| $S$ | $S \rightarrow aSbS$ | $\{a\}$ |
| $S$ | $S \rightarrow bSaS$ | $\{b\}$ |
| $S$ | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ |
| $S$ (combined) | — | $\{a, b, \varepsilon\}$ |

**Fixed point reached in 1 pass.**

Applying the FOLLOW rules:

- **Rule 1 (start symbol):** $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$.
- **Rule 2** on $S \rightarrow aSbS$: For the inner $S$ (after $b$), $\text{FIRST}(bS) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$ is added. For the trailing $S$, the symbol after it does not exist or we consider what follows $S$ in $S$ itself. By Rule 3, since the inner $S$ is followed by $b$ (non-nullable), and the trailing $S$ is at the end of the RHS, $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ — no new info.
- **Rule 2** on $S \rightarrow bSaS$: Similarly, $\{a\}$ is added to FOLLOW of inner $S$.
- **Rule 3** propagation: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ — fixed point.

$$
\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b, \varepsilon\} \qquad \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{a, b, \$\}
$$

---

**Part (a)(ii) — Predictive Parsing Table (2 marks)**

Apply the construction rules:

| $A \backslash a$ | `a` | `b` | `$` |
|-----------------|-----|-----|-----|
| $S$ | $S \rightarrow aSbS$ | $S \rightarrow bSaS$ | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |

**Justification (LL(1) check):** Each non-empty cell contains **exactly one** production. The three productions populate three **disjoint** cells (keyed on `a`, `b`, `$`). Therefore, **the grammar IS LL(1) and admits backtrack-free parsing**.

---

**Part (b) — Parse Trace for `a b a b $` (7 marks)**

| Step | Stack (top right) | Input | Action |
|------|-------------------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `$ S` | `a b a b $` | $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aSbS$; pop $S$, push $SbSa$ |
| 2 | `$ S b S a` | `a b a b $` | **Match** `a` |
| 3 | `$ S b S` | `b a b $` | $M[S, b] = S \rightarrow bSaS$ |
| 4 | `$ S b S a S b` | `b a b $` | **Match** `b` |
| 5 | `$ S b S a S` | `a b $` | $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aSbS$ |
| 6 | `$ S b S a S b S a` | `a b $` | **Match** `a` |
| 7 | `$ S b S a S b S` | `b $` | $M[S, b] = S \rightarrow bSaS$ |
| 8 | `$ S b S a S b S a S b` | `b $` | **Match** `b` |
| 9 | `$ S b S a S b S a S` | `$` | $M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 10 | `$ S b S a S b S a` | `$` | **Match** `a` |
| 11 | `$ S b S a S b S` | `$` | $M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 12 | `$ S b S a S b` | `$` | **Match** `b` |
| 13 | `$ S b S a S` | `$` | $M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 14 | `$ S b S a` | `$` | **Match** `a` |
| 15 | `$ S b S` | `$` | $M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 16 | `$ S b` | `$` | **Match** `b` |
| 17 | `$ S` | `$` | $M[S, \$] = S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 18 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** At every step, the parser consults **exactly one cell** of the table. The trace confirms **zero backtracking** — the input is consumed left-to-right in a single forward pass.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q.B (a)** Eliminate left recursion from the following grammar and compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for the resulting grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow A B \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow A a \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Construct the predictive parsing table for the transformed grammar. Is the resulting grammar LL(1)? If yes, parse the input string `b a a $`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Left Recursion Elimination and Set Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Detect left recursion.** $A \rightarrow AB$ is directly left-recursive. Apply the standard transformation with $A$'s productions: $\alpha_1 = B$ (left-recursive part), $\beta_1 = b$ (non-recursive part).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow b A' \\
A' &\rightarrow B A' \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\rightarrow A a \mid a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute FIRST sets (3 marks)**

| Non-terminal | Productions | FIRST set |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| $B$ | $B \rightarrow Aa$ | $\text{FIRST}(A) = \text{FIRST}(bA') = \{b\}$ |
| $B$ | $B \rightarrow a$ | $\{a\}$ |
| $B$ (combined) | — | $\{a, b\}$ |
| $A$ | $A \rightarrow bA'$ | $\{b\}$ |
| $A$ (combined) | — | $\{b\}$ |
| $A'$ | $A' \rightarrow BA'$ | $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{a, b\}$ |
| $A'$ | $A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ |
| $A'$ (combined) | — | $\{a, b, \varepsilon\}$ |

**Step 3: Compute FOLLOW sets (2 marks)**

- Start symbol: $A$ is start, so $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- $A \rightarrow bA'$: $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A')$.
- $A' \rightarrow BA'$: $\text{FIRST}(A') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{a, b\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
- $A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$: nothing new.
- $B \rightarrow Aa$: $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ is added to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

Convergence after one more pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(A) &= \{b\} & \text{FOLLOW}(A) &= \{a, \$\} \\
\text{FIRST}(A') &= \{a, b, \varepsilon\} & \text{FOLLOW}(A') &= \{a, \$\} \\
\text{FIRST}(B) &= \{a, b\} & \text{FOLLOW}(B) &= \{a, b\}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Table Construction and Parsing (7 marks)**

**Predictive Parsing Table:**

| $A \backslash a$ | `a` | `b` | `$` |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|
| $A$ | — | $A \rightarrow bA'$ | — |
| $A'$ | $A' \rightarrow BA'$ | $A' \rightarrow BA'$ | $A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| $B$ | $B \rightarrow a$ | $B \rightarrow Aa$ | — |

**LL(1) Verification (2 marks):** All non-empty cells contain exactly one production. **Grammar is LL(1)**.

**Parse Trace for `b a a $` (5 marks):**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `$ A` | `b a a $` | $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow bA'$ |
| 2 | `$ A' b` | `b a a $` | **Match** `b` |
| 3 | `$ A'` | `a a $` | $M[A', a] = A' \rightarrow BA'$ |
| 4 | `$ A' B` | `a a $` | $M[B, a] = B \rightarrow a$ |
| 5 | `$ A' a` | `a a $` | **Match** `a` |
| 6 | `$ A'` | `a $` | $M[A', a] = A' \rightarrow BA'$ |
| 7 | `$ A' B` | `a $` | $M[B, a] = B \rightarrow a$ |
| 8 | `$ A' a` | `a $` | **Match** `a` |
| 9 | `$ A'` | `$` | $M[A', \$] = A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 10 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The input `b a a` generates the leftmost derivation:
> $A \Rightarrow bA' \Rightarrow bBA' \Rightarrow baA' \Rightarrow baBA' \Rightarrow baaA' \Rightarrow baa\varepsilon = baa$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common mistake (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to **push the RHS in reverse order** onto the stack. Students often push $A' b$ instead of $b A'$. Remember: the top of the stack should be the **leftmost** symbol of the RHS.
> 2. **Common mistake (–1 to –2 marks):** Computing FIRST of a multi-symbol string like $\text{FIRST}(aSbS)$ incorrectly as the FIRST set of $S$. The correct answer is $\{a\}$ because the first symbol of the RHS is the terminal `a`.
> 3. **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting to include the **end-marker $\$$** as a column of the parsing table. KTU examiners explicitly test the $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ case.
> 4. **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Stating "grammar is LL(1)" without showing that **every cell has at most one production**. The justification is mandatory.
> 5. **Common mistake (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up the LL(1) table with the LR(0) / SLR table. Remember — for LL(1), rows are **non-terminals** and entries are **productions**; for SLR, rows are **states** and entries are **actions/gotos**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Backtrack-free Parsing**

- **Definition (1-liner):** A parsing strategy that, given a non-terminal and 1-token lookahead, **uniquely** determines the production — **no rescanning**.
- **Class name:** LL(1) — Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 lookahead.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n)$ linear, vs $O(c^n)$ for backtracking parsers.
- **Three mandatory conditions for LL(1):**
  1. No left recursion ($A \not\Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$).
  2. No common-prefix ambiguity ($\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$).
  3. Predictive table has at most one production per cell.
- **Pipeline to achieve backtrack-freeness:**
  Left-recursion elimination → Left factoring → FIRST/FOLLOW → Predictive table → LL(1) check.
- **FOLLOW of start symbol always contains $\$$**.
- **$\varepsilon$-production rule:** If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, place $A \rightarrow \alpha$ in $M[A, a]$ for **all** $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- **LL(1) failure modes KTU loves to test:**
  - Left-recursive grammars (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$).
  - Common-prefix grammars (e.g., $S \rightarrow aB \mid aC$).
  - Grammars with $\varepsilon$ in FIRST of multiple alternatives sharing a FOLLOW.
- **Counter-example to memorise:** The grammar $S \rightarrow A \mid b$, $A \rightarrow aA \mid a$ is **NOT** LL(1) because $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a\}$ overlaps with itself (multiple `a`-prefixed alternatives).
- **Left-recursion elimination formula (memorise verbatim):**
  $$A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid A\alpha_2 \mid \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; A \rightarrow \beta_1 A' \mid \beta_2 A', \quad A' \rightarrow \alpha_1 A' \mid \alpha_2 A' \mid \varepsilon$$
- **Left-factoring formula (memorise verbatim):**
  $$A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; A \rightarrow \alpha A', \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$
- **Practical tool support:** JavaCC, ANTLR, and Coco/R all implement LL($k$) table-driven parsers with **automatic conflict detection**.
- **Bottom-up analogue:** LALR(1) (used in YACC/Bison) achieves backtrack-freeness through a different mechanism (states + LR items), not LL(1) tables.
- **Push the RHS in REVERSE** so that the leftmost symbol ends up on top of the stack.
- **Match operation** = top of stack equals lookahead ⇒ pop and advance input.
- **Error recovery:** Use the **synchronising tokens** = $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ for non-terminal $A$ when popping during panic-mode recovery.
- **Examination mantra:** *"FIRST for choosing, FOLLOW for $\varepsilon$, disjointness for LL(1)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking, Recursive Descent Parsers, Table-Driven LL(1) Parsers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking, Recursive Descent Parsers, Table-Driven LL(1) Parsers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Left-Factoring — Formal Definition **Left-Factoring** is a systematic grammar transformation technique used in syntax analysis to eliminate the common left prefixes (common initial symbols) shared between two or more alternative productions of the same non-terminal. It is the canonical preparatory step for converting an ambiguous or backtracking-prone grammar into a form suitable for **predictive top-down parsing**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme, PCCST601 — Module 2):** > *"Left-factoring is a grammar transformation that defers the decision of which alternative production to apply until the parser has seen enough of the input to make the correct choice, thereby removing backtracking."* ### Formal Grammar Rule If a non-terminal $A$ has productions of the form: $$A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2 \;\vert\; \dots \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_n \;\vert\; \gamma$$ where $\alpha$ is the **longest common prefix** shared by two or more alternatives, then we rewrite the grammar as: $$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \gamma$$ $$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2 \;\vert\; \dots \;\vert\; \beta_n$$ Here, $A'$ is a freshly introduced non-terminal that absorbs the divergent suffixes. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Factor"?** Just as we factor $ab + ac = a(b + c)$ in algebra, we "factor out" the common prefix $\alpha$ from the right-hand sides, leaving the differing parts as separate alternatives of a new non-terminal. --- ## 1.2 Recursive Descent Parser — Formal Definition A **Recursive Descent Parser (RDP)** is a top-down, syntax-directed parser in which **each non-terminal of the grammar is implemented as a recursive procedure (function)**. The parser works by reading input tokens from left to right and recursively expanding non-terminals, choosing the appropriate production based on the current lookahead token. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *"A recursive descent parser is a collection of mutually recursive procedures, one for each non-terminal of the grammar, that simulate the derivation of the input string by a leftmost derivation."* **Key Constraint:** A pure recursive descent parser can only parse **LL(1) grammars** — that is, grammars where the parser can always determine the correct production to apply by examining only the **first symbol** (one token of lookahead) of the still-unparsed input. --- ## 1.3 Table-Driven LL(1) Parser — Formal Definition A **Table-Driven LL(1) Parser** (also called a **Predictive Parser**) is a non-recursive, table-based implementation of an LL(1) parser. It uses a **two-dimensional parsing table $M[A, a]$** indexed by a non-terminal $A$ and a terminal $a$ (the current lookahead), where each entry contains either a production to apply or an error indicator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "LL(1)" Acronym Decoded:** > - **First L** → scans input from **L**eft to right > - **Second L** → produces a **L**eftmost derivation > - **1** → uses **1** token of lookahead --- ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Analogy Imagine you are at a road junction (a non-terminal $A$) with multiple possible exits (alternative productions $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_2$). | Concept | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **Backtracking** | You take the first exit, drive 2 km, realize it's wrong, come back, then take the second exit. Wasted effort! | | **Left-Factoring** | The road sign is rewritten so the common initial stretch is shared, and the fork appears later — you only commit once you see a unique landmark. | | **Recursive Descent** | You follow a printed checklist: "At intersection, call subroutine `RoadA()`; inside it, call `RoadB()` for the next intersection..." | | **Table-Driven LL(1)** | You carry a **map** ($M[A, a]$) that, given your current position ($A$) and the next visible road sign ($a$), instantly tells you which road to take. No recursion, no backtracking. | --- ## 1.5 The Backtracking Problem — Root Cause A top-down parser chooses a production by looking at the input. **Backtracking** occurs when: 1. The chosen production fails to derive the input. 2. The parser must "undo" the choice and try the next alternative. 3. This wastes time and, worse, can be **exponential** for deeply nested ambiguities. > [!WARNING] > Backtracking makes a parser **impractical for production compilers**. LL(1) grammars, prepared via left-factoring, eliminate this entirely. --- ## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in Compiler Design - **Foundation of all top-down parsers** used in real-world languages (e.g., early C, Pascal, JavaCC-generated parsers). - **Interview staple** — almost every compiler/PL placement interview asks about LL(1) tables. - **KTU Module 2 is exclusively dedicated to predictive parsing** — this topic alone carries **~20-25%** of the syllabus weight. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Left-Factoring Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic Given a context-free grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, left-factoring proceeds as follows: **Step 1 — Identify the Non-Terminal.** For every non-terminal $A \in V$, examine all productions $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ in $P$. **Step 2 — Compute the Longest Common Prefix (LCP).** Find the longest string $\alpha$ that is a prefix of two or more right-hand sides of $A$. This can be visualized as grouping all RHS that begin with the same token sequence. **Step 3 — Extract the Common Prefix.** Replace every production of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_i$ with the factored form: $$A \rightarrow \alpha A'$$ $$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2 \;\vert\; \dots \;\vert\; \beta_n$$ where $\beta_i$ is the **suffix** of the $i$-th alternative after removing $\alpha$. **Step 4 — Preserve Unaffected Alternatives.** Productions that do not begin with $\alpha$ are kept unchanged. **Step 5 — Repeat.** Re-examine the new grammar. The newly introduced non-terminal $A'$ may itself require left-factoring. Iterate until no non-terminal has a common prefix among two or more of its productions. --- ## 2.2 FIRST and FOLLOW Sets — The Heart of LL(1) These two sets are the **mathematical foundation** of every predictive parser. ### 2.2.1 FIRST(α) — Definition $$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{\, a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\} \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a \gamma \text{ for some } \gamma \,\}$$ In words: **FIRST(α)** is the set of all terminal symbols that can appear as the **first symbol** of any string derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha$ can derive $\varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$. ### 2.2.2 FOLLOW(A) — Definition $$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\, a \in T \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \text{ for some } \alpha, \beta \,\}$$ In words: **FOLLOW(A)** is the set of all terminals that can **immediately follow** the non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form. If $A$ can be the rightmost symbol in a derivation, the end-of-input marker **\$** is in FOLLOW(A). > [!NOTE] > **\$** is a special pseudo-terminal appended to the input string to mark its end. It is **always** in FOLLOW of the start symbol $S$. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Definition / Formula | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **LL(1) Condition** | $A \rightarrow \alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ is LL(1) iff $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$ | Decides if a grammar is predictively parseable | | 2 | **Table Entry Rule** | $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ iff $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Populating the LL(1) parsing table | | 3 | **ε-Production Rule** | $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ for all $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Handling empty productions | | 4 | **FIRST of Terminal** | $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ | Base case for terminals | | 5 | **FIRST of Sequence** | $\text{FIRST}(X_1 X_2 \dots X_n) = \text{FIRST}(X_1) \cup \text{FIRST}(X_2) \text{ if } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(X_1) \dots$ | Computing FIRST for RHS | | 6 | **FOLLOW Rule 1** | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | Start symbol baseline | | 7 | **FOLLOW Rule 2** | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Direct follow propagation | | 8 | **FOLLOW Rule 3** | If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ with $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Inherited follow | | 9 | **Left-Factoring Transform** | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2 \;\vert\; \gamma$ becomes $A \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \gamma$ and $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \;\vert\; \beta_2$ | Eliminates common prefix | | 10 | **LL(1) Grammar Test** | Grammar is LL(1) if parsing table $M$ has **at most one entry** in every cell | Validates predictive parsability | --- ## 2.4 Recursive Descent Parser — The Procedure Skeleton For each non-terminal $A$ in the grammar, we write a procedure $A()$: ``` procedure A(): choose a production A → X1 X2 ... Xn for each symbol Xi in the RHS (left to right): if Xi is a terminal 'a': if lookahead == a: advance() // match else: error() if Xi is a non-terminal B: B() // recursive call ``` > [!NOTE] > In a **true** recursive descent parser (no backtracking), the "choose" step must be **deterministic** — driven by the LL(1) table or `if-else` chains over the lookahead. --- ## 2.5 Table-Driven LL(1) Parser — The Architecture The parser has three components: 1. **Input Buffer** — contains the input string followed by $\$$ 2. **Stack** — initially contains $\,\text{\$}\,S\,$ (end-marker below the start symbol $S$) 3. **Parsing Table $M[A, a]$** — a 2D array mapping (non-terminal, terminal) pairs to productions 4. **Output** — the sequence of productions applied (the leftmost derivation) > [!TIP] > The stack's top symbol and the current input symbol jointly drive every action. This makes the parser **explicitly table-driven** and easy to trace by hand — exactly what KTU exams test. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of LL(1) Parsing | |---|---| | **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** | Configuration files, build scripts (e.g., early Make, JSON) | | **Expression Evaluators** | Hand-written calculators, formula engines in spreadsheets | | **Compiler Front-Ends (Educational)** | Wirth's Pascal compiler, classic textbook compilers | | **Data Validation** | Structured input validation in network protocols | | **Teaching Tool** | Foundation for understanding all parsing; precedes LALR(1) in syllabus | > [!NOTE] > Modern production compilers (GCC, Clang) use more powerful **LALR(1) or LR(1)** bottom-up parsers. However, **LL(1) remains the standard teaching vehicle** because it makes the left-to-right, top-down intuition visible — exactly the KTU Module 2 mandate. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Left-Factoring in Full **Given Grammar $G_1$:** $$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; E - T \;\vert\; T$$ This is the classic arithmetic expression grammar with **left recursion** (covered in another sub-topic, but shown here for completeness). Even after left-recursion elimination, the result often requires left-factoring. ### Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion Group left-recursive alternatives and the non-left-recursive one: $$E \rightarrow T \;\vert\; E + T \;\vert\; E - T$$ Apply standard left-recursion elimination: $$E \rightarrow T E'$$ $$E' \rightarrow + T E' \;\vert\; - T E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ ### Step 2: Identify Common Prefix in $E'$ The productions of $E'$ are: $$E' \rightarrow +TE' \;\vert\; -TE' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ Here, the **left-factored form is already in place** because the prefixes are distinct ($, -,$ and $\varepsilon$). So $E'$ does **not** need further factoring. But consider a different grammar. ### Step 3: A Grammar That Genuinely Needs Left-Factoring **Grammar $G_2$:** $$S \rightarrow iE tS \;\vert\; iE tSeS \;\vert\; a$$ $$E \rightarrow b$$ (Adapted "if-then" / "if-then-else" ambiguity.) ### Longest Common Prefix Computation The RHS starting with the same prefix: - $iE tS$ - $iE tSeS$ The common prefix is $\alpha = iE t$. The suffixes are: - $\beta_1 = S$ - $\beta_2 = eS$ The third alternative ($\gamma = a$) has no common prefix, so it stays as-is. ### Step 4: Apply the Left-Factoring Transformation Replace: $$S \rightarrow iEtS \;\vert\; iEtSeS \;\vert\; a$$ With: $$S \rightarrow iEtS\,S' \;\vert\; a$$ $$S' \rightarrow eS \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ ### Step 5: Verify No Further Factoring Needed - $S$ has alternatives $iEtS\,S'$ and $a$ → distinct first symbols, OK. - $S'$ has alternatives $eS$ and $\varepsilon$ → distinct first symbols (except $\varepsilon$ handled by FOLLOW), OK. - $E \rightarrow b$ has only one production, OK. > [!TIP] > **Always re-examine** the new non-terminal $S'$ — it may itself exhibit common prefixes and require another round of factoring. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Complete FIRST/FOLLOW Computation **Given the Left-Factored Grammar $G_2'$:** $$S \rightarrow iEtS\,S' \;\vert\; a$$ $$S' \rightarrow eS \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ $$E \rightarrow b$$ **Terminals:** $\{i, t, e, a, b, \$\}$ **Non-terminals:** $\{S, S', E\}$ ### Computing FIRST Sets **Rule Application — First Pass:** **FIRST(b):** Since $b$ is a terminal: $$\text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$$ **FIRST(E):** From $E \rightarrow b$: $$\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$$ **FIRST(iEtSS'):** $i$ is a terminal, so: $$\text{FIRST}(iEtSS') = \{i\}$$ **FIRST(a):** $$\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$$ **FIRST(S):** Union of FIRST of all RHS: $$\text{FIRST}(S) = \text{FIRST}(iEtSS') \cup \text{FIRST}(a) = \{i, a\}$$ **FIRST(eS):** $$\text{FIRST}(eS) = \{e\}$$ **FIRST(ε):** $$\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$$ **FIRST(S'):** Union of FIRST of all RHS: $$\text{FIRST}(S') = \text{FIRST}(eS) \cup \text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{e, \varepsilon\}$$ **No changes on second pass. FINAL FIRST SETS:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(S) &= \{i, a\} \\ \text{FIRST}(S') &= \{e, \varepsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(E) &= \{b\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Computing FOLLOW Sets **Initialization:** $$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$$ **Rule 2 Application (Direct Follow):** From $S \rightarrow iEtS\,S'$: - The symbol immediately before the end $\,\text{\$}\,$ on the stack derivation context is $S'$, so the actual placement is `S → i E t S S'`. Looking inside this production: - After $E$: comes $t$, so: $$\text{FIRST}(t) = \{t\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E)$$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{t\}$$ - After $S$ (the first $S$ in $iEtS\,S'$): comes $S'$, so: $$\text{FIRST}(S') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{e\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$, e\}$$ - After $S'$ (the second symbol): nothing follows within the production, so apply Rule 3. **Rule 3 Application (Inherited Follow):** - $S'$ is at the end of $S \rightarrow iEtS\,S'$, so: $$\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S')$$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$, e\}$$ From $S' \rightarrow eS$: - After $S$ in this production: nothing follows within RHS, so: $$\text{FOLLOW}(S') \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$, e\} \text{ (already complete)}$$ **No further changes. FINAL FOLLOW SETS:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{\$, e\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(S') &= \{\$, e\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(E) &= \{t\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Constructing the LL(1) Parsing Table **Rule:** For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$: - For every terminal $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$: set $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ - If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$: for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$: set $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ ### Production 1: $S \rightarrow iEtS\,S'$ - $\text{FIRST}(iEtSS') = \{i\}$ - Set $M[S, i] = S \rightarrow iEtSS'$ ### Production 2: $S \rightarrow a$ - $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ - Set $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow a$ ### Production 3: $S' \rightarrow eS$ - $\text{FIRST}(eS) = \{e\}$ - Set $M[S', e] = S' \rightarrow eS$ ### Production 4: $S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ - $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$, so use $\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$, e\}$ - Set $M[S', \$] = S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ - Set $M[S', e] = S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ > [!WARNING] > **Multiple Entries Detected!** > $M[S', e]$ gets **two** entries: $S' \rightarrow eS$ and $S' \rightarrow \varepsilon$. > **Therefore, the grammar is NOT LL(1)** — this is the classic manifestation of the dangling-else ambiguity. In KTU exams, this triggers a follow-up: "Modify the grammar to make it LL(1)" or "Resolve the ambiguity by associating `else` with the nearest `if`." ### Final LL(1) Parsing Table $M$ (with conflict) | | **a** | **b** | **e** | **i** | **t** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **S** | S→a | | | S→iEtSS' | | | | **S'** | | | S'→eS / S'→ε | | | S'→ε | | **E** | | E→b | | | | | Empty cells = syntax error. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Parsing Trace (String: `ibta`) Assume the **conflict is resolved** by preferring $S' \rightarrow eS$ over $\varepsilon$ (i.e., we greedily match `else`). Stack: `[$ S]`, Input: `i b t a $`. | Step | Stack (top→bottom) | Input (head→tail) | Production Applied | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | \$ S | i b t a \$ | S → iEtSS' | | 2 | \$ S' S t E i | i b t a \$ | match `i` | | 3 | \$ S' S t E | b t a \$ | E → b | | 4 | \$ S' S t | t a \$ | match `t` | | 5 | \$ S' S | a \$ | S → a | | 6 | \$ S' | \$ | S' → ε | | 7 | \$ | \$ | **ACCEPT** ✓ | > [!NOTE] > This trace is **exactly** the kind of table-driven parse KTU expects you to write out for 7 marks. **Always** show the stack with the top on the **left**. --- ## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix | Transformation | Input Form | Output Form | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Left Recursion Elimination | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ | $A \rightarrow \beta A'$, $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ | Prevents infinite recursion in top-down | | Left Factoring | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta \;\vert\; \alpha\gamma$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$, $A' \rightarrow \beta \;\vert\; \gamma$ | Defers choice, removes backtracking | | FIRST Computation | Grammar rules | Set of initial terminals | Determines table entries | | FOLLOW Computation | Grammar rules | Set of following terminals | Handles ε-productions in table | | Table Construction | FIRST + FOLLOW | $M[A, a]$ | Automates parser decisions | | Parse Simulation | Stack + Table + Input | Accept / Error | Validates string membership | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Left-Factoring Transformation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Original Grammar: A has productions with common prefix alpha"] --> B["Identify Longest Common Prefix alpha across RHS"] B --> C{"Is alpha non-empty
and shared by 2+ rules?"} C -- "No" --> Z["Grammar is already LL 1 ready"] C -- "Yes" --> D["Group productions by prefix alpha"] D --> E["Create new non terminal A prime"] E --> F["A produces alpha A prime for grouped rules"] E --> G["A prime produces the suffixes beta 1, beta 2, ..., beta n"] E --> H["Unaffected productions kept under A"] F --> I["Re-examine A prime for further factoring"] I --> C G --> I H --> I ``` ## 4.2 Recursive Descent Parser — Call Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD Main["Main Driver"] --> ProcS["Procedure S"] ProcS --> Decision1{"Lookahead"} Decision1 -- "i" --> Branch1["Match i, call E, match t, call S, call S prime"] Decision1 -- "a" --> Branch2["Match a"] Branch1 --> ProcE["Procedure E"] ProcE --> MatchB["Match b"] Branch1 --> ProcSprime["Procedure S prime"] ProcSprime --> Decision2{"Lookahead"} Decision2 -- "e" --> Branch3["Match e, call S"] Decision2 -- "dollar or e" --> Branch4["Derive epsilon"] Branch2 --> Return1["Return success"] MatchB --> Return1 Branch3 --> ProcS Branch4 --> Return1 ProcS --> Return1 ``` ## 4.3 Table-Driven LL(1) Parser — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] IB["Input Buffer: a b t a dollar"] end subgraph ControlLayer["Control Layer"] Stack["STACK: dollar S S prime t E i"] end subgraph DecisionLayer["Decision Layer"] Table["PARSING TABLE M of A comma a"] end subgraph ActionLayer["Action Layer"] Action{"Top of stack X,
Input symbol a"} Act1["If X is terminal a: match and pop"] Act2["If X is non terminal: consult M of X comma a, push RHS"] Act3["If X equals dollar: ACCEPT"] Act4["If M is empty: ERROR"] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"] Out["Productions applied: leftmost derivation"] end InputLayer --> Action Stack --> Action Table --> Act2 Action --> Act1 Action --> Act2 Action --> Act3 Action --> Act4 Act1 --> OutputLayer Act2 --> OutputLayer Act3 --> OutputLayer Act4 --> OutputLayer ``` ## 4.4 LL(1) Grammar Construction Pipeline (End-to-End) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Input: Context Free Grammar G"] --> Step1["Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion"] Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Apply Left Factoring"] Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Compute FIRST sets for all symbols"] Step3 --> Step4["Step 4: Compute FOLLOW sets for all non terminals"] Step4 --> Step5["Step 5: Construct LL 1 Parsing Table M"] Step5 --> Check{"Every cell M of A comma a
has at most one entry?"} Check -- "Yes" --> Success["Grammar is LL 1: Build Predictive Parser"] Check -- "No" --> Refactor["Return to Step 1 or Step 2 and refactor"] Refactor --> Step1 Success --> Trace["Use stack simulation to parse input strings"] ``` ## 4.5 FIRST/FOLLOW Computation — Recursive Dependency ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FirstComputation["FIRST Computation"] F1["FIRST of terminal a equals a"] --> F2["FIRST of epsilon equals epsilon"] F2 --> F3["FIRST of X1 X2 ... Xn: add FIRST of Xi while epsilon in FIRST"] end subgraph FollowComputation["FOLLOW Computation"] Fo1["dollar in FOLLOW of S"] --> Fo2["If A derives alpha B beta, add FIRST of beta minus epsilon to FOLLOW of B"] Fo2 --> Fo3["If beta is epsilon or A derives alpha B, add FOLLOW of A to FOLLOW of B"] end FirstComputation --> FollowComputation FollowComputation --> FirstComputation ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice the **mutual recursion** between FIRST and FOLLOW — this is why KTU examiners expect you to iterate the computation until **no new symbols** are added in any pass. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 **Q: Define left-factoring. Why is it required in the context of top-down parsing?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Left-factoring is a grammar transformation that defers the parser's decision of which production to apply by extracting the longest common prefix shared among two or more right-hand sides of the same non-terminal into a new sub-production. It is required because, in top-down parsing, the parser must choose a production based only on the lookahead. When two productions share a prefix, the parser cannot disambiguate without backtracking. Left-factoring removes this ambiguity by pushing the choice point to a newly introduced non-terminal, making the grammar suitable for **non-backtracking predictive parsing**. > [!NOTE] > Marks Breakdown: Definition (2 marks), Reason with example reference (1 mark). --- ### Question A.2 **Q: What is an LL(1) parser? Explain the meaning of LL(1).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** An LL(1) parser is a top-down, predictive parser that: - **First L** — scans input from **L**eft to right. - **Second L** — produces a **L**eftmost derivation. - **1** — uses **1** token of lookahead to decide which production to apply. It is implemented either as a set of mutually recursive procedures (recursive descent) or as a table-driven stack machine. It works only for **LL(1) grammars** — those whose parsing table $M[A, a]$ has at most one production in every cell. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question B-A: Comprehensive Problem on Left-Factoring + LL(1) Table `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — **14 Marks** **Q: Consider the following grammar for arithmetic expressions:** $$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; E - T \;\vert\; T$$ $$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; T / F \;\vert\; F$$ $$F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **(i)** Eliminate left recursion from the grammar. **(ii)** Perform left-factoring on the resulting grammar if required. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **(iii)** Compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals. **(iv)** Construct the LL(1) parsing table. Is the grammar LL(1)? --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-A #### Part (a)(i) Left-Recursion Elimination **For E:** Productions are $E \rightarrow E + T$, $E \rightarrow E - T$, $E \rightarrow T$. Apply the standard formula $A \rightarrow A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$, $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$: $$E \rightarrow T\,E'$$ $$E' \rightarrow + T\,E' \;\vert\; - T\,E' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ **For T:** Productions are $T \rightarrow T * F$, $T \rightarrow T / F$, $T \rightarrow F$: $$T \rightarrow F\,T'$$ $$T' \rightarrow * F\,T' \;\vert\; / F\,T' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ **F is already left-recursion-free.** #### Part (a)(ii) Left-Factoring - $E'$ alternatives: $+TE'$, $-TE'$, $\varepsilon$ → first symbols are distinct. **No factoring needed.** - $T'$ alternatives: $*FT'$, $/FT'$, $\varepsilon$ → first symbols are distinct. **No factoring needed.** The final grammar is: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow T\,E' \\ E' &\rightarrow +TE' \;\vert\; -TE' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\ T &\rightarrow F\,T' \\ T' &\rightarrow *FT' \;\vert\; /FT' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (b)(iii) FIRST and FOLLOW Sets **FIRST computation:** - $\text{FIRST}(id) = \{id\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(( = \{(\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(F) = \text{FIRST}((E)) \cup \text{FIRST}(id) = \{(, id\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F\,T') = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{(, id\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T\,E') = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{(, id\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(+TE') = \{+\}$, $\text{FIRST}(-TE') = \{-\}$, $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+, -, \varepsilon\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(*FT') = \{*\}$, $\text{FIRST}(/FT') = \{/\}$, $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*, /, \varepsilon\}$ **FOLLOW computation:** - $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}$ (start symbol + the `)` in $F \rightarrow (E)$) - $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$: $E'$ is at the end of $E \rightarrow TE'$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$, )\}$$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$: $T$ is followed by $E'$, so $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+, -\}$ plus, since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, also $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$: $$\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, -, \$, )\}$$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$: $T'$ is at the end of $T \rightarrow FT'$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T')$ $$\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, -, \$, )\}$$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$: $F$ is followed by $T'$, so $\text{FIRST}(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*, /\}$ plus $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$ (since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$): $$\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*, /, +, -, \$, )\}$$ **Summary:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(E) &= \{(, id\}, & \text{FOLLOW}(E) &= \{\$, )\} \\ \text{FIRST}(E') &= \{+, -, \varepsilon\}, & \text{FOLLOW}(E') &= \{\$, )\} \\ \text{FIRST}(T) &= \{(, id\}, & \text{FOLLOW}(T) &= \{+, -, \$, )\} \\ \text{FIRST}(T') &= \{*, /, \varepsilon\}, & \text{FOLLOW}(T') &= \{+, -, \$, )\} \\ \text{FIRST}(F) &= \{(, id\}, & \text{FOLLOW}(F) &= \{*, /, +, -, \$, )\} \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (b)(iv) LL(1) Parsing Table Construction Apply the rule: $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ for each $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, and for $\varepsilon$-productions, use FOLLOW. | Production | Terminals from FIRST | Table Cells Filled | |---|---|---| | $E \rightarrow TE'$ | $(, id$ | $M[E, (] = E \rightarrow TE'$; $M[E, id] = E \rightarrow TE'$ | | $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ | $+$ | $M[E', +] = E' \rightarrow +TE'$ | | $E' \rightarrow -TE'$ | $-$ | $M[E', -] = E' \rightarrow -TE'$ | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | FOLLOW(E') = $\{\$, )\}$ | $M[E', \$] = E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$; $M[E', )] = E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | $T \rightarrow FT'$ | $(, id$ | $M[T, (] = T \rightarrow FT'$; $M[T, id] = T \rightarrow FT'$ | | $T' \rightarrow *FT'$ | $*$ | $M[T', *] = T' \rightarrow *FT'$ | | $T' \rightarrow /FT'$ | $/$ | $M[T', /] = T' \rightarrow /FT'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | FOLLOW(T') = $\{+, -, \$, )\}$ | $M[T', +] = M[T', -] = M[T', \$] = M[T', )] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | $($ | $M[F, (] = F \rightarrow (E)$ | | $F \rightarrow id$ | $id$ | $M[F, id] = F \rightarrow id$ | **Final Parsing Table:** | | **+** | **−** | **\*** | **/** | **(** | **)** | **id** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **E** | | | | | E→TE' | | E→TE' | | | **E'** | E'→+TE' | E'→−TE' | | | | E'→ε | | E'→ε | | **T** | | | | | T→FT' | | T→FT' | | | **T'** | T'→ε | T'→ε | T'→\*FT' | T'→/FT' | | T'→ε | | T'→ε | | **F** | | | | | F→(E) | | F→id | | **Conclusion:** Every cell has at most one production → **The grammar IS LL(1).** --- ### Question B-B (Alternative Choice): Recursive Descent Parser Design `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks** **Q: Consider the following LL(1) grammar:** $$S \rightarrow ( L ) \;\vert\; a$$ $$L \rightarrow L , S \;\vert\; S$$ **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **(i)** Eliminate left recursion. **(ii)** Compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets. Verify the grammar is LL(1). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **(iii)** Construct the LL(1) parsing table. **(iv)** Write the **recursive descent parser procedures** (pseudocode) for $S$ and $L$ using a global variable `lookahead` and a procedure `match(t)`. --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B-B #### Part (a)(i) Left-Recursion Elimination For $L$: $L \rightarrow L, S \;\vert\; S$. Apply the formula: $$L \rightarrow S\,L'$$ $$L' \rightarrow , S\,L' \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ **Final Grammar:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow (L) \;\vert\; a \\ L &\rightarrow S\,L' \\ L' &\rightarrow , S\,L' \;\vert\; \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (a)(ii) FIRST and FOLLOW **FIRST sets:** - $\text{FIRST}((L)) = \{(\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{(, a\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(SL') = \{(, a\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(,SL') = \{,\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(L') = \{,, \varepsilon\}$ - $\text{FIRST}(L) = \{(, a\}$ **FOLLOW sets:** - $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$, ,, )\}$ - From $S \rightarrow (L)$: $S$ doesn't appear alone there. - From $L \rightarrow SL'$: $S$ is followed by $L'$, so $\text{FIRST}(L') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{,\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(L')$, also $\text{FOLLOW}(L) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$. - $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$, ), ,\}$ (start $\$\$, plus $)$ from $S \rightarrow (L)$) - $\text{FOLLOW}(L) = \{)\}$ (followed by $)$ in $S \rightarrow (L)$) - $\text{FOLLOW}(L') = \text{FOLLOW}(L) = \{)\}$ **LL(1) Verification:** - For $S$: $\text{FIRST}((L)) \cap \text{FIRST}(a) = \{(\} \cap \{a\} = \emptyset$ ✓ - For $L'$: $\text{FIRST}(,SL') \cap \text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{,\} \cap \{\varepsilon\} = \emptyset$ ✓ - **Grammar is LL(1).** #### Part (b)(iii) LL(1) Parsing Table | | **(** | **)** | **,** | **a** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **S** | S→(L) | | | S→a | | | **L** | L→SL' | | | L→SL' | | | **L'** | | L'→ε | L'→,SL' | | | **Trace for input `(a,a)$`** (omitted for brevity — KTU typically does the trace on the original chosen question B-A, but a trace of one or two steps here suffices for partial credit). #### Part (b)(iv) Recursive Descent Parser Pseudocode ``` global lookahead // current input token procedure match(t): if lookahead == t: lookahead = nextToken() else: error("syntax error") procedure S(): if lookahead == '(': match('(') L() match(')') elif lookahead == 'a': match('a') else: error("syntax error in S") procedure L(): S() L_prime() procedure L_prime(): if lookahead == ',': match(',') S() L_prime() elif lookahead == ')': // derive epsilon — do nothing return else: error("syntax error in L'") ``` > [!TIP] > Notice how the structure of the procedures **mirrors** the structure of the productions. Each `if-elif` branch in the procedure corresponds to one row of the LL(1) table. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Incomplete FOLLOW computation (–3 marks typical):** Students often forget that if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(X)$ in a production $A \rightarrow \alpha B X$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ must be added to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$. KTU examiners specifically check this. > > 2. **Single-pass FIRST/FOLLOW (–2 marks):** FIRST and FOLLOW are mutually recursive. You **must iterate** until a pass produces no new additions. Writing the sets after only one pass is a guaranteed deduction. > > 3. **Forgetting to left-factor recursively (–2 marks):** When you introduce a new non-terminal $A'$, you must check whether $A'$ itself has a common prefix. The KTU "Dec 2023" paper had a 14-mark question where students lost marks for stopping after one factoring step. > > 4. **Stack drawn wrong in trace (–1 to –2 marks):** Always draw the stack with the **TOP on the LEFT**. Reversing this is a convention violation and examiners deduct. > > 5. **Failing to identify LL(1) conflicts (–2 marks):** If a cell has multiple entries, the answer is "the grammar is **not** LL(1) — refactor it." Many students skip this explicit conclusion. > > 6. **Confusing recursive descent with recursive descent-with-backtracking (–1 mark):** The "true" recursive descent **never backtracks**. Make this distinction crystal clear in definitions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Left-Factoring & LL(1) Parsing:** > > - **Left-Factoring** is the act of "factoring out" the longest common prefix of competing RHS into a new non-terminal. > - It is **not** the same as left-recursion elimination (that removes $A \rightarrow A\alpha$); they are **two distinct** transformations, often applied in sequence. > - **FIRST(α)** = terminals that can begin strings derived from α. Includes ε if α can derive the empty string. > - **FOLLOW(A)** = terminals that can appear immediately after $A$ in some sentential form. Always contains \$ for the start symbol. > - The **LL(1) table entry rule**: for $A \rightarrow \alpha$, fill $M[A, a]$ for $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$; if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, fill for $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$. > - A grammar is **LL(1)** iff every table cell has at most one production. > - **Recursive Descent Parser** = one recursive procedure per non-terminal; chooses production by inspecting the lookahead. > - **Table-Driven LL(1) Parser** = stack + parsing table + input buffer; the universal machine. > - The **stack simulation** trace is the most-tested KTU problem — memorize the format (Stack, Input, Action) with **top of stack on the left**. > - **Conflicts in the table indicate ambiguity** (e.g., the dangling-else problem) — refactor the grammar to remove them. > - FIRST and FOLLOW must be computed **iteratively** — never stop at one pass. > - In FOLLOW Rule 3, the propagation $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ applies when $B$ is at the end of a production $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or when $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ in $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$.

topics.title: Building a Calculator with YACC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Building a Calculator with YACC # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **YACC (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler)** is a classic **parser generator** tool originally written by Stephen C. Johnson at Bell Labs (1975). It takes a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** specification written in **Backus-Naur Form (BNF)** style and produces a **C-language parser** that implements a **LALR(1) (Look-Ahead LR with 1 token)** bottom-up parsing table automatically. For KTU PCCST601 Module 3, the canonical artifact is a **desk calculator** — a program that reads arithmetic expressions from standard input, evaluates them, and prints the numeric result. This single example unifies **lexical analysis (via LEX)**, **syntax analysis (via YACC)**, and **semantic evaluation (via synthesized attributes)** into one working front-end. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists under Module 3 deliverables: - **Specification of a simple syntax-directed translator using YACC** - **Implementation of a desk calculator with LEX and YACC** - **Construction of LALR parsing tables** - **Handling of shift/reduce and reduce/reduce conflicts** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Imagine you walk into a restaurant and read a menu written in a foreign language. You don't understand it, so you hire two specialists: 1. **LEX** is your **"word expert"** — he scans the menu line by line, identifies each ingredient/token (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `(`, `)`), reads numbers like a cashier reading prices, and hands each token to the kitchen as a labeled ingredient. 2. **YACC** is your **"grammar chef"** — he has memorized the recipe book (grammar). When LEX hands him ingredients in sequence, YACC checks whether the sequence follows the recipe rules. If yes, he assembles the dish (parses) and tells you the final price (evaluates the expression). 3. The **C compiler** is the **restaurant builder** — it converts the chef's instructions into an actual executable kitchen that runs forever, taking orders and serving results. > [!NOTE] > In KTU parlance: **LEX produces tokens → YACC parses tokens using grammar → Action code computes the result**. The parser is therefore a **syntax-directed translator**, not just a recognizer. ## 1.3 The Lex–Yacc Handshake | Tool | Role | Input | Output | |---|---|---|---| | **LEX** | Lexical Analyzer Generator | Regular expressions (`.l` file) | `lex.yy.c` + token codes | | **YACC** | Parser Generator | BNF grammar + actions (`.y` file) | `y.tab.c` (the parser) | | **C Compiler** (`gcc`) | Code generator | `lex.yy.c`, `y.tab.c`, helpers | Executable `a.out` | The handshake works through three shared symbols: - **Token codes** (e.g., `NUMBER = 258`) returned by `yylex()`. - **`yylval`** — the *value stack* carrier, a global union that ships the *semantic value* of each token from LEX into YACC. - **`yyparse()`** — the master function exported by YACC, which repeatedly calls `yylex()` until it accepts or errors out. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a YACC Source File (`.y`) A YACC specification has **three canonical sections** separated by the magic delimiter `%%`: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SECTION 1 — Declarations │ │ %{ C-declarations, includes, typedef %} │ │ %token, %left, %right, %nonassoc │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ SECTION 2 — Grammar Rules + Actions │ │ Productions of the form: │ │ nonterm : symbols { semantic action } │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ SECTION 3 — Auxiliary Routines │ │ yyerror(), main(), yylex() stub, etc. │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.1.1 Section 1 — Declarations - **`%token NUMBER`** declares terminal symbols. YACC auto-assigns integer codes starting at **258** (codes 0–255 reserved for single ASCII characters). - **`%left`, `%right`, `%nonassoc`** declare operator precedence. Tokens declared *later* on the same line bind **tighter**. Tokens declared *earlier* have **lower** precedence. - `%left '+' '-'` → lowest precedence → arithmetic `+`/`-`. - `%left '*' '/'` → higher precedence → arithmetic `*`/`/`. - `%right UMINUS` (a fictitious token) → used only for unary minus precedence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the precedence declaration?** The ambiguous grammar `E → E + E | E * E | …` has **shift/reduce conflicts**. YACC resolves them *automatically* based on the precedence table — no need to write an unambiguous grammar. ### 2.1.2 Section 2 — Grammar + Actions Each rule has the form: $$\text{head} \;\rightarrow\; \text{body}_1 \;\vert\; \text{body}_2 \;\vert\; \dots$$ with an optional **semantic action** `{}` attached. Inside an action: - **`$$`** = value of the head symbol (left-hand side). - **`$1, $2, …, $n`** = values of the body symbols (right-hand side). - **`$N`** = value with explicit type tag (for typed grammars). For our calculator, every symbol has the semantic type **`int`**, so `$1 + $3` simply sums two ints. ### 2.1.3 Section 3 — Auxiliary Routines Mandatory routines: - **`int yylex(void)`** — supplied by LEX (`lex.yy.c`) or hand-coded. - **`void yyerror(const char *s)`** — invoked by YACC on syntax errors. - **`int main(void)`** — calls `yyparse()` and returns its exit status. ## 2.2 The Lexical Sister File (`.l`) The companion LEX file reads characters and produces tokens: | Pattern | Action | Token Returned | |---|---|---| | `[0-9]+` | `yylval = atoi(yytext);` | `NUMBER` | | `[ \t]` | *(no action — skip)* | none | | `\n` | `return '\n';` | newline | | `.` | `return yytext[0];` | single-char operator | The single-character operators `+ - * / ( )` are returned as their own ASCII codes, which is why YACC sees `'+'`, `'-'`, etc. as terminals with codes `43, 45, 42, 47, 40, 41`. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below condenses **every fact, token, and rule** you need to reproduce the desk calculator from scratch in a 14-mark KTU question. | # | Concept | Notation / Value | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | First user token code | **258** | YACC reserves 0–255 for ASCII | | 2 | Value carrier | **`yylval`** | Global union of token values | | 3 | Action placeholder for LHS | **`$$`** | Head value | | 4 | Action placeholder for RHS | **`$i`** | i-th symbol value | | 5 | Precedence directive | `%left`, `%right`, `%nonassoc` | Later = tighter | | 6 | Unary minus precedence hack | `%right UMINUS` | `expr '-' expr %prec UMINUS` | | 7 | Default conflict resolution | **Shift** for left-assoc, **Reduce** for right-assoc | Built from precedence | | 8 | Parser call | `yyparse()` | Returns 0 on success | | 9 | Error routine signature | `void yyerror(const char *s)` | Must be defined | | 10 | Lexer output file | `lex.yy.c` | Compiled with `gcc` | | 11 | Yacc output file | `y.tab.c` + `y.tab.h` | `-d` flag exports header | | 12 | Yacc command | `yacc -d calc.y` | `-d` → `y.tab.h` for LEX | | 13 | Lex command | `lex calc.l` | Generates `lex.yy.c` | | 14 | Final link | `gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll` | `-ll` for libfl | ## 2.4 Build Pipeline — Engineering Reality In a real-world compiler-toolchain project (and in the KTU lab exam), the **commands are**: ```bash yacc -d calc.y # Step 1: parse grammar → y.tab.c, y.tab.h lex calc.l # Step 2: tokenize → lex.yy.c gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll # Step 3: compile + link ./calc # Step 4: run, type "3+4*2\n", see result ``` > [!TIP] > KTU lab examiners frequently test these **exact four commands** for 2 marks. Memorize the order and the `-d` and `-ll` flags. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Complete, Exam-Ready Calculator Specification Below is the **canonical, verified** YACC specification. Every line is annotated for KTU mark allocation. ### 3.1.1 The Lex File — `calc.l` ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" %} %% [0-9]+ { yylval = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; } [ \t] ; /* skip whitespace */ \n return '\n'; /* end of expression */ . return yytext[0]; /* operators as themselves */ %% ``` **Line-by-line analysis:** - `#include "y.tab.h"` → pulls in the `#define NUMBER 258` from YACC. - `yylval = atoi(yytext)` → writes the integer value into the value stack slot. - `return NUMBER` → gives YACC the **token code 258**. - `return yytext[0]` → returns the ASCII code of the operator (e.g., `'+' = 43`). ### 3.1.2 The Yacc File — `calc.y` ```c %{ #include int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} %token NUMBER %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %right UMINUS %% list: | list '\n' | list expr '\n' { printf("= %d\n", $2); } ; expr: expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; } | expr '-' expr { $$ = $1 - $3; } | expr '*' expr { $$ = $1 * $3; } | expr '/' expr { $$ = $1 / $3; } | '(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | '-' expr %prec UMINUS { $$ = -$2; } | NUMBER { $$ = $1; } ; %% void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { return yyparse(); } ``` ## 3.2 Parsing Walk-Through — `3 + 4 * 2` This is the **highest-yield KTU derivation** — expect 4 to 6 marks for "trace the parse for an expression." The grammar is **inherently ambiguous** (every operator can recurse on both sides), so YACC uses **precedence + left-associativity** to resolve: | Precedence Table (declared order) | |---| | Lowest: `+`, `-` (left-assoc) | | Middle: `*`, `/` (left-assoc) | | Highest: `UMINUS` (right-assoc) | **Step 1 — Tokenize.** LEX produces the stream: $$\text{NUMBER(3) } \;`+'\; \text{ NUMBER(4) } \;`\*'\; \text{ NUMBER(2) } \;`\backslash n'$$ **Step 2 — Build the value stack as we shift.** | Step | Stack | Input | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `$` | `3 + 4 * 2 \n $` | shift `3` | | 2 | `$ 3` | `+ 4 * 2 \n $` | reduce `NUMBER → expr` → push `expr=3` | | 3 | `$ expr(3)` | `+ 4 * 2 \n $` | shift `+` | | 4 | `$ expr(3) +` | `4 * 2 \n $` | shift `4`, reduce → `expr(4)` | | 5 | `$ expr(3) + expr(4)` | `* 2 \n $` | shift `*` (since `*` > `+` precedence) | | 6 | `$ expr(3) + expr(4) *` | `2 \n $` | shift `2`, reduce → `expr(2)` | | 7 | `$ expr(3) + expr(4) * expr(2)` | `\n $` | reduce by `E → E * E` → `expr(8)` | | 8 | `$ expr(3) + expr(8)` | `\n $` | reduce by `E → E + E` → `expr(11)` | | 9 | `$ expr(11) \n` | `$` | reduce `list → list expr \n`, print `= 11` | **Step 3 — Verify the action trace using the value-stack equations:** For Step 7, the rule `expr '*' expr` fires with: $$\$1 = 4, \quad \$2 = *, \quad \$3 = 2$$ $$\Rightarrow \$\$ = \$1 \times \$3 = 4 \times 2 = 8$$ For Step 8, the rule `expr '+' expr` fires with: $$\$1 = 3, \quad \$2 = +, \quad \$3 = 8$$ $$\Rightarrow \$\$ = \$1 + \$3 = 3 + 8 = 11$$ ## 3.3 Unary Minus — Why the `UMINUS` Hack is Needed The rule `expr '-' expr %prec UMINUS` rebinds the precedence of **the second `'-'`** to that of `UMINUS`, which is declared as right-associative and higher than `*`/`/`. Without this hack, YACC reports: ``` warning: rule useless in parser due to conflicts ``` and the unary minus fails. With it, `-3 * 4` correctly parses as `(-3) * 4 = -12`, not as `-(3 * 4) = -12` (numerically the same here, but `-3 - 4 = 7` differs). ## 3.4 Conflict Diagnostics — KTU's Favourite Pitfall > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners repeatedly ask: *"What conflicts occur if you remove the precedence declarations?"* If you delete all `%left`/`%right` lines, the **ambiguous grammar** `E → E + E | E * E | (E) | -E | NUMBER` produces: | Conflict Type | Count | Cause | |---|---|---| | **Shift/Reduce** | **8** | Every rule-pair with operators of differing precedence (e.g., `+` vs `*`) | | **Reduce/Reduce** | **0** | All `expr` alternatives differ in first symbol | YACC reports these as: ``` 8 shift/reduce conflicts ``` and **silently applies default resolution**: shift wins for left-associative operators, which is exactly what we want. So the calculator *runs*, but with **warning messages** — which examiners deduct marks for. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Symbol-to-Action Map > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important table** for solving "explain the semantic actions" type questions. | Rule | $1 | $2 | $3 | Action | Result `$$` | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | `expr '+' expr` | left | `+` | right | `$$ = $1 + $3` | sum | | `expr '-' expr` | left | `-` | right | `$$ = $1 - $3` | difference | | `expr '*' expr` | left | `*` | right | `$$ = $1 * $3` | product | | `expr '/' expr` | left | `/` | right | `$$ = $1 / $3` | quotient (integer) | | `'(' expr ')'` | `(` | inner | `)` | `$$ = $2` | inner value | | `'-' expr %prec UMINUS` | `-` | inner | — | `$$ = -$2` | negation | | `NUMBER` | num | — | — | `$$ = $1` | the number itself | ## 3.6 Sample Run Output ``` 3+4*2 = 11 (3+4)*2 = 14 -7+10 = 3 10/3 = 3 ``` --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Build & Run Pipeline The block diagram below traces the **entire flow** from source files to a running executable — a recurring 7-mark question in KTU ESE. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["calc.l
LEX source"]:::inputFile B["calc.y
YACC source"]:::inputFile C["yacc -d calc.y"]:::tool D["y.tab.c
y.tab.h"]:::output E["lex calc.l"]:::tool F["lex.yy.c
tokenizer"]:::output G["gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -ll -o calc"]:::tool H["calc
executable"]:::output I["stdin
arithmetic input"]:::input J["stdout
= result"]:::output A --> E B --> C C --> D E --> F D --> G F --> G G --> H I --> H H --> J classDef inputFile fill:#ffe7b3,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000 classDef tool fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef input fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The Lex–Yacc Runtime Handshake This diagram captures **how tokens and values flow** at runtime — the heart of every "explain the working" question. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Input participant M as main() participant P as yyparse() participant L as yylex() from LEX participant V as yylval (Union) participant S as Value Stack participant A as Semantic Action participant O as stdout U->>M: "3+4*2\n" M->>P: yyparse() loop until end-of-input P->>L: yylex() L->>V: yylval = atoi(yytext) V-->>P: returns NUMBER (258) P->>S: shift NUMBER, value 3 P->>L: yylex() L-->>P: returns '+' (43) P->>S: shift '+' P->>L: yylex() L->>V: yylval = 4 V-->>P: returns NUMBER (258) P->>S: shift NUMBER, value 4 P->>L: yylex() L-->>P: returns '*' (42) P->>S: shift '*' (precedence: shift wins) P->>L: yylex() L->>V: yylval = 2 V-->>P: returns NUMBER P->>S: shift NUMBER, value 2 P->>A: reduce E*E -> $1=4, $3=2 A->>S: push 8 P->>A: reduce E+E -> $1=3, $3=8 A->>S: push 11 P->>A: reduce list -> list expr '\n' A->>O: printf("= %d\n", 11) end P-->>M: return 0 M-->>U: exit ``` ## 4.3 LALR Parsing Table Snapshot for the Calculator Although YACC generates this table automatically, the KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to **understand** it. The simplified table below shows the canonical entries for terminals `+`, `*`, `(`, `)`, `$`, `id`. | State | `+` | `*` | `(` | `)` | `$` | `id` | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | — | — | s2 | — | — | s3 | start | | 1 | s4 | s5 | — | — | acc | — | after first `expr` | | 2 | — | — | s2 | — | — | s3 | inside `(` | | 3 | r(E→id) | r(E→id) | r(E→id) | r(E→id) | r(E→id) | r(E→id) | reduce by `E→id` | | 4 | — | — | s2 | — | — | s3 | after `+` | | 5 | — | — | s2 | — | — | s3 | after `*` | | 6 | s4 | s5 | — | s7 | — | — | after `expr + expr` | | 7 | r(E→(E)) | r(E→(E)) | r(E→(E)) | r(E→(E)) | r(E→(E)) | r(E→(E)) | reduce by `E→(E)` | | 8 | r(E→E+E) | s5 | — | — | — | — | reduce, then shift `*` | > `sN` = shift to state `N`, `r(rule)` = reduce by rule, `acc` = accept. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** *What is the role of YACC in compiler construction? List any two advantages of using YACC.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** YACC (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler) is a **parser generator** that takes a **Context-Free Grammar** specified in Backus-Naur Form (BNF) and produces a **C-language LALR(1) parser** automatically. Two advantages: **(i)** it automates the construction of parsing tables, eliminating error-prone manual work; **(ii)** it supports **semantic actions** (synthesized attributes via `$$` and `$i`) that allow the grammar to act as a **syntax-directed translator** — so the same specification simultaneously parses *and* evaluates. --- > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **Q2.** *What is the difference between `%left`, `%right`, and `%nonassoc` declarations in YACC? Give one example each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **`%left`** declares a token as **left-associative** — `a + b + c` parses as `(a + b) + c`. Example: `%left '+' '-'`. - **`%right`** declares a token as **right-associative** — `a = b = c` parses as `a = (b = c)`. Example: `%right '='`. - **`%nonassoc`** declares a token as **non-associative** — `a < b < c` is a syntax error. Example: `%nonassoc '<' '>'`. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice ### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q (a).** *Explain the structure of a YACC source file with neat sections. Write the YACC specification for a desk calculator that evaluates arithmetic expressions involving `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, parentheses, and unary minus. Mention the precedence and associativity used.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **YACC File Structure (2 marks):** A `.y` file has three sections separated by `%%`: 1. **Declarations Section** — contains C declarations (`%{ … %}`), token declarations (`%token`), and precedence declarations (`%left`, `%right`, `%%nonassoc`). 2. **Grammar Rules Section** — contains productions in BNF with embedded semantic actions in `{ … }`. 3. **Auxiliary Routines Section** — contains user-written C functions like `main()`, `yyerror()`, and prototypes. **Precedence and Associativity (2 marks):** - `+`, `-` — left-associative, lowest precedence. - `*`, `/` — left-associative, higher precedence than `+`/`-`. - `UMINUS` — right-associative, highest precedence (used for unary minus). **YACC Specification (3 marks):** ```c %{ #include int yylex(void); void yyerror(const char *s); %} %token NUMBER %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' %right UMINUS %% expr: expr '+' expr { $$ = $1 + $3; } | expr '-' expr { $$ = $1 - $3; } | expr '*' expr { $$ = $1 * $3; } | expr '/' expr { $$ = $1 / $3; } | '(' expr ')' { $$ = $2; } | '-' expr %prec UMINUS { $$ = -$2; } | NUMBER { $$ = $1; } ; %% void yyerror(const char *s) { fprintf(stderr, "Error: %s\n", s); } int main(void) { return yyparse(); } ``` --- **Q (b).** *Write the corresponding LEX specification for the above calculator. Explain how `yylval` is used to communicate the numeric value from LEX to YACC. Trace the parsing and evaluation of the input `7 - 3 * 2`. Show the value stack at each step.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **LEX Specification (3 marks):** ```c %{ #include "y.tab.h" %} %% [0-9]+ { yylval = atoi(yytext); return NUMBER; } [ \t] ; \n return '\n'; . return yytext[0]; %% ``` **Role of `yylval` (2 marks):** `yylval` is a global union defined by YACC in `y.tab.h`. When LEX recognises a multi-character token like `42`, it stores the integer value (`42`) into `yylval` *before* returning the token code (`NUMBER`). YACC's parser then reads this value via `$1`, `$2`, … inside the matching semantic action. Without `yylval`, only token codes would flow — no semantic values. **Parsing Trace for `7 - 3 * 2` (2 marks):** | Step | Token | Action | Value Stack (top → bottom) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | NUMBER(7) | shift | `7` | | 2 | `NUMBER → expr` | reduce, `$$ = 7` | `expr=7` | | 3 | `-` | shift | `- expr=7` | | 4 | NUMBER(3) | shift | `3 - expr=7` | | 5 | `NUMBER → expr` | reduce, `$$ = 3` | `expr=3 - expr=7` | | 6 | `*` | shift (`*` > `-` prec) | `* expr=3 - expr=7` | | 7 | NUMBER(2) | shift | `2 * expr=3 - expr=7` | | 8 | `NUMBER → expr` | reduce, `$$ = 2` | `expr=2 * expr=3 - expr=7` | | 9 | `expr * expr → expr` | reduce, `$$ = 3*2 = 6` | `expr=6 - expr=7` | | 10 | `expr - expr → expr` | reduce, `$$ = 7-6 = 1` | `expr=1` | | 11 | `\n` | accept, print `= 1` | — | **Output:** `= 1` ✓ --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **Q (a).** *Explain the concept of shift/reduce conflicts in YACC. How does the precedence declaration resolve the shift/reduce conflict in the arithmetic expression grammar? Show how the rule `expr '-' expr %prec UMINUS` allows handling of unary minus without conflict.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Shift/Reduce Conflict (2 marks):** A shift/reduce conflict arises when the parser, holding a handle on the stack, cannot decide whether to **shift** the next token onto the stack or **reduce** the top of stack by some rule. In the grammar `E → E + E | E * E`, after reading `a + b`, the parser sees `*` and must decide whether to reduce `a + b` first (giving lower precedence to `+`) or shift `*` (giving higher precedence). Without external info, it is ambiguous. **Resolution via Precedence (3 marks):** The declarations ```yacc %left '+' '-' %left '*' '/' ``` tell YACC two things: **(i)** `*` has higher precedence than `+`; **(ii)** both are left-associative. When a conflict occurs, YACC consults the table: - If the operator on the stack has **higher precedence** than the lookahead → **shift**. - If the lookahead has **higher precedence** → **reduce**. - If equal precedence → use associativity (left-assoc → reduce, right-assoc → shift). For input `a + b * c`, at the moment we see `*` on top with `+` below, `*` wins and we shift — yielding `a + (b * c)`. **Unary Minus Trick (2 marks):** The rule ```yacc expr : '-' expr %prec UMINUS { $$ = -$2; } ``` rebinds the precedence of the second `'-'` (which is the rule's operator slot) to that of the fictitious token `UMINUS`. Since `UMINUS` is declared `%right` with highest precedence, the parser always **shifts** when it sees a `-` after an operator, treating it as unary negation rather than subtraction. Example: `-5 + 3` correctly parses as `(-5) + 3 = -2`. --- **Q (b).** *List the build commands (LEX → YACC → C-compile → run) required to construct the calculator. Write the LEX file completely. State the meaning of `-d` flag in YACC and `-ll` flag in GCC during the link step.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Build Commands (3 marks):** ```bash yacc -d calc.y # 1. parse grammar -> y.tab.c, y.tab.h lex calc.l # 2. tokenize -> lex.yy.c gcc y.tab.c lex.yy.c -o calc -ll # 3. compile + link ./calc # 4. execute ``` **Complete LEX File (2 marks):** *(see 3.1.1)* **Flag Meanings (2 marks):** - **`yacc -d calc.y`** → the `-d` flag instructs YACC to write the file `y.tab.h`, which contains `#define` statements for every token (e.g., `#define NUMBER 258`). This header is `#include`d by the LEX file so that `return NUMBER;` refers to the same numeric code that YACC expects. - **`gcc ... -ll`** → links against the **LEX library (`libl`/`libfl`)**, which provides the default `main()` function that calls `yylex()` in a loop until EOF. If you supply your own `main()` (as our `calc.y` does), the `-ll` flag is still commonly used but is **not strictly required** — you may omit it. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting `%prec UMINUS`** → unary minus parses as `(0 - x) - y` instead of `-(x) - y`. Costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Writing `=` instead of `$$`** → undefined symbol, compile error. Examiners deduct the entire action's marks (≈ 1 mark each). > 3. **Omitting `return '\n'` in LEX** → the parser never receives an end-of-expression signal; the `list → list expr '\n'` rule never fires, so no output is printed. Silent failure. > 4. **Forgetting `#include "y.tab.h"` in LEX** → `yylval` is undeclared, causing cryptic compile errors that students blame on the wrong file. > 5. **Confusing `%left` with `%right`** → wrong associativity means `a - b - c` evaluates as `a - (b - c)` instead of `(a - b) - c`. Lost 2 marks on the trace. > 6. **Not showing the value stack in a trace question** → worth up to 2 marks; an answer with only "parser reduces" is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density revision checklist** — memorise every bullet below before walking into Module 3. - **YACC** = parser generator that builds an **LALR(1)** parser from a **BNF grammar**. - A `.y` file has **3 sections**: declarations (`%{ … %}`, `%token`, `%left/right/nonassoc`), grammar rules with actions, auxiliary C routines (`main`, `yyerror`). - A `.l` (LEX) file is the **lexical companion** that produces tokens consumed by YACC via `yylex()`. - **Value transfer** LEX → YACC happens through the global union **`yylval`**, assigned in LEX and accessed via `$1`, `$2`, … in YACC. - **First user token code** is **258**; ASCII characters keep their own codes (0–255). - **Precedence order** is declared **low-to-high**; later declarations bind tighter. - **Associativity**: left-assoc → reduce, right-assoc → shift, non-assoc → error on duplicates. - **Ambiguous grammars** (like `E → E + E | E * E`) **are deliberately used** with precedence directives to keep YACC specifications short and readable. - **Unary minus** requires the `%prec UMINUS` trick with a fictitious `%right UMINUS` token. - **Conflicts**: ambiguous expression grammar yields **8 shift/reduce** conflicts, automatically resolved by YACC per the precedence table; warnings remain. - **Build pipeline**: `yacc -d calc.y` → `lex calc.l` → `gcc y.tab.c lex.yy.c -o calc -ll` → `./calc`. - **`-d`** exports `y.tab.h` (token `#define`s for LEX); **`-ll`** links the LEX runtime library. - **Calculator grammar** typically includes a **`list` non-terminal** that loops over many expressions terminated by `\n`, enabling an interactive session. - **Semantic actions** are written in C inside `{ … }`; `$$` is the LHS value, `$i` is the i-th RHS symbol's value. - The action `printf("= %d\n", $2)` is usually placed in the `list → list expr '\n'` rule, *not* in the `expr` rules, to print only after a complete line. - **LALR** stands for *Look-Ahead LR*; it merges LR(1) states with identical LR(0) cores — fewer states than canonical LR, more than SLR. - **Integer division**: the calculator does integer division (e.g., `7/2 = 3`); extending to floats requires changing `yylval` type and using `atof`/`%f`. - **Error recovery**: `yyerror()` prints a message and returns; the default YACC error recovery discards tokens until a synchronising token (like `'\n'` for the `list` rule) is found. ---

topics.title: Intermediate Representations: An IR Taxonomy, Graphical IRs - Syntax-Related Trees, Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Representations: An IR Taxonomy, Graphical IRs - Syntax-Related Trees, Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Intermediate Representation (IR)?

An **Intermediate Representation (IR)** is a structured, machine-independent abstraction of the source program that lives between the **front-end** (lexical and syntax analysis) and the **back-end** (code generation and optimization) of a compiler. It is the compiler's "internal working language" — a bridge that allows the same front-end to target multiple machines and the same back-end to accept multiple source languages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU/Aho-Sethi-Ullman standard):**  
> *An Intermediate Representation is a data structure that encodes the semantics of a program in a form that is closer to the target machine than the source language, but is still independent of any specific target architecture, enabling analysis, optimization, and code generation.*

### 1.1.1 Why do we need an IR?
