| Reason | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Retargeting** | One front-end (say, for C) can target x86, ARM, RISC-V using multiple back-ends. |
| **Reuse of optimizations** | Optimizations like constant folding, dead-code elimination, and loop-invariant code motion are written **once** at the IR level. |
| **Separation of concerns** | Front-end engineers need not know about register allocation; back-end engineers need not know about parsing. |
| **Verification** | IRs are easier to formally verify than machine code. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Blueprint Analogy:**  
> Think of compiling a house. The architect draws a **blueprint** (the IR) — not the English description, and not the final concrete-and-brick house. The blueprint is precise enough for the structural engineer to optimize the design (remove a redundant column) and the electrician to wire it up later. The IR is exactly this blueprint: it is **richer than the source** and **cleaner than the target machine code**.

## 1.3 Graphical IRs — Overview

A **Graphical IR** represents the program as a **graph**:
- **Nodes** = operations, expressions, or program entities
- **Edges** = relationships (data flow, control flow, lexical order)

The two principal families of Graphical IRs in the KTU syllabus are:
1. **Syntax-Related Trees** — capture the **syntactic structure** of the program (Parse Tree, AST, DAG).
2. **Graphs** — capture **execution semantics** (Control Flow Graph, Data Flow Graph, Call Graph, Dependence Graph).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PCCST601 explicitly demands knowledge of *IR Taxonomy* and *Graphical IRs (Syntax-Related Trees and Graphs)*. These are the foundation of the optimization phase covered in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IR Taxonomy (The Three-Dimensional Classification)

The classic textbook classification of IRs (popularized in the Dragon Book) classifies an IR along **three independent axes**:

### 2.1.1 Axis 1 — Structural Level (or "Height" of the IR)

| Level | Closest to | Example IRs | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Level IR (HIR)** | Source language | **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**, Parse Tree | Type checking, high-level optimizations |
| **Medium-Level IR (MIR)** | Independent | **3-Address Code (3AC)**, DAG | Most optimizations (constant folding, CSE) |
| **Low-Level IR (LIR)** | Target machine | Register Transfer Language, Stack machine code | Register allocation, peephole optimization |

### 2.1.2 Axis 2 — Specificity to Source or Target

- **Source-related IR** — preserves source language structure (AST, parse tree).
- **Target-related IR** — preserves target machine details (RTL, instruction list).
- **Independent IR** — deliberately abstract from both (canonical 3AC).

### 2.1.3 Axis 3 — Operational Behavior

- **Declarative IR** — describes **what** the program computes (e.g., AST, DAG, expression trees).
- **Imperative IR** — describes **how** the computation is sequenced (e.g., 3AC, SSA form, machine code).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** Any single IR can be plotted in the 3-D space of *(Level, Specificity, Behavior)*. Modern compilers like LLVM use **multiple IRs** — one per level — and translate between them.

## 2.2 Graphical IRs — Syntax-Related Trees

### 2.2.1 Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree)

- Built directly from the **derivation steps** of the grammar.
- Every grammar symbol (including non-terminals and tokens) becomes a node.
- Includes **punctuation, parentheses, keywords** as nodes.
- **Height** $\approx 2 \times \text{(number of operators)}$ for typical expressions.

### 2.2.2 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

- **Abstracts away** syntax rules — no interior non-terminals, no punctuation nodes.
- **Internal nodes** = operators/constructs; **leaves** = operands (identifiers/constants).
- **Why AST is smaller:** For a binary expression with $n$ operator nodes, AST has roughly $n + m + 1$ nodes where $m$ is the number of unique operand leaves. Parse trees are often **3× to 5× larger** for the same expression.

### 2.2.3 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

- A **compressed AST** where **Common Subexpressions (CSE)** are merged.
- If a subexpression occurs $k$ times in the source, DAG stores it **once** and shares the result.
- **Nodes** with multiple parents = points of CSE.

### 2.2.4 Control Flow Graph (CFG)

- **Nodes** = **Basic Blocks** (maximal sequences of straight-line 3AC with no internal branches).
- **Edges** = possible flow of control.
- Entry and Exit are explicit distinguished nodes.

### 2.2.5 Other Graphical IRs

| IR | What it captures |
|---|---|
| **Data Flow Graph (DFG)** | Data dependencies between operations |
| **Dependence Graph (DG)** | Data + control dependencies (used in parallelization) |
| **Call Graph** | Function call relationships (interprocedural) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property | Parse Tree | AST | DAG | CFG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Node count (approx.)** | $\approx 2n + m + 1$ | $\approx n + m + 1$ | $\leq n + m + 1$ | = number of basic blocks |
| **Edge count** | $\approx 2n$ | $\approx n$ | $\approx n$ | $\leq 2 \times \vert B \vert$ |
| **Captures syntax** | Yes (full) | No (abstract) | No | No |
| **Captures control flow** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Captures CSE** | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Source-related** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Typical use** | Parsing | Type check | Optimization | Data-flow analysis |

Where $n$ = number of operator nodes and $m$ = number of unique operand leaves. Vertical bars denote cardinality (size of a set).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modern JIT compilers (LLVM, HotSpot, V8) maintain **multiple IRs simultaneously** — an AST for semantic analysis, an SSA-form 3AC for optimization, and a machine-specific MIR for register allocation. The taxonomy you learn today is exactly the architecture used in industry.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Why HIR first?** The parser already produces an AST; reusing it saves work and preserves source-level structure useful for type checking and high-level rewrites.
2. **Why translate to MIR?** Source-level constructs (e.g., arrays, structs) must be broken into primitive operations so data-flow analysis becomes tractable.
3. **Why lower to LIR last?** Register allocation and instruction scheduling need to see the target's register classes and pipeline structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — AST Construction for `a + b * c`

> **Step 0 — Tokenize:**  
> Identifiers $a, b, c$; operators $+, *$.

> **Step 1 — Apply precedence** (multiplication binds tighter than addition).  
> Group as $a + (b \times c)$.

> **Step 2 — Build leaves:**  
> Three leaf nodes for identifiers $a$, $b$, $c$.

> **Step 3 — Build operator nodes bottom-up.**  
> The $\times$ node has children $b$ and $c$.  
> The $+$ node (root) has children $a$ and the $\times$ subtree.

**Resulting AST (7 nodes total):**

$$
N_{AST} = 1_{(root)} + 1_{(+)} + 1_{(*)} + 3_{(leaves)} = 6 \text{ nodes}
$$

(Counting only operator + operand nodes; if we count the root separately: $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$.)

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DAG for `(a + b) * (a + b) + (a + b) * c`

This expression has the **common subexpression** $(a + b)$ appearing three times.

> **Step 1 — List unique subexpressions:**  
> $a$, $b$, $c$, $(a + b)$, $(a + b) \cdot (a + b)$, $(a + b) \cdot c$, the final $+$ at the root.

> **Step 2 — Build a single + node and reuse it three times.**  
> The DAG will have **one** node labeled $+$ (with children $a$ and $b$), even though the source has three occurrences.

> **Step 3 — Build the two $\times$ nodes and the root.**  
> $\times_1$ has children = the shared $+$ node and itself (so $(\text{a+b}) \cdot (\text{a+b})$).  
> $\times_2$ has children = the shared $+$ node and $c$.  
> Root $+$ has children = $\times_1$ and $\times_2$.

**Node-count analysis:**

$$
N_{AST} = 2_{(\times)} + 1_{(+)} + 3_{(operands)} = 6 \text{ nodes}
$$

$$
N_{DAG} = 2_{(\times)} + 1_{(+)} + 3_{(operands)} = 6 \text{ nodes}
$$

> **The catch:** Although node *count* is similar, the DAG has only **one** $+$ node instead of three, which means **fewer edges and less memory**. The savings grow with the number of CSE occurrences.

**Compression Ratio:**

$$
\text{CSE Compression Factor} = \frac{\text{Number of } (+) \text{ nodes in AST}}{\text{Number of } (+) \text{ nodes in DAG}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Control Flow Graph (CFG) for an `if-else` block

**Source code segment:**

```
1.  t1 = x > 0
2.  if t1 goto L1
3.  y = 2
4.  goto L2
L1: y = 1
L2: end
```

> **Step 1 — Identify basic blocks** (a basic block = a maximal straight-line sequence with one entry and one exit).  
> **B1:** `t1 = x > 0`  
> **B2:** `if t1 goto L1`  
> **B3:** `y = 2; goto L2`  
> **B4:** `y = 1`  
> **B5:** `end`

> **Step 2 — Draw edges** based on control flow.  
> B1 → B2 (sequential)  
> B2 → B3 (false branch)  
> B2 → B4 (true branch)  
> B3 → B5  
> B4 → B5

> **Step 3 — Verify acyclicity for data flow within blocks** (CFG may have cycles; DAG must not).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Syntax Trees

| Aspect | Parse Tree | AST | DAG |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parent node duplication for CSE** | No (every occurrence expanded) | No (every occurrence expanded) | **Yes — merged** |
| **Punctuation nodes** | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| **Non-terminal interior nodes** | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| **Used for parsing/validation** | Yes (front-end) | No (post-parse) | No (post-parse) |
| **Used for optimization** | No (too verbose) | Limited | **Yes (CSE, etc.)** |
| **Size order** | Largest | Middle | Smallest |
| **Multiple parents allowed** | No (tree) | No (tree) | **Yes (DAG)** |

## 3.5 The "Conversion Logic" for Every Tree Step

Whenever we convert **Parse Tree → AST**, the rule is:
- **Erase** every interior node whose label is a non-terminal.
- **Collapse** chains of single-child non-terminals into one node.
- **Promote** children directly to the grandparent.

When we convert **AST → DAG**:
- Walk the AST in post-order.
- For every node, check if an *equivalent* node already exists in the DAG (same operator + same children).
- If yes, return the existing node; else, create a new node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This post-order CSE-merging algorithm is what every compiler (GCC, LLVM, javac) implements in its IR-builder phase.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IR Taxonomy — The Three-Dimensional View

```mermaid
graph TD
    IR0[IR TAXONOMY] --> AX1[AXIS 1 STRUCTURAL LEVEL]
    IR0 --> AX2[AXIS 2 SPECIFICITY]
    IR0 --> AX3[AXIS 3 OPERATIONAL BEHAVIOR]
    AX1 --> L1[HIGH LEVEL IR]
    AX1 --> L2[MEDIUM LEVEL IR]
    AX1 --> L3[LOW LEVEL IR]
    L1 --> EX1[AST PARSE TREE]
    L2 --> EX2[3AC DAG SSA]
    L3 --> EX3[RTL MACHINE CODE]
    AX2 --> SP1[SOURCE RELATED]
    AX2 --> SP2[TARGET RELATED]
    AX2 --> SP3[INDEPENDENT]
    AX3 --> OB1[DECLARATIVE]
    AX3 --> OB2[IMPERATIVE]
    EX1 --> OB1
    EX2 --> OB2
    EX3 --> OB2
```

## 4.2 AST for the Expression `a + b * c`

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1((PLUS)) --> n2((a))
    n1 --> n3((STAR))
    n3 --> n4((b))
    n3 --> n5((c))
```

## 4.3 Parse Tree (Concrete) for `a + b * c` — Grammar `E -> E + T | T`, `T -> T * F | F`, `F -> id`

```mermaid
graph TD
    pt1((E)) --> pt2((E))
    pt1 --> pt3((PLUS))
    pt1 --> pt4((T))
    pt2 --> pt5((T))
    pt2 --> pt6((STAR))
    pt4 --> pt7((T))
    pt4 --> pt8((F))
    pt5 --> pt9((T))
    pt5 --> pt10((F))
    pt7 --> pt11((F))
    pt9 --> pt12((F))
    pt8 --> pt13((id a))
    pt11 --> pt14((id b))
    pt10 --> pt15((F))
    pt12 --> pt15
    pt15 --> pt16((id c))
```

> **Observation:** The parse tree has **17 nodes** (counting duplicates); the corresponding AST has only **6 nodes**. That is roughly a **2.8× reduction**.

## 4.4 DAG for `(a + b) * (a + b) + (a + b) * c` — Showing CSE

```mermaid
graph TD
    root((PLUS ROOT)) --> mul1((STAR))
    root --> mul2((STAR))
    mul1 --> shared((PLUS SHARED))
    mul1 --> shared
    mul2 --> shared
    mul2 --> cnode((c))
    shared --> anode((a))
    shared --> bnode((b))
```

> **Note the multiple parents** of the `PLUS SHARED` node — this is the defining feature of a DAG and the mechanism by which Common Subexpression Elimination is realized.

## 4.5 Control Flow Graph (CFG) for the `if-else` Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    b1((B1 ENTRY)) --> b2((B2 TEST X GT 0))
    b2 -->|true| b4((B4 Y EQUALS 1))
    b2 -->|false| b3((B3 Y EQUALS 2))
    b3 --> b5((B5 EXIT))
    b4 --> b5
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a Compiler Uses Multiple IRs

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[SOURCE CODE] --> FE[FRONT END]
    FE --> AST1[ABSTRACT SYNTAX TREE]
    AST1 --> TC[TYPE CHECKING]
    TC --> IR3AC[3 ADDRESS CODE MIR]
    IR3AC --> OPT[OPTIMIZATION PHASE]
    OPT --> IR3AC2[OPTIMIZED 3AC]
    IR3AC2 --> BE[BACK END]
    BE --> RTL[RTL LOW LEVEL IR]
    RTL --> ASM[TARGET ASSEMBLY]
    AST1 -.->|high level opt| OPT
    IR3AC -.->|SSA construction| OPT
```

> **Key Insight:** A modern compiler does **not** pick a single IR — it maintains a *pipeline* of IRs, each chosen for the analysis it best supports. The taxonomy you have just learned is the conceptual map of this pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define an Intermediate Representation. List any three desirable properties of an ideal IR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Intermediate Representation (IR)** is a data structure used internally by a compiler to represent the source program during translation. It sits between the front-end and the back-end and is independent of both the source language and the target machine.

Three desirable properties of an ideal IR:
1. **Ease of generation** — must be easy to produce from the source via the front-end.
2. **Ease of manipulation** — must support efficient analysis and transformation (e.g., for optimization).
3. **Translatability** — must be straightforward to translate into the target machine code.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for properties — 1 mark each if the property is named; 0.5 mark each if only listed without justification.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a Parse Tree and an Abstract Syntax Tree with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Parse Tree | Abstract Syntax Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Includes every grammar symbol, punctuation, and non-terminal | Abstracts away non-terminals and punctuation |
| Size | Large (often 2× to 5× the AST) | Compact, proportional to operators and operands |
| Use | Syntax checking by parser | Type checking, IR generation, optimization |

**Example — `a + b * c`:**
- **Parse Tree** has nodes for `E`, `E`, `T`, `F`, `+`, `*`, and each `id`.
- **AST** has just one `+` node at the root, one `*` child, and three leaf identifiers.

*(1 mark per row of comparison + 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are given below.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) Explain the IR Taxonomy proposed in the Dragon Book. Discuss the three structural levels with examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The IR Taxonomy classifies intermediate representations along three independent axes:

**Axis 1 — Structural Level** (also called "height" of the IR):
- **High-Level IR (HIR):** Closest to the source. Examples — **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**, **Parse Tree**. Preserves source-language constructs like `if-else`, `for`, and array accesses. Used for type checking and high-level optimizations such as inlining and constant folding at the language level.
- **Medium-Level IR (MIR):** Independent of both source and target. The classic example is **Three-Address Code (3AC)**, where each instruction has at most one operator on the right-hand side, e.g., `t1 = a + b`. Variants include **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** form, which adds a versioning discipline to variables. This is the level where most data-flow optimizations happen.
- **Low-Level IR (LIR):** Closest to the target. Examples — **Register Transfer Language (RTL)**, machine-specific instruction lists. Used for register allocation, instruction scheduling, and peephole optimization.

**Axis 2 — Specificity:** Whether the IR is source-related (AST), target-related (RTL), or neutral (3AC).

**Axis 3 — Operational Behavior:** Whether the IR is **declarative** (e.g., AST — describes the *what*) or **imperative** (e.g., 3AC — describes the *how* in a fixed order).

> A well-designed compiler (e.g., LLVM) chains multiple IRs: it consumes the **AST** from the front-end, lowers it to **LLVM IR** (a typed 3AC/SSA hybrid), and finally lowers that to **machineInstr** LIR per target. This staged translation is the **direct application** of the three-level taxonomy.

*(2 marks for the three levels + 2 marks for examples + 2 marks for the explanation of how they inter-relate + 1 mark for the conclusion / industrial example.)*

#### (b) Construct the **AST** and the **DAG** for the expression `a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d`. Identify the common subexpression and compute the compression factor. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify subexpressions:**
- Inner: $(b - c)$ — appears **twice** (this is the CSE).
- Outer: $a + a \cdot (b - c) + (b - c) \cdot d$.

**Step 2 — AST construction** (treating the second `a` as a distinct leaf, since it is syntactically separate but refers to the same identifier):

$$
\text{AST Node Set: } \{ \text{root }(+), (+)_{left}, (*), (-), (*)_2, (-)_2, a, a, b, c, b, c, d \}
$$

**AST Node Count:**

$$
N_{AST} = 4_{(operators)} + 1_{(root)} + 7_{(leaves)} = 12 \text{ nodes}
$$

**Step 3 — DAG construction** — the $(b - c)$ subexpression is shared:

$$
N_{DAG} = 4_{(operators)} + 1_{(root)} + 4_{(unique leaves)} = 9 \text{ nodes}
$$

**Step 4 — Compression factor:**

$$
\text{Compression Factor} = \frac{N_{AST}}{N_{DAG}} = \frac{12}{9} = 1.33
$$

The $(b-c)$ operator node count dropped from 2 to 1 — a 50% reduction for that subexpression.

**The DAG (described):** A single shared `−` node with children $b$ and $c$; two $\times$ nodes each pointing to the shared `−`; a top-level `+` combining the inner `+` (which has $a$ and $a$ as children) with the second $\times$ (which has the shared `−` and $d$).

*(2 marks for AST diagram/description + 2 marks for DAG with shared node + 2 marks for node counts and compression factor + 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) What are Graphical Intermediate Representations? List and briefly explain the **syntax-related trees** and the **graphs** used as IRs in a modern compiler. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Graphical IRs** represent a program as a graph in which nodes denote program entities (operators, statements, basic blocks) and edges denote relationships (data flow, control flow, lexical containment).

**1. Syntax-Related Trees:**
- **Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree):** The literal derivation tree from the context-free grammar; contains every non-terminal and terminal.
- **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST):** A compact tree that retains only operators (internal nodes) and operands (leaves); strips away non-terminals and punctuation. It is the standard HIR.
- **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):** A compact, *shared* version of the AST in which common subexpressions are represented by a single node with multiple parents. It enables Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) at the IR level.

**2. Graphs:**
- **Control Flow Graph (CFG):** Nodes are *basic blocks*; edges denote possible transfer of control. Used for almost every data-flow optimization.
- **Data Flow Graph (DFG):** Nodes are operations; edges denote data dependencies. The basis of instruction scheduling.
- **Dependence Graph:** A unified DFG + control-dependence graph; used in vectorization and parallelization.
- **Call Graph:** Nodes are functions; edges are `calls` relationships; used for interprocedural analysis.

**Why they matter:** Each graphical IR exposes a *specific kind of structural regularity* (syntactic, control, data, or inter-procedural) to the optimization phase, allowing each analysis to be expressed compactly.

*(1 mark for definition + 3 marks for syntax-related trees + 3 marks for graphs.)*

#### (b) Construct the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** for the following 3-address code segment. Clearly identify the basic blocks and label all edges. (7 marks)

**3-Address Code:**

```
1.  i = 1
2.  j = 1
3.  t1 = 10 * i
4.  t2 = t1 + j
5.  if t2 > 100 goto L1
6.  i = i + 1
7.  j = j + 1
8.  goto L2
L1: end
L2: (label for the loop back-target)
```

> *Note: In practice, the loop back-target is the first instruction (line 3 in a `do-while` style). For the purposes of this question, we treat L2 as the label on the start of the loop body.*

**Step 1 — Identify basic blocks:**

| Block | Lines |
|---|---|
| **B1** (entry) | 1, 2 |
| **B2** (loop body) | 3, 4, 5 |
| **B3** (increment) | 6, 7, 8 |
| **B4** (exit) | L1 (end) |

**Step 2 — Draw the edges:**

$$
B1 \to B2, \quad B2 \xrightarrow{\text{true}} B4, \quad B2 \xrightarrow{\text{false}} B3, \quad B3 \to B2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mis-identifying basic blocks.** A basic block ends at a *branch*, *jump target*, or *jump itself*. Many students incorrectly include the branch target *inside* the block.
> 2. **Forgetting the back-edge.** For a loop, B3 must have an edge back to B2; this is what makes the graph a *graph* and not a tree.
> 3. **Not distinguishing the two outgoing edges of B2** with labels (`true`/`false` or `T`/`F`).

**Step 3 — Final CFG:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    bb1((B1 ENTRY)) --> bb2((B2 LOOP BODY))
    bb2 -->|true| bb4((B4 EXIT END))
    bb2 -->|false| bb3((B3 INCREMENT))
    bb3 --> bb2
```

*(1 mark for basic-block identification + 2 marks for edge list + 2 marks for the graph + 2 marks for labels/loop back-edge.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 IRs**

- [ ] **IR = bridge** between front-end and back-end; **machine-independent**, but lower than source.
- [ ] **IR Taxonomy** has **three axes**: Structural Level (HIR/MIR/LIR), Specificity (source/target/neutral), and Operational Behavior (declarative/imperative).
- [ ] **High-Level IR** = AST, parse tree — used for type checking and high-level opts.
- [ ] **Medium-Level IR** = 3AC, SSA, DAG — used for most optimizations.
- [ ] **Low-Level IR** = RTL, machine instructions — used for register allocation and scheduling.
- [ ] **Parse Tree** = full grammar derivation; **largest**, includes every symbol.
- [ ] **AST** = abstract; strips non-terminals and punctuation; **internal nodes are operators, leaves are operands**.
- [ ] **DAG** = AST with **Common Subexpression Elimination**; nodes can have **multiple parents** — this is the defining feature.
- [ ] **Parse Tree size** $\approx 2n + m + 1$; **AST size** $\approx n + m + 1$; **DAG size** $\leq n + m + 1$ (where $n$ = operator nodes, $m$ = unique operands).
- [ ] **CFG** nodes = basic blocks; edges = control flow; **has cycles** (loops).
- [ ] **DAG** is **acyclic by definition** (otherwise it is a CFG).
- [ ] **DAG construction** uses **post-order traversal** with CSE merging.
- [ ] **Call Graph** = interprocedural IR; nodes = functions.
- [ ] Modern compilers (LLVM, GCC) use **multiple IRs** in a pipeline: AST → 3AC/SSA → RTL.
- [ ] A node with **multiple parents in a DAG** $\Rightarrow$ **CSE** detected.
- [ ] Vertical-bar notation in tables: $\vert B \vert$ means *cardinality of set $B$* (number of basic blocks).

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Losing-Marks Trap:**  
> Students often confuse *Parse Tree* vs *AST* vs *DAG* sizes. Memorize the formulas $2n+m+1$ vs $n+m+1$ vs $\leq n+m+1$ and the rule that a **DAG node can have multiple parents** while a **tree node cannot**. KTU examiners ask this **every year**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code, Three-Address Code, Representing Linear Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code, Three-Address Code, Representing Linear Codes # Module 3 : Linear Intermediate Representations (IRs) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are Linear Intermediate Representations? In the architecture of a modern optimizing compiler, the source code of a high-level programming language (like C, Java, or Python) is not directly translated into the target machine language. Instead, it is transformed progressively through one or more **Intermediate Representations (IRs)**. An IR acts as a "bridge language" — a structured, machine-independent abstraction that sits between the front-end (which understands the source language) and the back-end (which produces target machine code). Among the various flavors of IRs, **Linear IRs** (also called **one-address-at-a-time** or **linear code IRs**) are the simplest and most classical. In a Linear IR, the program is represented as a **simple, sequential list (or sequence) of instructions**. There is no nested structure, no complex control-flow graph with branching edges, and no hierarchical expression trees. Each instruction is processed in the order it appears, much like reading a book from top to bottom. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Linear IR):** A Linear IR is an intermediate code representation in which the program is organized as a **sequential, ordered list of instructions** without a built-in hierarchy or graph structure. The two principal members of this family are **Stack-Machine Code** and **Three-Address Code (TAC)**. ### 1.2 The Two Principal Families of Linear IR Linear IRs come in two dominant flavors, each suited for a different kind of compilation pipeline: 1. **Stack-Machine Code** — A "compact" Linear IR where instructions implicitly operate on a **last-in, first-out (LIFO) operand stack**. The operands are not named inside the instruction itself; they live on the stack. 2. **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — A "verbose" Linear IR where each instruction explicitly names up to **three operands**: one for the destination (result) and up to two for the source (operands). This is the **de-facto standard** used in production compilers such as LLVM, GCC, and the earlier Turbo/Coffee compilers. > [!NOTE] > **Why Use Linear IRs at All?** Linear IRs are highly portable, easy to generate from a syntax tree, easy to print and debug, and trivial to optimize using simple local peephole or data-flow techniques. They form the analytical sweet spot between the high-level AST and the low-level target machine code. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Two Kitchens Imagine you are a head chef who must pass recipes to two very different line cooks: - **The Stack-Machine Cook** is like a chef who works at a **single stack of plates**. You say: *"Put tomatoes on the stack, put basil on the stack, chop the top two ingredients together."* The chef doesn't need to remember the names of the ingredients — the **stack itself** is the workspace. This is efficient but hard to keep track of if you have many simultaneous dishes. - **The Three-Address Cook** is like a chef who insists on **labeled bowls**. You say: *"Put the chopped tomatoes into Bowl A, the chopped basil into Bowl B, then put the mixture of A and B into Bowl C."* Every ingredient is explicitly named, every result is stored in a named container. This is more verbose but far easier to debug, reorder, and analyze. The source code is your master recipe. The Linear IR is the **set of precise step-by-step instructions** you hand to the cooks. ### 1.4 Physical / Operational Metrics | Parameter | Stack-Machine Code | Three-Address Code | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Implicit Workspace** | Operand Stack | Named Temporaries / Variables | | **Operand Count per Instruction** | Variable (usually 0 or 1) | Up to **3** | | **Compactness** | **High** (smaller IR size) | **Lower** (larger IR size) | | **Optimization Ease** | Harder (implicit operands) | Easier (explicit operands) | | **Real-World Example** | JVM Bytecode, MSIL (.NET) | LLVM IR, GCC RTL | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stack-Machine Code: The Theory ### 2.1.1 Architecture of a Stack Machine In a **Stack-Machine IR**, every instruction operates on an implicit **operand stack** maintained conceptually by the compiler or runtime. The stack is the memory of the machine. The instructions themselves are of two broad classes: 1. **Stack-Pushing Instructions**: Bring a value onto the top of the stack. * `PUSH c` — Push the integer constant $c$ onto the stack. * `PUSH x` — Push the value of variable $x$ onto the stack. 2. **Stack-Popping (or Compute-and-Push) Instructions**: Pop one or more operands from the stack, perform an operation, and push the result back onto the stack. * `ADD` — Pop the top two values, compute their sum, push the result. * `MUL`, `SUB`, `DIV`, `MOD`, `NEG`, etc., follow the same pattern. * Comparison operators (`CMP`, `EQ`, `LT`) typically pop two values and push either `1` (true) or `0` (false). * Store-back instructions like `STORE x` pop a value from the stack and assign it to variable $x$. ### 2.1.2 Execution Semantics Let the stack be denoted by a top-pointer $\top$. After the instruction `PUSH v`, the stack state evolves as: $$\top \leftarrow \top + 1$$ $$\text{stack}[\top] \leftarrow v$$ For a binary arithmetic operator $\oplus$, the `ADD` instruction performs: $$a \leftarrow \text{stack}[\top]$$ $$b \leftarrow \text{stack}[\top - 1]$$ $$\top \leftarrow \top - 1$$ $$\text{stack}[\top] \leftarrow a \oplus b$$ ### 2.1.3 Worked Example: Translating an Expression Consider the high-level arithmetic expression: $$E = (a + b) \cdot c$$ The Stack-Machine Code representation is shown in the table below: | Step | Instruction | Stack State (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) | | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | `PUSH a` | `..., a` | | 2 | `PUSH b` | `..., a, b` | | 3 | `ADD` | `..., (a+b)` | | 4 | `PUSH c` | `..., (a+b), c` | | 5 | `MUL` | `..., (a+b)*c` | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Notice that the operators do not explicitly mention their operands. The operand order is **implicitly encoded** by their position on the stack. This makes Stack-Machine code very **compact** but harder to read and optimize, because the compiler must perform **stack simulation** to understand what each instruction actually computes. ### 2.1.4 Advantages & Disadvantages of Stack-Machine Code **Advantages:** * **Compactness:** Each instruction is typically 1 byte (the opcode) with optional operands, making the IR very small in memory. * **Simple Code Generation:** Translating an expression tree to stack code is trivial — it's essentially a **post-order tree traversal**. * **Easy to Interpret:** A simple interpreter loop (`fetch` $\rightarrow$ `decode` $\rightarrow$ `execute`) can run stack-machine code without complex register allocation. **Disadvantages:** * **Implicit Data Flow:** The same value at different points in the program may sit in different stack positions, making data-flow analysis complicated. * **Stack Depth Ambiguity:** At any point in the program, the **stack height is not statically known** without simulating the code. * **Poor Locality:** Values flow on and off the stack, making it harder to keep them in fast CPU registers. --- ## 2.2 Three-Address Code (TAC): The Theory ### 2.2.1 The Canonical Form **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is the most important and widely-used Linear IR in compiler construction. It is sometimes abbreviated as **3AC** or referred to as **3-Address Code**. Its defining property is that every instruction contains at most **three operands (addresses)**: typically one destination and two sources. The canonical form of a TAC instruction is: $$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$ Where: * $x$ is the destination operand (where the result is stored). * $y$ and $z$ are the source operands (inputs to the operation). * `op` is a **binary operator** (e.g., `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `&&`, `<<`, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Three" in Three-Address Code:** Each instruction involves at most **three addresses (names/locations)** — one for the destination and two for the operands. Note that **assignment statements** like `x = y` and **unary operations** like `x = -y` are *also* valid TAC instructions; they simply use fewer addresses. ### 2.2.2 Categories of TAC Instructions A complete TAC instruction set covers: 1. **Binary Arithmetic / Logic:** `x = y + z`, `x = y * z`, `x = y AND z`, etc. 2. **Assignment / Copy:** `x = y`. 3. **Unary Operations:** `x = -y`, `x = NOT y`, `x = y`. 4. **Pointer / Address Operations:** `x = &y` (address-of), `x = *y` (dereference), `*x = y` (store through pointer). 5. **Array / Structure Access:** Often desugared into pointer arithmetic, e.g., `x = y[i]` becomes `x = *(y + i)`. 6. **Conditional / Unconditional Jumps:** `if x goto L`, `if False x goto L`, `goto L`. 7. **Procedure Calls:** `param x`, `call p, n` (call procedure $p$ with $n$ parameters), `x = call p`, `return y`. 8. **Indexed Copy:** `x[i] = y`, `x = y[i]`. ### 2.2.3 The Role of Temporaries Because TAC restricts each instruction to a single operator, **complex expressions must be broken down** into a sequence of simpler instructions using **temporary variables** (often called `t1`, `t2`, `t3`, ...). Consider the source expression: $$E = (a + b) \cdot c - d / e$$ Translated into TAC, this becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ t_2 &= t_1 \cdot c \\ t_3 &= d / e \\ t_4 &= t_2 - t_3 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Temporaries Matter:** Temporaries break the inherent tree structure of an expression into a linear sequence. This **linearization** is precisely what makes TAC amenable to optimization. Each temporary is a named storage location that holds an intermediate value, which can later be tracked, propagated, or eliminated by an optimizer. ### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages of Three-Address Code **Advantages:** * **Explicit Data Flow:** Every value is named, making **def-use chains**, **liveness analysis**, and **register allocation** straightforward. * **Optimization-Friendly:** Local optimizations (constant folding, copy propagation, common subexpression elimination) are natural on TAC. * **Human-Readable:** TAC can be printed in a clear, assembly-like format for debugging. * **Decoupled from Machine:** The same TAC can be retargeted to multiple architectures. **Disadvantages:** * **Larger Code Size:** Each complex expression expands into many TAC instructions. * **More Compiler Effort:** Generating optimal TAC requires care with temporary naming and ordering. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes all essential notations, instruction forms, and data-structure parameters for Linear IRs. **Memorize this for Module 3.** | **Concept** | **Symbol / Form** | **Meaning / Property** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Stack Instruction (Push)** | `PUSH v` | $\top \leftarrow \top + 1;\; \text{stack}[\top] \leftarrow v$ | | **Stack Instruction (Pop)** | `POP` | $\top \leftarrow \top - 1$ | | **Stack Instruction (Binary)** | `ADD` | $a \leftarrow \text{stack}[\top];\; b \leftarrow \text{stack}[\top-1];\; \text{stack}[\top-1] \leftarrow a \oplus b$ | | **TAC General Form** | $x = y\;\text{op}\;z$ | $x$ = destination, $y,z$ = sources, `op` = binary operator | | **TAC Unary Form** | $x = \text{op}\;y$ | e.g., $x = -y$, $x = \text{NOT}\; y$ | | **TAC Copy** | $x = y$ | Assignment without arithmetic | | **TAC Branch** | `if x goto L` | If $x$ is true, jump to label $L$ | | **TUC Procedure Call** | `call p, n` | Invoke procedure $p$ with $n$ arguments | | **Quadruple Fields** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | 4-tuple representation of a TAC instruction | | **Triple Fields** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | 3-tuple; operands may reference other triples | | **Indirect Triple** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` + pointer list | Triples ordered via separate indirection array | | **SSA Form** | Each var defined exactly once | Static Single Assignment; uses $\phi$-functions | | **TAC Compactness Ratio** | $S_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Stack IR} \vert}{\vert \text{TAC IR} \vert}$ | Usually $S_{\text{ratio}} \in [0.4, 0.7]$ | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The choice between Stack-Machine Code and Three-Address Code is not academic — it has direct engineering consequences in industry: * **Stack-Machine IRs in Production:** The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** uses a stack-based bytecode. The **.NET Common Intermediate Language (CIL/MSIL)** is also stack-based. The **WebAssembly (Wasm)** binary format uses a stack-based encoding. These choices were made because stack code is **dense** (smaller file sizes, faster network transmission) and **simple to verify** (verifiers can run a quick stack simulation). * **Three-Address IRs in Production:** **LLVM IR** (the backbone of `clang`, `rustc`, `swiftc`) is a 3-address SSA-based IR. **GCC's GIMPLE and RTL** are TAC derivatives. **Java's HotSpot Server Compiler** also lowers stack-based bytecode into a 3-address IR before heavy optimization. TAC's explicit naming is essential for the sophisticated optimizations these compilers perform. * **Compiler Pipeline Insight:** Many modern compilers use **both**. They accept a stack-based bytecode (for compactness during distribution) but quickly **lower it to TAC** (or SSA) for analysis and optimization. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through the complete, line-by-line translation of source-level constructs into both Stack-Machine Code and Three-Address Code. Every algebraic and semantic step is shown explicitly. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Stack-Machine Code for a Complex Expression **Source Expression:** $$E = (a \cdot b) + (c - d) / e$$ ### Step-by-Step Translation We perform a **post-order traversal** of the expression tree. The leaves (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`) are pushed; the internal nodes consume operands and push results. | Line # | Instruction | Stack Effect (Top of Stack $\rightarrow$ Bottom) | Explanation | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `PUSH a` | `a` | Load the value of $a$ onto the stack. | | 2 | `PUSH b` | `b, a` | Load the value of $b$ onto the stack. | | 3 | `MUL` | `(a*b)` | Pop the top two values ($b$ then $a$), compute $a \cdot b$, push result. | | 4 | `PUSH c` | `c, (a*b)` | Load the value of $c$ onto the stack. | | 5 | `PUSH d` | `d, c, (a*b)` | Load the value of $d$ onto the stack. | | 6 | `SUB` | `(c-d), (a*b)` | Pop the top two values ($d$ then $c$), compute $c - d$, push result. | | 7 | `PUSH e` | `e, (c-d), (a*b)` | Load the value of $e$ onto the stack. | | 8 | `DIV` | `((c-d)/e), (a*b)` | Pop the top two values ($e$ then $c-d$), compute $(c-d)/e$, push result. | | 9 | `ADD` | `((a*b) + (c-d)/e)` | Pop the top two values, compute the final sum, push result. | > [!NOTE] > **Stack Depth Analysis:** The **maximum stack depth** required to evaluate this expression is **3** (after line 7). The **final stack depth** is **1**. A compiler must guarantee that the stack never exceeds the available memory at runtime. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Three-Address Code for a Complex Expression **Same Source Expression:** $$E = (a \cdot b) + (c - d) / e$$ ### Step-by-Step Translation We introduce fresh temporaries $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ for each sub-expression. $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a \cdot b \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 1: compute } a \cdot b \text{)} \\ t_2 &= c - d \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 2: compute } c - d \text{)} \\ t_3 &= t_2 \;/\; e \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 3: divide by } e \text{)} \\ t_4 &= t_1 + t_3 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 4: sum the two halves)} \end{aligned} $$ Notice that the **stack code requires 9 instructions** while the **TAC requires only 4**. However, the TAC is more explicit and easier for an optimizer to manipulate. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Three-Address Code for Control Flow **Source Code:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{if } (x < y) \text{ then} \\ &\quad z = x + y; \\ &\text{else} \\ &\quad z = x - y; \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-Step Translation We use **labels** ($L_1, L_2$, etc.) and **conditional jumps**. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{if False } x < y \text{ goto } L_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(evaluate condition; jump to else if false)} \\ &t_1 &= x + y \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(then-branch: } z = x+y \text{)} \\ &z &= t_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(assign result to } z \text{)} \\ &\text{goto } L_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(skip the else-branch)} \\ &L_1:\; t_2 &= x - y \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(else-branch: } z = x-y \text{)} \\ &z &= t_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(assign result to } z \text{)} \\ &L_2:\; \text{(end of if-then-else)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pattern Recognition:** This "if-False goto" pattern followed by "goto" after the then-branch is the **canonical short-circuit translation** for `if-then-else` in TAC. KTU examiners frequently test this pattern. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Three-Address Code for a `while` Loop **Source Code:** $$ \begin{aligned} &i = 1; \\ &\text{while } (i < 100) \text{ do} \\ &\quad \text{sum} = \text{sum} + i; \\ &\quad i = i + 1; \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-Step Translation $$ \begin{aligned} i &= 1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(initialize loop counter)} \\ L_1: \text{ if False } i < 100 \text{ goto } L_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(loop guard)} \\ t_1 &= \text{sum} + i \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(loop body: compute sum)} \\ \text{sum} &= t_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(store back)} \\ t_2 &= i + 1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(increment counter)} \\ i &= t_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(update counter)} \\ \text{goto } L_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(back-edge of the loop)} \\ L_2:\; \text{(after the loop)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Comparison This matrix gives a definitive side-by-side comparison for the same source code, useful for KTU 14-mark answers. | Source Construct | Stack-Machine Code | Three-Address Code | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `x = 5` | `PUSH 5`
`STORE x` | `x = 5` | | `x = y + z` | `PUSH y`
`PUSH z`
`ADD`
`STORE x` | `t1 = y + z`
`x = t1` | | `x = -y` | `PUSH y`
`NEG`
`STORE x` | `t1 = -y`
`x = t1` | | `x = a[i]` | `PUSH a`
`PUSH i`
`INDEX`
`STORE x` | `t1 = a + i`
`x = *t1` | | `a[i] = x` | `PUSH a`
`PUSH i`
`INDEX`
`PUSH x`
`STORE_T` | `t1 = a + i`
`*t1 = x` | | `goto L` | `BR L` | `goto L` | | `if x < y goto L` | `PUSH x`
`PUSH y`
`CMP`
`BL L` (branch if less) | `if False x < y goto L_next`
`goto L` | --- ## 3.6 Derivation: Quantitative Compactness Analysis **Claim:** Stack-Machine Code is more compact than TAC for purely arithmetic expressions. **Setup:** Consider a balanced binary expression tree with $n$ leaf operands. The tree has $n - 1$ internal (operator) nodes and $n - 1$ edges. **Stack-Machine Code Size ($S_{\text{stack}}$):** * Each leaf operand requires exactly **1 instruction** (a `PUSH`). * Each internal operator requires exactly **1 instruction** (e.g., `ADD`, `MUL`). $$S_{\text{stack}} = n + (n - 1) = 2n - 1$$ **Three-Address Code Size ($S_{\text{TAC}}$):** * Each leaf operand becomes part of an instruction (no separate push needed if it appears directly). * Each internal operator node requires **1 TAC instruction** using a fresh temporary. $$S_{\text{TAC}} = n - 1$$ Wait — this simple analysis shows TAC is *shorter* for arithmetic expressions! The compactness advantage of stack code appears primarily when **operand encoding is variable-length**. For example, on the JVM, a single-byte opcode (`iadd`, `imul`) is paired with one-byte local-variable indices, whereas TAC requires an explicit 4-byte operand per field in a quadruple. **Refined Analysis (with quadruple overhead):** Assume each TAC quadruple has a fixed overhead of $K$ bytes per instruction (typically $K = 16$ bytes in a naive implementation, or $K = 8$ bytes in a packed form), while each stack instruction takes only $1$ byte for the opcode plus variable operand sizes. $$S_{\text{stack}} = 2n - 1 \;\;\text{(bytes, approximate)}$$ $$S_{\text{TAC}} = K \cdot (n - 1) \;\;\text{(bytes)}$$ Setting $S_{\text{stack}} < S_{\text{TAC}}$: $$2n - 1 < K(n - 1)$$ Solving for $n$: $$2n - 1 < Kn - K$$ $$K - 1 < (K - 2)n$$ $$n > \dfrac{K - 1}{K - 2}$$ For $K = 4$, we get $n > 1.5$, i.e., **for any expression with more than one operand, stack code is more compact**. This quantifies why production VM designers prefer stack code for distribution. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Linear IRs Fit in a Compiler The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a compiler's middle-end, showing exactly where Linear IRs are created and consumed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Code in HLL] --> B[Lexical Analyzer] B --> C[Syntax Analyzer] C --> D[Abstract Syntax Tree AST] D --> E[Semantic Analyzer] E --> F[AST with Type Info] F --> G1[Stack Code Generator] F --> G2[TAC Code Generator] G1 --> H1[Stack Machine IR] G2 --> H2[Three Address Code IR] H1 --> I[IR Selector Lowering Stage] H2 --> I I --> J[Optimizer SSA Form] J --> K[Optimized Linear IR] K --> L[Instruction Selector] L --> M[Target Machine Code] M --> N[Final Executable] ``` **Diagram Explanation:** * The **AST** (Abstract Syntax Tree) is the hierarchical representation output by the parser. * Either a **Stack-Code Generator** or a **TAC Generator** can be invoked to produce a Linear IR. * Both IR flavors are typically **lowered into a unified SSA-based IR** for optimization. * The **Instruction Selector** then maps the optimized IR to target machine code. --- ## 4.2 Data-Structure Schematic: Representing TAC with Quadruples ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["Quadruple 1: op=ADD, arg1=t1, arg2=t2, result=t3"] --> Q2["Quadruple 2: op=MUL, arg1=t3, arg2=c, result=t4"] Q2 --> Q3["Quadruple 3: op=STORE, arg1=t4, arg2=null, result=x"] Q3 --> Q4["Quadruple 4: op=GOTO, arg1=L1, arg2=null, result=null"] ``` **Explanation:** * Each box represents one **quadruple** (a 4-field tuple) stored in an array or linked list. * The quadruples are stored in **sequential order** matching the program's execution order. * Jumps (`GOTO L1`) reference labels, which are resolved later by **back-patching** during code generation. --- ## 4.3 Data-Structure Schematic: Representing TAC with Triples ```mermaid flowchart LR T0["Triple 0: op=PUSH, arg1=a, arg2=null"] --> T1["Triple 1: op=PUSH, arg1=b, arg2=null"] T1 --> T2["Triple 2: op=ADD, arg1=(0), arg2=(1)"] T2 --> T3["Triple 3: op=PUSH, arg1=c, arg2=null"] T3 --> T4["Triple 4: op=MUL, arg1=(2), arg2=(3)"] ``` **Explanation:** * A **triple** has only 3 fields: `op`, `arg1`, `arg2`. * When a triple needs to reference another triple's result, it uses the **index** of that triple inside parentheses, e.g., `arg1 = (0)` means "the result of triple #0". * Triples are more space-efficient than quadruples but harder to optimize (moving a triple changes its index, breaking references). --- ## 4.4 Translation Pipeline Schematic: Stack $\rightarrow$ TAC $\rightarrow$ SSA ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC["Source Expression: a + b * c"] --> S1["PUSH a"] S1 --> S2["PUSH b"] S2 --> S3["PUSH c"] S3 --> S4["MUL"] S4 --> S5["ADD"] S5 --> TAC1["t1 = b * c"] TAC1 --> TAC2["t2 = a + t1"] TAC2 --> SSA1["t1 = b_0 * c_0"] SSA1 --> SSA2["t2 = a_0 + t1"] ``` **Explanation:** * The top half shows the **Stack-Machine Code** representation. * The middle half shows the corresponding **TAC** representation. * The bottom half shows the **SSA (Static Single Assignment)** form — note the subscripts (`a_0`, `b_0`, `c_0`) which indicate version numbers. SSA extends TAC with renaming so every variable is defined exactly once. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > Explain **Stack-Machine Code** as a Linear Intermediate Representation. Mention any two real-world systems that use it. **Model Answer:** Stack-Machine Code is a type of linear intermediate representation in which instructions operate on an implicit last-in-first-out (LIFO) operand stack. There are two main classes of instructions: * **Push instructions** (e.g., `PUSH a`, `PUSH 5`) place values onto the top of the stack. * **Compute-and-push instructions** (e.g., `ADD`, `MUL`) pop the required number of operands from the stack, perform the operation, and push the result. **Key Properties:** 1. The operands are **not named** inside the instruction; they are tracked via their stack position. 2. The IR is **compact** because each instruction is short (typically one byte opcode). 3. The code is generated naturally by a **post-order traversal** of the expression tree. **Two Real-World Systems:** * **Java Virtual Machine (JVM) Bytecode** * **Microsoft Intermediate Language (MSIL)** for the .NET Framework Other valid examples include **WebAssembly (Wasm)** and **Forth**. **[Marks Distribution: Definition - 1, Properties - 1, Examples - 1]** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` > What is **Three-Address Code (TAC)**? Write the canonical form of a TAC instruction and explain the role of **temporary variables**. **Model Answer:** Three-Address Code (TAC) is a linear intermediate representation in which each instruction contains at most **three operands (addresses)**: one destination and up to two source operands. **Canonical Form:** $$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$ Where $x$ is the destination, $y$ and $z$ are the source operands, and `op` is a binary operator. **Role of Temporary Variables:** Temporary variables (e.g., $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots$) are used to break down **complex expressions** into a sequence of simple instructions, each performing only **one operator**. For example, the expression `(a + b) * c` requires two TAC instructions: $$t_1 = a + b$$ $$t_2 = t_1 * c$$ Temporaries provide **explicit names** to intermediate values, which makes the IR amenable to data-flow analysis and optimizations such as copy propagation, constant folding, and dead-code elimination. **[Marks Distribution: TAC definition - 1, Canonical form - 1, Role of temporaries - 1]** --- ## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > * When translating control flow, students **commonly forget the terminating `goto`** after the `then`-branch of an `if-then-else`, causing fall-through into the `else`-branch. Always emit a `goto L_end` immediately after the `then`-branch. > * When writing Stack-Machine code, students **often forget that binary operators consume two operands** and produce one — write out the stack effect explicitly to avoid partial-credit loss. > * When asked for **Quadruples vs Triples**, do not just write the structure; **clearly state the trade-off** (quadruples are easier to optimize; triples save space). --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) > **(a)** Translate the following C-style expression into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** and into **Stack-Machine Code**. Show the step-by-step translation with temporaries and stack states. **(7 Marks)** > > `result = (p - q) * (r + s) / t` > > **(b)** Write the TAC representation of the following C-style code fragment, clearly showing labels, conditional jumps, and unconditional jumps. **(7 Marks)** > > ```c > if (a > b) { > x = a + b; > } else { > x = a - b; > } > if (x == 0) { > y = 100; > } > ``` --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a): Expression Translation **Three-Address Code (TAC):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= p - q \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(evaluate left product operand)} \\ t_2 &= r + s \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(evaluate right product operand)} \\ t_3 &= t_1 \cdot t_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(perform multiplication)} \\ t_4 &= t_3 \;/\; t \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(perform division)} \\ \text{result} &= t_4 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(store into result)} \end{aligned} $$ **Stack-Machine Code (with stack state at each step):** | Line | Instruction | Stack (top $\rightarrow$ bottom) | Explanation | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | `PUSH p` | `p` | Load $p$ | | 2 | `PUSH q` | `q, p` | Load $q$ | | 3 | `SUB` | `(p-q)` | Compute $p-q$ | | 4 | `PUSH r` | `r, (p-q)` | Load $r$ | | 5 | `PUSH s` | `s, r, (p-q)` | Load $s$ | | 6 | `ADD` | `(r+s), (p-q)` | Compute $r+s$ | | 7 | `MUL` | `((p-q)*(r+s))` | Compute product | | 8 | `PUSH t` | `t, ((p-q)*(r+s))` | Load $t$ | | 9 | `DIV` | `(((p-q)*(r+s))/t)` | Compute division | | 10 | `STORE result` | (empty) | Store into `result` | **Maximum Stack Depth:** 3 elements (after line 5). --- #### Part (b): Control-Flow Translation We translate the two `if` statements as a **sequence** with appropriate labels. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{if False } a > b \text{ goto } L_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 1: check outer if-condition)} \\ &t_1 &= a + b \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 2: then-branch of outer if)} \\ &x &= t_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 3: assign to } x \text{)} \\ &\text{goto } L_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 4: skip else-branch)} \\ &L_1:\; t_2 &= a - b \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 5: else-branch of outer if)} \\ &x &= t_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 6: assign to } x \text{)} \\ &L_2:\; \text{if False } x == 0 \text{ goto } L_3 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 7: check inner if-condition)} \\ &y &= 100 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(line 8: inner then-branch)} \\ &L_3:\; \text{(continue)} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Points Checked by Examiner:** * Correct use of `if False` to invert the condition. ✓ * Explicit `goto L_2` after the then-branch. ✓ * Distinct labels `L_1`, `L_2`, `L_3` for each block. ✓ * Use of temporaries $t_1, t_2$. ✓ --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` (14 Marks) > **(a)** Explain the three principal data structures used to represent Three-Address Code: **Quadruples**, **Triples**, and **Indirect Triples**. For each, give the structure and one advantage and one disadvantage. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Consider the source expression `x = a[i] + b[j]`. Generate the corresponding **Three-Address Code** and then represent it using **Quadruples** in a clear table. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a): Data Structures for TAC Representation **1. Quadruples (4-tuples):** A quadruple represents each TAC instruction with **four fields**: $$\text{quadruple} = (\text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2}, \text{result})$$ * **op**: the operator * **arg1**, **arg2**: source operands (may be null for unary ops) * **result**: destination operand * **Advantage:** Because the `result` is an **explicit named location**, instructions can be moved or reordered during optimization without invalidating references. * **Disadvantage:** Requires more memory (four fields per instruction) and an auxiliary symbol table for temporary names. **2. Triples (3-tuples):** A triple uses only **three fields**: $$\text{triple} = (\text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2})$$ * **op**: the operator * **arg1**, **arg2**: source operands; if the source is the result of a *previous* triple, it is referenced by the **index** of that triple (e.g., `(0)`, `(1)`). * **Advantage:** Saves space — no need for a `result` field or temporary names; references are direct indices. * **Disadvantage:** When the optimizer **moves a triple**, its index changes, and all references to it become invalid. Reordering triples is costly. **3. Indirect Triples:** Indirect Triples decouple the *execution order* from the *storage location* by using an extra layer of indirection. * The triples themselves are stored in a pool (like Triples). * A separate **list of pointers** (`instruction list`) defines the execution order by pointing into the triple pool. * **Advantage:** Reordering or moving instructions is **cheap** — only the pointer list needs updating, not the triples themselves. * **Disadvantage:** Adds an extra level of indirection, slightly slowing access at runtime or during optimization. **Summary Table:** | Structure | Fields | Space | Reorder Cost | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Quadruple | 4 | High | Low | | Triple | 3 | Low | High | | Indirect Triple | 3 + pointer list | Medium | Low | --- #### Part (b): TAC and Quadruple Generation for `x = a[i] + b[j]` **Step 1: Generate the TAC.** We treat array access as pointer arithmetic. Let the base address of `a` be conceptually `a_base`, and similarly for `b`. $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= i \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{element}) \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(compute offset for } a[i] \text{)} \\ t_2 &= a + t_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(address of } a[i] \text{)} \\ t_3 &= *t_2 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(load value of } a[i] \text{)} \\ t_4 &= j \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{element}) \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(compute offset for } b[j] \text{)} \\ t_5 &= b + t_4 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(address of } b[j] \text{)} \\ t_6 &= *t_5 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(load value of } b[j] \text{)} \\ t_7 &= t_3 + t_6 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(perform the addition)} \\ x &= t_7 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(store into } x \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ For simplicity, if we assume $\text{sizeof}(\text{element}) = 1$, the offsets reduce to: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a[i] \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(i.e., load } a[i] \text{ directly)} \\ t_2 &= b[j] \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(i.e., load } b[j] \text{ directly)} \\ t_3 &= t_1 + t_2 \\ x &= t_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Represent as Quadruples.** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | (0) | `[]=` | `a` | `i` | `t1` | | (1) | `[]=` | `b` | `j` | `t2` | | (2) | `+` | `t1` | `t2` | `t3` | | (3) | `=` | `t3` | — | `x` | **Reading the Table:** * Quadruple `(0)` reads `a[i]` and stores the result in temporary `t1`. * Quadruple `(1)` reads `b[j]` and stores the result in temporary `t2`. * Quadruple `(2)` adds `t1` and `t2`, storing in `t3`. * Quadruple `(3)` copies `t3` into `x`. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 (Linear IRs):** * ✅ **Linear IR = Sequential instruction list** (no nested/graph structure). Two main types: **Stack-Machine Code** and **Three-Address Code (TAC)**. * ✅ **Stack-Machine Code** uses an **implicit operand stack**. Instructions are compact (`PUSH`, `POP`, `ADD`, etc.). Used by JVM, MSIL, Wasm. * ✅ **TAC** has canonical form **$x = y\;\text{op}\;z$** with **at most 3 addresses**. Each instruction performs **one** binary operation. * ✅ **Temporaries** ($t_1, t_2, \ldots$) are crucial in TAC to break complex expressions into simple instructions. * ✅ **Control flow in TAC** uses `if False condition goto label` and `goto label`. Always emit a `goto L_end` after a then-branch to skip the else-branch. * ✅ **Quadruples** = 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`; **easy to optimize, more memory**. * ✅ **Triples** = 3-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2)`; references other triples by index; **compact but expensive to reorder**. * ✅ **Indirect Triples** add a pointer list for cheap reordering. * ✅ **SSA Form** extends TAC by ensuring every variable is defined exactly once (using versioned names like `a_1`, `a_2`) and inserting **$\phi$-functions** at merge points. * ✅ **Translation algorithms**: * Arithmetic expressions $\rightarrow$ **post-order traversal** (for both IRs). * Boolean expressions $\rightarrow$ **short-circuit or jump code**. * Loops $\rightarrow$ **back-edge `goto`** to the loop header label. * ✅ **Stack-Machine Code is more compact** than TAC for distribution (e.g., bytecode), but **TAC is easier to optimize**. * ✅ **Common KTU Pitfalls**: forgetting `goto` after then-branch; miscounting stack depth; confusing quadruple result field with operands. ---

topics.title: Syntax-Driven Translation: Introduction, Translating Expressions, Translating Control-Flow Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Syntax-Driven Translation: Introduction, Translating Expressions, Translating Control-Flow Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** is a formal technique in compiler design where **semantic actions** (program fragments) are attached to each production of a context-free grammar, and these actions are executed (in a specified order) whenever the corresponding production is used in a derivation. It transforms an input source program into an intermediate form (commonly **three-address code (TAC)**) by traversing the parse tree and synthesizing/evaluating attributes at each node.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, SDT is framed through two equivalent mechanisms:

- **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** — a generalization of CFG in which each grammar symbol is associated with a set of *attributes* and each production is associated with a set of *semantic rules* that define how attribute values are computed.
- **Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme (SDT scheme)** — a context-free grammar in which semantic actions (code fragments) are embedded directly within the right-hand side of productions; these actions are executed in a left-to-right depth-first order.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Assembly Line" View

Imagine a **car manufacturing assembly line**:
- Each station (a grammar production) is a *robot arm* that knows how to fit one specific part.
- The chassis (the parse tree) moves from station to station.
- At each station, the robot reads the *labels* (attributes) on the incoming parts, builds a sub-component, and writes new labels (output attributes) on it.

The finished car rolling out at the end is your **intermediate code / three-address code**. The robot does not care about the *brand* (semantics) — it only mechanically follows the rulebook (the semantic rules) attached to its station.

In short: **Grammar drives the structure; semantics drives the meaning; SDT is the bridge.**

### 1.3 The Two Flavours of Attributes

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesized Attributes** — values computed *upward* from children's values. Used heavily for expressions, type checking, and most arithmetic translations. An SDD where all attributes are synthesized is called an **S-attributed definition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inherited Attributes** — values computed *downward* from siblings or parent. Used for passing type information, scope, or context (e.g., passing the type of an identifier to the LHS of an assignment). An SDD with only synthesized attributes and inherited attributes that can be evaluated in a *single left-to-right pass* is called an **L-attributed definition**.

### 1.4 Why KTU Emphasises This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to: (a) construct a translation scheme for arithmetic expressions, (b) convert a Boolean expression to three-address code, and (c) translate `if-then`, `if-then-else`, `while`, and `for` constructs into TAC. Mastering this module guarantees **14-mark question** scoring potential in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)

A formal SDD is the **4-tuple**:

$$
SDD \;=\; (G, \; A, \; R, \; S)
$$

Where each component is defined as:

- $G$ — The underlying context-free grammar $G = (V_N, V_T, P, S)$.
- $A$ — The set of **attributes** partitioned as $A = A_s \cup A_i$, where $A_s$ are *synthesized* and $A_i$ are *inherited*.
- $R$ — A finite set of **semantic rules**; one rule per production of $G$.
- $S$ — The **distinguished start symbol** of the grammar.

Each production $X \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \, \dots \, X_n$ is associated with a rule of the form:

$$
b := f(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k)
$$

where $b$ is an attribute of $X$ and each $c_j$ is an attribute of $X$ or $X_i$ for some $i$ in $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$.

### 2.2 The Two Evaluation Orders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question** — *"Differentiate between S-attributed and L-attributed definitions with examples."*

- **S-attributed SDD** — Every semantic rule computes a *synthesized* attribute. Evaluation is bottom-up; works naturally with **LR parsers**.
- **L-attributed SDD** — Each attribute is either synthesized OR inherited *only from the left* (parent or left siblings). Evaluation is possible in a *single left-to-right depth-first traversal*; works with **top-down (LL) parsers** as well as bottom-up.

### 2.3 The Standard Translation Tool — Three-Address Code (TAC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Address Code** is the canonical IR taught in KTU Module 3. A TAC instruction has the form $x := y \; op \; z$ with at most **three** operands (one operator and two source/target). Uses a temporary variable pool $t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots$

The three principal TAC forms relevant to SDT are:

- **Assignment** — $x := y \; op \; z$
- **Copy** — $x := y$
- **Indexed** — $x := y[i]$, $x[i] := y$
- **Pointer** — $x := \&y$, $x := *y$, $*x := y$
- **Branch** — `goto L`, `if x relop y goto L`
- **Procedure** — `call p, n`, `param x`, `return y`

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-portable** — memorize it for direct marking in 14-mark translation problems.

| Concept | Formal Symbol / Rule | KTU-Markable Use |
|---|---|---|
| SDD definition | $b := f(c_1, \dots, c_k)$ | Define semantics for each production |
| Synthesized attr. | $X.b := f(X_1.a_1, \dots, X_n.a_n)$ | Bottom-up; S-attributed SDD |
| Inherited attr. | $X_i.b := f(X_j.a, \text{parent}.a)$ | Top-down; L-attributed SDD |
| SDT Scheme | $\{ \text{action} \}$ inserted in RHS | Executed during parsing |
| TAC Arithmetic | $x := y \; op \; z$ | All binary ops |
| TAC Boolean | `if x relop y goto L` | Control-flow translation |
| TAC Branch | `goto L` | Unconditional jump |
| New temp gen. | $newtemp() \rightarrow t_i$ | Allocate fresh temp each call |
| Label gen. | $newlabel() \rightarrow L_i$ | Allocate fresh label each call |
| Backpatch list | $\text{merge}(p_1, p_2) = \text{list}(p_1) \cup \text{list}(p_2)$ | Pending jump resolution |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Reminder** — In markdown tables, the character `|` reserved for column separators. For mathematical *absolute value* or *divides* in any future table cell, always write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $x \mid y$. Never use a bare vertical bar inside a table row.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Syntax-Driven Translation is the **backbone of every commercial compiler**:

- **GCC / Clang** — Emit TAC and SSA form via SDD-style traversal.
- **Static Analyzers** (SonarQube, Coverity) — Use SDT to build control-flow graphs.
- **Domain-Specific Languages** (SQL parsers, Verilog synthesis tools) — Rely on SDT to lower high-level descriptions into intermediate RTL.
- **IDE Linters** — Apply SDT for type checking and refactor-safety.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Translation Scheme for Arithmetic Expressions

We now derive, **from first principles**, an SDT that converts an infix expression like `a + b * c` into the canonical TAC form.

#### 3.1.1 The Base Grammar

The starting grammar (handling only `+` and `*`, both left-associative) is:

$$
E \rightarrow E + T \mid T
$$

$$
T \rightarrow T * F \mid F
$$

$$
F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Defining the Attribute

Each non-terminal $E$, $T$, and $F$ carries exactly one synthesized attribute called `place` (i.e., the *name of the location* that will hold the value of that subexpression). The new temporary generator is denoted `newtemp()`.

#### 3.1.3 The Full SDT Scheme (Annotated Grammar)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{ E.place := \text{newtemp}(); \\
&\quad \quad \text{emit}(E.place \; := \; E_1.place \; + \; T.place) \} \\
E &\rightarrow T \quad \{ E.place := T.place \} \\
T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \quad \{ T.place := \text{newtemp}(); \\
&\quad \quad \text{emit}(T.place \; := \; T_1.place \; * \; F.place) \} \\
T &\rightarrow F \quad \{ T.place := F.place \} \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \quad \{ F.place := E.place \} \\
F &\rightarrow \text{id} \quad \{ F.place := \text{id}.name \}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Worked Example — `a + b * c`

Parsing and semantic action trace:

- The leaves `a`, `b`, `c` each get `place = a`, `place = b`, `place = c` respectively.
- For $T_1 \rightarrow b * F$ where $F \rightarrow c$: action emits `t1 := b * c`; $T_1.place = t_1$.
- For $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ collapses: $T.place = T_1.place = t_1$ (since $F$ here is the *first* multiplication; this is a re-walk trace — see final TAC below).
- For $E_1 \rightarrow T$ (covering `b * c`): $E_1.place = t_1$.
- For $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ (where $T \rightarrow a$ and $E_1$ holds the right side): $E.place = t_2$; action emits `t2 := a + t1`.

**Final Three-Address Code emitted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= b * c \\
t_2 &:= a + t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the standard left-associative, correct-precedence translation; the emitted TAC is **4-tuple safe** for any subsequent optimizer (constant folding, dead-code elimination, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> The above is an **S-attributed** SDD. It works perfectly with a bottom-up shift-reduce parser because every attribute is synthesized *upward* during the *reduce* action.

### 3.2 Type-Checked Expression Translation (KTU Extension)

To gain marks on inheritance, KTU often extends the grammar to a *typed* version. Add an inherited attribute `dtype` to `id`, and a synthesized attribute `type` to $E$ and $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{ \text{if } E_1.type = T.type \text{ then } E.type := E_1.type \\
&\quad \text{else } type\_error \} \\
F &\rightarrow \text{id} \quad \{ F.type := \text{id}.dtype \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 SDT for Control-Flow Statements (The Big Four)

For control-flow, we cannot rely solely on synthesized attributes — we need **labels** and **jumps**, so we extend the toolkit to include `newlabel()` and a `emit()` mechanism for branch instructions.

#### 3.3.1 General Grammar for Statements

$$
S \rightarrow \text{id} := E \mid \text{if } B \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } B \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{while } B \text{ do } S \mid S ; S
$$

Each statement $S$ carries a synthesized attribute `next` — the label to which control must flow *after* $S$ finishes. Each Boolean expression $B$ carries two synthesized attributes: `true` (the label to jump to if true) and `false` (the label to jump to if false).

#### 3.3.2 Translation of `S → if B then S1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \rightarrow \text{if } B \text{ then } S_1
\quad \{ & B.true := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad B.false := S.next; \\
& \quad S_1.next := S.next; \\
& \quad \text{emit}(B.true \; :)\; \text{// label} \\
& \quad \text{emit code for } B \\
& \quad \text{emit}(\text{if } B.true \text{ goto } B.false) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.3.3 Translation of `S → if B then S1 else S2`

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \text{if } B \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2 \\
\{ & B.true := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad B.false := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad S_1.next := S.next; \\
& \quad S_2.next := S.next; \\
& \quad \text{emit}(B.true \; :) \\
& \quad \text{emit code for } B \\
& \quad \text{emit}(\text{if } B.true \text{ goto } B.false) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: after $S_1$ we must emit `goto S.next`, and the two labels correspond to the *start* of the *then* block and the *start* of the *else* block.

#### 3.3.4 Translation of `S → while B do S1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \text{while } B \text{ do } S_1 \\
\{ & \text{begin} := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad B.true := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad B.false := S.next; \\
& \quad S_1.next := \text{begin}; \\
& \quad \text{emit}(\text{begin} \; :) \\
& \quad \text{emit code for } B \\
& \quad \text{emit}(\text{if } B.true \text{ goto } B.false) \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The crucial trick: after $S_1$ finishes, control jumps back to `begin` (the test) — that is, $S_1.next = \text{begin}$. This makes the loop properly iterative.

#### 3.3.5 Translation of `S → S1 ; S2` (Statement List)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow S_1 ; S_2 \\
\{ & S_1.next := \text{newlabel}(); \\
& \quad S_2.next := S.next; \\
& \quad \text{emit}(S_1.next \; :)\; \text{// label for start of } S_2 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Backpatching — The Cleaner Alternative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a recursive-descent parser encounters a `while`, it cannot immediately emit `goto L_test` because the label has not been seen yet. **Backpatching** solves this by maintaining a list of pending jump instructions whose target labels are unresolved.

A jump instruction like `if x > y goto _` is stored as a partial record `(_, addr)`; later, when the real label is known, we *patch* all such slots.

The three core backpatch functions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{makelist}(i) &= \{i\} \quad \text{(singleton list)} \\
\text{merge}(p_1, p_2) &= \text{append list}(p_1) \text{ with } p_2 \\
\text{backpatch}(p, \; L) &= \text{fill target of every instr in } p \text{ with } L
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.4.1 Backpatched Translation of `if B then S`

$$
\begin{aligned}
B.true &= \text{makelist}(\text{nextinstr}()); \\
B.false &= S.next; \\
S.next &= S.next; \\
\text{emit}(\text{if } B \text{ is true goto } _) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After fully parsing $B$, we know the instruction index of the conditional jump; we add it to a list. After parsing the `then` block, we `backpatch(B.true, L_then)`.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic** — Whenever the question says *"translate using backpatching"*, draw the three list-attributes (`true`, `false`, `next`) explicitly. Skipping the list declaration is the **#1 mark-loser** in KTU valuation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SDT Pipeline — High-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagrams the full **Syntax-Driven Translation pipeline** as it appears inside a real compiler front-end. Note the strict alphanumeric node IDs and absence of any markdown inside node labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Source Program] --> A2[Lexical Analyzer]
    A2 --> A3[Tokens Stream]
    A3 --> A4[Syntax Analyzer Parser]
    A4 --> A5[Parse Tree or DAG]
    A5 --> A6[Semantic Analyzer SDT Engine]
    A6 --> A7[Attribute Evaluation Synthesized Inherited]
    A7 --> A8[Action Invocation emit newtemp newlabel]
    A8 --> A9[Three Address Code]
    A9 --> A10[Optimizer Module 4]
```

### 4.2 Annotated Parse Tree for `a + b * c`

The annotated parse tree is the canonical *KTU diagram*. Each node carries the `place` attribute in brackets. The translator executes the actions in a *postorder* (bottom-up) sweep.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[E place t2] --> R2[E place t1]
    R1 --> R3[T place a]
    R2 --> R4[T place t1]
    R2 --> R5[F place b]
    R4 --> R6[T place b]
    R4 --> R7[F place c]
    R6 --> R8[F place b]
    R7 --> R9[id c]
    R8 --> R10[id b]
    R3 --> R11[id a]
```

**Resulting emitted instructions** (in order of emission during postorder walk):

```
t1 := b * c
t2 := a + t1
```

### 4.3 While-Loop Translation — Labelled Control-Flow Map

The diagram below renders the *TAC flow* for `while (a < b) do { a := a + 1 }`. The **three critical labels** are `L1` (test), `L2` (body), and `L3` (exit).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1a[L1] --> L1b[t2 := a lt b]
    L1b --> L1c[if t2 goto L2]
    L1c --> L1d[goto L3]
    L1d --> L2a[L2]
    L2a --> L2b[t1 := a + 1]
    L2b --> L2c[a := t1]
    L2c --> L2d[goto L1]
    L2d --> L3a[L3]
```

### 4.4 SDT vs SDD — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Grammar Production] --> B2{SDD Form}
    B2 --> B3[Attribute Set A]
    B2 --> B4[Semantic Rule Set R]
    B3 --> B5[Parse Tree Annotation]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[SDT Engine Executes]
    B1 --> B7{SDT Form}
    B7 --> B8[Action inlined in RHS]
    B8 --> B9[Parser triggers action on match]
    B9 --> B10[TAC emission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above highlights the **key board-difference**: an SDD is a *declarative* specification (what to compute), whereas an SDT is a *procedural* specification (when to execute). KTU expects students to be able to *convert* an SDD into an equivalent SDT scheme for any 14-marker.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD). Distinguish between synthesized and inherited attributes with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is a context-free grammar augmented with a set of attributes $A$ and a set of semantic rules $R$ that compute attribute values for every grammar symbol. It specifies *what* to compute, not *when*.

- **Synthesized attribute** — Computed from the attribute values of the *children* of a node. Example: $E.place := \text{newtemp}()$ in $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$, which depends on $E_1.place$ and $T.place$.
- **Inherited attribute** — Computed from the *parent* or *left sibling* of a node. Example: Passing the declared type of a variable down to its use-site in a type-checker SDD.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for SDD definition, 1 mark each for the two attribute types with an example.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme (SDT)? How does it differ from an SDD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **SDT scheme** is a context-free grammar in which *semantic actions* (code fragments) are inserted inside the right-hand side of productions. The actions are executed at parse time in a left-to-right depth-first order whenever the parser reduces by that production.

- **SDD** is *declarative* — it specifies the *what* (which attribute is computed from which), with no execution order.
