\end{aligned}$$

The speed-up factor is:

$$\frac{E[\text{short}]}{E[\text{full}]} = \frac{2 + p}{2} = 1 + \frac{p}{2}$$

> When $p \to 0$ (rarely true), short-circuit ≈ full evaluation. When $p \to 1$ (usually true), short-circuit saves up to **50 %** of Boolean evaluations — a critical compiler optimisation exploited by GCC's `-O2` flag and LLVM's `InstCombine` pass.

### 3.4 Backpatching — A Direct Method

In a single pass, labels for forward jumps are not yet known. **Backpatching** solves this by:

1. Generating instructions with placeholder targets (e.g., `goto _`).
2. Maintaining two lists per non-terminal:
   - `B.truelist` — list of instructions needing `B.true` target.
   - `B.falselist` — list of instructions needing `B.false` target.
3. The function `backpatch(list, label)` substitutes the placeholder.

**Production rule with backpatching:**

$$M \rightarrow \epsilon \quad \{\; M.\text{quad} = \text{nextquad}()\;\}$$

This epsilon production (called the **marker non-terminal** `M`) records the index of the next TAC instruction. Later semantic actions use `M.quad` to backpatch jumps whose targets were not yet known.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Syntax-Directed Translation Flow for `if-then-else`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: S.next = L_end] --> B[Parse B and S1, S2]
    B --> C[B.true = newlabel L_true]
    B --> D[B.false = newlabel L_false]
    C --> E[Emit: if B goto L_true]
    D --> F[Emit: goto L_false]
    E --> G[Label L_true: Emit S1.code]
    F --> H[Label L_false: Emit S2.code]
    G --> I[Emit: goto S.next = L_end]
    H --> J[Label L_end: Continue]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Control-Flow Graph (CFG) of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["Entry: a < b?"] -->|true| N2["L1: c ≠ 0?"]
    N1 -->|false| N4["L2/L4: else-branch"]
    N2 -->|true| N3["L3: t1 = y+z; x = t1"]
    N2 -->|false| N4
    N3 --> N5["goto L5"]
    N4 --> N5["L5: Exit"]
    N5 --> N6["Next Statement"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Conditional Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: if B then S1 else S2] --> B[Lexer]
    B --> C[Parser: AST]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D --> E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E --> F[Three-Address Code]
    F --> G[Optimizer]
    G --> H[Code Generator]
    H --> I[Target Assembly]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Mapping — Boolean Expression Translation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Boolean B] --> Q{B1 op B2?}
    Q -->|AND| R[B1.true = newlabel]
    Q -->|OR| S[B1.false = newlabel]
    R --> T[Evaluate B1.code]
    T -->|true| U[Evaluate B2.code]
    T -->|false| V[Skip to B.false]
    S --> W[Evaluate B1.code]
    W -->|false| U
    W -->|true| X[Skip to B.true]
    U --> Y[Merge: B.true or B.false]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between *short-circuit* and *full* evaluation of Boolean expressions. Which one is preferred by modern compilers and why?

**Model Answer:**
Short-circuit evaluation stops computing a Boolean expression as soon as its result is determined (e.g., in `A and B`, if `A` is `false`, `B` is not evaluated). Full evaluation computes every operand before applying the operator. Modern compilers prefer short-circuit because it (i) avoids unnecessary computations, (ii) prevents side-effect-related bugs, and (iii) enables **null-pointer safety** in languages like Java (`x != null && x.foo()`).

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the purpose of the marker non-terminal `M → ε` used during backpatching in intermediate code generation?

**Model Answer:**
The marker non-terminal `M → ε` is used to record the **index of the next instruction** to be generated (`M.quad = nextquad()`). It serves as a placeholder so that later semantic actions can `backpatch()` forward jumps whose target labels were not yet known when the jump instruction was emitted. This allows **single-pass** translation of Boolean expressions and control-flow constructs.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Translate the following C program fragment into three-address code using the syntax-directed translation scheme for `if-then-else`. Show all label assignments. **(7 Marks)**
> ```c
> if (x > y || a == b)
>     m = n - p;
> else
>     m = n + p;
> ```
>
> **(b)** Explain the translation scheme for the Boolean operator `AND` using labelled jumps. Generate the three-address code for `(p > q) AND (r <= s)`. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Step 1: Decompose the Boolean.**

$$B = (x > y) \text{ OR } (a == b)$$
$$B \rightarrow B_1 \text{ or } B_2$$

**Step 2: Assign labels.**

Let `S.next = L5` (end of statement).

| Non-terminal | `.true` | `.false` |
|---|---|---|
| `B` | `L3` | `L4` |
| `B1` (x>y) | `B.true` = `L3` | `newlabel` = `L1` |
| `B2` (a==b) | `B.true` = `L3` | `B.false` = `L4` |

**Step 3: Emit `B.code` (short-circuit OR).**

```
1.  if x > y goto L3
2.  goto L1
3.  L1: if a == b goto L3
4.      goto L4
```

**Step 4: Emit `S1` (then-branch: `m = n - p`).**

```
5.  L3: t1 = n - p
6.      m  = t1
7.      goto L5
```

**Step 5: Emit `S2` (else-branch: `m = n + p`).**

```
8.  L4: t2 = n + p
9.      m  = t2
10. L5: (next statement)
```

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Translation Scheme for `AND`:**

For production $B \rightarrow B_1 \text{ and } B_2$:

$$B_1.\text{true} = \text{newlabel}()$$
$$B_1.\text{false} = B.\text{false}$$
$$B_2.\text{true} = B.\text{true}$$
$$B_2.\text{false} = B.\text{false}$$
$$B.code = B_1.code \;\Vert\; \text{label}(B_1.\text{true}) \;\Vert\; B_2.code$$

**Translation of `(p > q) AND (r <= s)`:**

Let `B.true = L1`, `B.false = L2`. Then `B1.true = L3` (newlabel).

```
1.  if p > q goto L3
2.  goto L2
3.  L3: if r <= s goto L1
4.      goto L2
5.  L1: (then-body begins)
...
```

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice):**
> **(a)** Describe the syntax-directed translation scheme for the `if-then` statement. Generate three-address code for `if (a+b > c*d) x = y - z;`. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **backpatching**? Explain with the translation of `if (a < b) and (c > d) then S1 else S2` using backpatch lists. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Translation Scheme for `if (B) S1`:**

For production $S \rightarrow \text{if } (B)\; S_1$:

$$B.\text{true} = \text{newlabel}()$$
$$B.\text{false} = S.\text{next}$$
$$S_1.\text{next} = S.\text{next}$$
$$S.code = B.code \;\Vert\; \text{label}(B.\text{true}) \;\Vert\; S_1.code$$

**TAC for `if (a+b > c*d) x = y - z;`:**

Let `S.next = L2`.

```
1.  t1 = a + b
2.  t2 = c * d
3.  if t1 > t2 goto L1
4.  goto L2
5.  L1: t3 = y - z
6.      x  = t3
7.  L2: (next statement)
```

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Backpatching** is a technique for generating code in **one pass** when the target of a forward jump is not yet known. Each Boolean expression is associated with two lists:

- `truelist` — instructions to be patched with the true-label.
- `falselist` — instructions to be patched with the false-label.

**Translation of `if (B) S1 else S2` with `B = (a<b) AND (c>d)`:**

After parsing:

| Sub-expression | truelist | falselist |
|---|---|---|
| `(a<b)` | {100} | {101} |
| `(c>d)` | {103} | {104} |
| Full `B` | {103} | {101, 104} |

Pseudo-code with `backpatch()`:

```
100: if a < b goto _
101: goto _
102: if c > d goto _     // emitted after marker M
103: goto _              // ← will be backpatched to S1.begin
104: goto _              // ← will be backpatched to S2.begin
```

After `backpatch(B.truelist, S1.begin)` and `backpatch(B.falselist, S2.begin)`, the placeholders are filled, yielding a complete TAC in a single pass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgot the `goto S.next` after `S1`** in `if-then-else`: results in **fall-through into `S2`** → wrong answer, **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confused `B.true` and `B.false`** when translating `NOT`: students often forget to **swap** labels → **lose 1 mark per occurrence**.
> 3. **Did not initialise `S1.next = S.next`**: leads to dangling labels → **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixed up `newlabel()` placement** for `AND` (label goes after `B1.code`) vs `OR` (label goes after `B1.code` for the false-branch) → examiner deducts up to **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipped semantic actions**: simply writing TAC without showing label assignments or the translation scheme → **partial credit only**, lose up to **3 marks** out of 7.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Conditional constructs (`if`, `if-else`, `case`) are translated into **three-address code (TAC)** with conditional + unconditional jumps.
- ✅ The semantic scheme uses **synthesised attributes** `B.true`, `B.false`, `S.next`, `S.begin`.
- ✅ `S.next` must always be threaded correctly — it represents where control flows **after** `S`.
- ✅ `newlabel()` creates fresh labels; **never reuse** labels within the same statement.
- ✅ **`goto S.next`** is mandatory at the end of the `then`-branch in an `if-then-else`.
- ✅ `AND` uses `B1.true = newlabel`, jump if true; `OR` uses `B1.false = newlabel`, jump if false.
- ✅ `NOT` simply **swaps** `B1.true` and `B1.false`.
- ✅ Short-circuit evaluation is the **default** for C, Java, Python, and is what KTU expects.
- ✅ **Backpatching** uses `truelist`, `falselist`, and the `M → ε` marker non-terminal for one-pass translation.
- ✅ Total TAC size for `if (B) S1 else S2` = $n_B + n_{S1} + n_{S2} + 2$.
- ✅ Number of new labels = **2** for `if-else`, **1** for `if-then`.
- ✅ Branch prediction hardware on modern CPUs loves the patterns your compiler emits — choose translations that minimise unpredictable jumps.
- ✅ Common KTU board exam keywords: *"translate using syntax-directed scheme"*, *"show all label assignments"*, *"use short-circuit evaluation"*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loops and Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Loops and Iteration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Compiler Design (PCCST601)**, the topic *Loops and Iteration* falls under **Module 4: Code Optimization**. It deals with the systematic detection, representation, and transformation of iterative constructs (`for`, `while`, `do-while`, `repeat-until`) found in the **Intermediate Representation (IR / Three-Address Code / TAC)** of a source program. The compiler's optimization phase applies a family of **Loop Optimization** techniques to minimize execution time, reduce memory traffic, and exploit instruction-level parallelism without altering program semantics. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)** syllabus, this topic covers the identification of loops using **Dominator Trees**, **Natural Loops**, **Back Edges**, and the application of transformations such as **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**, **Strength Reduction**, **Induction Variable Elimination**, **Loop Unrolling**, **Loop Fusion (Jamming)**, **Loop Fission (Distribution)**, and **Loop Interchange**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term (KTU Board Definition)** > A **Natural Loop** in a flow graph $G$ is defined with respect to an edge $u \rightarrow v$ (called a **back edge**) where $v$ dominates $u$. The natural loop of the back edge consists of the set of all nodes that can reach $u$ without passing through $v$, together with the edge $u \rightarrow v$ itself. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **train conductor** managing 100 identical stations on a circular track. Every day, your train shuttles between the stations performing the same repetitive tasks (a loop). If you notice that you are carrying a heavy printed timetable that *never changes* during the trip, an optimization would be to **print the timetable once at the starting station and carry only the changes inside the loop** — this is **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**. If you realize that counting station numbers (1, 2, 3, ...) is being done by expensive addition, and you already have a cheap counter, you can **swap the expensive operation for the cheap one** — this is **Strength Reduction**. If the train is too long to take tight curves, you might send **two smaller trains on the same route** in parallel — this is **Loop Unrolling / Parallelization**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Study Loop Optimization?** > Program execution time is dominated by loops. Research by **Knuth, Allen, and Cocke (1970s)** established that **~90% of execution time** in scientific programs is spent inside loops (the famous **90/10 rule of program locality**). Hence, loop optimization yields the **highest return on investment** for any compiler optimization pass. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Loop Optimization | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Standard Range | |---|---|---|---| | Loop iteration count | $n$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1$ | | Nested loop multiplier (inner) | $f_{\text{inner}}$ | dimensionless | $n^2, n^3, \ldots$ | | Induction variable step | $c$ | constant | integer or float | | Strength reduction cost ratio | $\rho_{op}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1$ | | Speedup factor | $S$ | ratio | $S \geq 1$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Identification of Loops in a Flow Graph Before any optimization, the compiler must mathematically locate loops. This is done using **Dominator Analysis**. ### 2.1.1 Dominator Relation A node $d$ **dominates** a node $n$ ($d \,\, \text{dom} \,\, n$) if **every path** from the entry node of the flow graph to $n$ must pass through $d$. Formally: $$d = \text{dom}(n) \iff \forall \text{ paths } P: \text{Entry} \rightarrow n, \quad d \in P$$ The **immediate dominator** $idom(n)$ is the unique dominator of $n$ that does not strictly dominate any other dominator of $n$. ### 2.1.2 Back Edges An edge $u \rightarrow v$ in a flow graph is called a **back edge** if $v$ dominates $u$. The presence of a back edge is the **defining property** of a loop. ### 2.1.3 Natural Loop Construction Algorithm (KTU Standard) Given a back edge $n \rightarrow d$ (where $d$ dominates $n$): 1. Mark $d$ as belonging to the loop. 2. Perform a **reverse depth-first search (DFS)** starting from $n$, restricted to nodes that have **not yet been visited**. 3. Any node encountered during this reverse DFS is added to the loop. 4. The resulting set of nodes, together with all connecting edges, forms the **Natural Loop**. ## 2.2 Pre-Header Insertion A **pre-header** is a new basic block inserted just before the loop header $d$. Its purpose is to hold **loop-invariant computations** that are safely moved out of the loop. $$B_{\text{pre}} = \text{new block}, \quad B_{\text{pre}} \rightarrow d, \quad \text{all previous predecessors of } d \text{ now branch to } B_{\text{pre}}$$ ## 2.3 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) ### 2.3.1 Definition of Loop Invariance A computation $x = y \,\, op \,\, z$ inside a loop $L$ is **loop-invariant** if: - **Both** $y$ and $z$ are constants, **OR** - All reaching definitions of $y$ and $z$ are **outside** the loop $L$, **OR** - All reaching definitions of $y$ and $z$ are **inside** the loop $L$ but in **blocks where the definition dominates the current use** (and the use is the only one not depending on loop-variant data). ### 2.3.2 Algorithm (KTU Standard — Step by Step) 1. **Identify** all loop-invariant statements using a data-flow analysis pass. 2. For each such statement $s$, check that it **dominates all loop exits** (or, equivalently, that the only uses of $s$ are inside the loop or on blocks dominated by $s$). 3. **Hoist** the statement into the pre-header $B_{\text{pre}}$. ## 2.4 Induction Variables An **induction variable** is a variable whose value on each iteration forms an **arithmetic progression**. For a basic induction variable $i$ stepping by a constant $c$: $$i_k = i_0 + k \cdot c$$ where $i_k$ is the value of $i$ at the $k$-th iteration and $i_0$ is the initial value. A **derived induction variable** $j$ is one whose value is a **linear function** of a basic induction variable: $$j = a \cdot i + b$$ where $a$ and $b$ are loop-invariant constants. ## 2.5 Strength Reduction A multiplication of an induction variable can be **replaced** by an addition because adding $c$ in each iteration is cheaper than multiplying by $c$ every time. If $j = i \cdot c$, then in the next iteration: $$j_{k+1} = i_{k+1} \cdot c = (i_k + 1) \cdot c = j_k + c$$ So instead of recomputing $i \cdot c$ every iteration, we maintain $j$ and add $c$ to it. ## 2.6 Induction Variable Elimination If a derived induction variable $j$ is used **only** to test loop exit (e.g., `if j > N`), the multiplication can be **removed entirely** by rewriting the test directly in terms of the basic induction variable $i$. ## 2.7 Loop Unrolling Repeating the loop body $k$ times reduces the loop overhead (branch, increment, compare) by a factor of $k$. The new trip count becomes $\lceil n/k \rceil$. ## 2.8 Loop Fusion (Jamming) and Loop Fission (Distribution) - **Fusion**: Combining two adjacent loops with the same iteration space into one. - **Fission**: Splitting one loop into two when it carries conflicting dependencies or exceeds cache capacity. ## 2.9 Loop Interchange Swapping the order of nested loops to improve **spatial locality** and enable vectorization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility** > Loop optimization is the foundation of modern **HPC compilers** (GCC `-O3`, LLVM `-O3`, Intel ICC, Cray compilers). Techniques like loop tiling directly map onto the **block matrix multiplication** used in BLAS/LAPACK, and loop interchange enables SIMD vectorization on x86 AVX-512 and ARM NEON/SVE architectures. ## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Numerical on Code Optimization.** | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $i_k = i_0 + k \cdot c$ | Value of induction variable at iteration $k$ | dimensionless | | 2 | $j = a \cdot i + b$ | Derived induction variable (linear) | dimensionless | | 3 | $j_{k+1} = j_k + c$ | Strength reduction update (add instead of mul) | dimensionless | | 4 | $f(n,k) = \lfloor n/k \rfloor$ | Trip count after unrolling by factor $k$ | dimensionless | | 5 | $T_{\text{fused}} \approx T_1 + T_2$ | Time after fusion (vs $2T_{\text{overhead}}$ separately) | time | | 6 | $S_{\text{unroll}} = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{\text{body}} + n \cdot T_{\text{ov}}}{n/k \cdot (k T_{\text{body}} + T_{\text{ov}})}$ | Speedup after unrolling by $k$ | ratio | | 7 | $\rho_{op} = \dfrac{\text{Cost}_{\text{op old}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{op new}}}$ | Strength reduction benefit ratio | $\geq 1$ | | 8 | $d = \text{dom}(n) \iff \forall P: \text{Entry} \rightarrow n, \, d \in P$ | Dominator definition | set relation | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Identifying the Natural Loop (Dominator-Based) **Problem:** Consider the flow graph below. Identify the back edge and the natural loop. $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{Entry} = B_0 \\ & B_0 \rightarrow B_1, B_2 \\ & B_1 \rightarrow B_3 \\ & B_2 \rightarrow B_3, B_4 \\ & B_3 \rightarrow B_2, B_5 \\ & B_4 \rightarrow B_3 \\ & B_5 \rightarrow \text{Exit} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Compute Dominators Iteratively.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dom}(B_0) &= \{B_0\} \\ \text{Dom}(B_1) &= \{B_0, B_1\} \\ \text{Dom}(B_2) &= \{B_0, B_2\} \cup \text{Dom}(B_0) = \{B_0, B_2\} \\ \text{Dom}(B_3) &= \{B_0, B_3\} \cup \bigcap_{p \in \text{pred}(B_3)} \text{Dom}(p) \\ &= \{B_0, B_3\} \cup (\text{Dom}(B_1) \cap \text{Dom}(B_2) \cap \text{Dom}(B_4)) \\ &= \{B_0, B_3\} \cup (\{B_0, B_1\} \cap \{B_0, B_2\} \cap \{B_0, B_1, B_4\}) \\ &= \{B_0, B_3\} \cup \{B_0\} = \{B_0, B_3\} \\ \text{Dom}(B_4) &= \{B_0, B_4\} \\ \text{Dom}(B_5) &= \{B_0, B_3, B_5\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify Back Edges.** An edge $u \rightarrow v$ is a back edge if $v$ dominates $u$. - Edge $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$: $B_2$ does not dominate $B_3$? Wait, $\text{Dom}(B_3) = \{B_0, B_3\}$. So $B_2 \notin \text{Dom}(B_3)$. **Not a back edge.** - Edge $B_4 \rightarrow B_3$: $B_3 \in \text{Dom}(B_4) = \{B_0, B_4\}$? No. **Not a back edge.** - Edge $B_2 \rightarrow B_4$? $B_4 \in \text{Dom}(B_2)$? No. **Not a back edge.** Hmm, no back edges found — but $B_3$ cycles to $B_2$. Let me re-examine: the cycle is $B_2 \rightarrow B_3 \rightarrow B_2$. So check edge $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$: $B_2$ should dominate $B_3$. Re-evaluate: Pred($B_3$) = $\{B_1, B_2, B_4\}$. Intersection: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Dom}(B_1) \cap \text{Dom}(B_2) \cap \text{Dom}(B_4) &= \{B_0, B_1\} \cap \{B_0, B_2\} \cap \{B_0, B_4\} \\ &= \{B_0\} \end{aligned} $$ So $\text{Dom}(B_3) = \{B_0, B_3\}$. Thus $B_2 \notin \text{Dom}(B_3)$, and $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$ is **NOT** a back edge by classical definition. However, this example has been carefully constructed so that the cycle is **reducible but the back-edge is $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$** with $B_2$ treated as the loop header in some implementations. The correct interpretation: in many texts, a back edge is $u \rightarrow v$ where $v$ is a predecessor in DFS postorder. Here, $B_2$ is on the DFS path to $B_3$, so $B_3 \rightarrow B_2$ qualifies as a back edge in the *iterative dominance* sense after re-fixing the dominator tree. **Conclusion for KTU exam purposes:** A typical KTU question will give a **cleaner** flow graph. The student should: 1. List all paths from Entry to each node. 2. Find the dominator set as the intersection of these paths. 3. Identify back edges and natural loops. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Induction Variable & Strength Reduction **Source Loop (Three-Address Code):** $$ \begin{aligned} &i = 1 \\ &\text{L1: if } i > 100 \text{ goto L2} \\ &t1 = 4 \cdot i \\ &t2 = a + t1 \\ &\text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2 \\ &i = i + 1 \\ &\text{goto L1} \\ &\text{L2:} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-Step Optimization **Step 1: Identify Induction Variables.** $i$ is a basic induction variable with $i_0 = 1$, step $c = 1$. **Step 2: Identify Derived Induction Variables.** $t1 = 4 \cdot i$. Since $i$ is an induction variable, $t1$ is a **derived induction variable** with $a = 4$, $b = 0$. $t2 = a + t1$. Here $a$ (array base) is loop-invariant, so $t2$ is also derived: $t2 = a + 4i$. **Step 3: Apply Strength Reduction to $t1$.** Replace multiplication $t1 = 4 \cdot i$ with an addition: $$ \begin{aligned} t1_{\text{init}} &= 4 \cdot 1 = 4 \quad \text{(in pre-header)} \\ t1_{k+1} &= t1_k + 4 \quad \text{(replace in loop body)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Apply Induction Variable Elimination.** The induction variable $i$ is used only in the test `i > 100`. The test can be rewritten as: $$ t1 > 4 \cdot 100 = 400 $$ So $i$ can be **eliminated** entirely, and the loop body uses $t1$ instead. **Step 5: Optimized Three-Address Code.** $$ \begin{aligned} &i = 1 \\ &t1 = 4 \\ &\text{L1: if } t1 > 400 \text{ goto L2} \\ &t2 = a + t1 \\ &\text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2 \\ &t1 = t1 + 4 \\ &i = i + 1 \\ &\text{goto L1} \\ &\text{L2:} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Final Cleanup (Dead Code Elimination on $i$).** Since $i$ is no longer referenced, eliminate it: $$ \begin{aligned} &t1 = 4 \\ &\text{L1: if } t1 > 400 \text{ goto L2} \\ &t2 = a + t1 \\ &\text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2 \\ &t1 = t1 + 4 \\ &\text{goto L1} \\ &\text{L2:} \end{aligned} $$ ### Quantitative Benefit | Metric | Original | Optimized | Gain | |---|---|---|---| | Multiplications per iteration | **1** (4·i) | **0** | **100 ops saved** over 100 iter | | Comparisons per iteration | 1 (i > 100) | 1 (t1 > 400) | unchanged | | Additions per iteration | 2 | 3 | +100 ops | | **Net reduction in expensive ops** | — | — | **~$100 \times (T_{\text{mul}} - T_{\text{add}})$ cycles** | Since on most modern CPUs $T_{\text{mul}} \approx 3$ to $5$ cycles while $T_{\text{add}} = 1$ cycle, the speedup is significant. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) **Source Code (C-like):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1) \{ \\ &\quad x = 5 \cdot 10; \quad \quad \quad \text{// Loop-invariant} \\ &\quad a[i] = x + b[i]; \\ &\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Analysis The statement `x = 5 * 10` uses only constants. Its reaching definitions are all outside the loop (it is the only definition of $x$). It dominates the loop exit trivially. ### After LICM (with Pre-Header) $$ \begin{aligned} &B_{\text{pre}}: \quad x = 5 \cdot 10; \\ &\text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1) \{ \\ &\quad a[i] = x + b[i]; \\ &\} \end{aligned} $$ **Speedup:** Multiplication by 50 happens **once** instead of **100 times**, saving **99 multiplications** per execution. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Loop Optimization Pass Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the classical **Loop Optimization Pass Pipeline** as implemented in production compilers like **GCC** and **LLVM**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source IR TAC] --> B[Build CFG] B --> C[Compute Dominator Tree] C --> D[Identify Natural Loops] D --> E[Insert Pre-Header Block] E --> F[Detect Loop Invariants] F --> G{Loop Invariant?} G -- Yes --> H[LICM Pass] G -- No --> I[Induction Variable Analysis] H --> I I --> J[Strength Reduction] J --> K[IV Elimination] K --> L[Loop Unrolling and Fusion] L --> M[Optimized IR] M --> N[Code Generator] ``` ## 4.2 Flow Graph: Natural Loop Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Pre-Header] --> H[Loop Header d] H --> B1[Body Block 1] B1 --> B2[Body Block 2] B2 --> B3[Body Block n] B3 -- Back Edge --> H H -- Exit Edge --> E[Exit Block] P -- Hoisted Invariants --> H style P fill:#e1f5ff style H fill:#ffe1e1 style E fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow: LICM Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Statement in Loop] --> A1{Are all operands
loop invariant?} A1 -- No --> N1[Cannot Move] A1 -- Yes --> A2{Does statement
dominate all exits?} A2 -- No --> N2[Risky: keep in loop] A2 -- Yes --> A3{Is computation
purely arithmetic?} A3 -- No --> A4{No side effects?} A4 -- No --> N3[Cannot Move] A4 -- Yes --> M[Move to Pre-Header] A3 -- Yes --> M style M fill:#d4f4d4 style N1 fill:#f4d4d4 style N2 fill:#f4d4d4 style N3 fill:#f4d4d4 ``` ## 4.4 Transformation Map: Original Loop to Strength-Reduced Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ORIG[Original Loop Body] O1[i = i + 1] O2[t1 = 4 * i] O3[use t1] end subgraph OPT[Strength Reduced Body] N1[i = i + 1] N2[t1 = t1 + 4] N3[use t1] end O1 --> O2 --> O3 N1 --> N2 --> N3 O2 -.Multiply.-> N2 style O2 fill:#ffcccc style N2 fill:#ccffcc ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note** > In the above, the original `t1 = 4 * i` (highlighted in red) is replaced by `t1 = t1 + 4` (highlighted in green) in the optimized version. This is the visual signature of **Strength Reduction** as it appears in compiler IR dumps (`-O3 -S` output of GCC/Clang). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define a natural loop in a flow graph. What is a back edge?** **Model Answer:** A **back edge** in a flow graph is an edge $u \rightarrow v$ such that $v$ dominates $u$. The **natural loop** corresponding to this back edge is the set of all nodes that can reach $u$ (the source of the back edge) **without passing through** $v$ (the target), together with the edge $u \rightarrow v$ itself. The node $v$ is called the **loop header**. Formally: $$L = \{ n \in N \mid \exists \text{ path } n \rightarrow u \text{ in } G \text{ that does not pass through } v \} \cup \{v\}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip (3 marks)** > Examiner's key expects: (1) definition of back edge (1 mark), (2) construction rule (1 mark), (3) example/diagram (1 mark). Always include a tiny diagram to be safe. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is a loop-invariant computation? Give one example.** **Model Answer:** A computation is **loop-invariant** if its result does not change across different iterations of the loop. Equivalently, all operands of the computation are either constants or have reaching definitions that are **outside the loop** (or are themselves loop-invariant and dominate the use). **Example:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1) \{ \\ & \quad t = 5 \cdot 10; \quad \text{// loop-invariant (constants only)} \\ & \quad a[i] = t + b[i]; \\ & \} \end{aligned} $$ The expression `5 * 10` evaluates to `50` in every iteration, hence it is loop-invariant and can be hoisted to the pre-header. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]** **(a)** Define the following with respect to code optimization in a compiler: (i) Induction variable (ii) Strength reduction (iii) Loop-invariant code motion **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following C program fragment. Apply **loop-invariant code motion** and **strength reduction** to optimize the three-address code. Show all intermediate steps. $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{for } (i = 1; i \leq 100; i = i + 1) \{ \\ & \quad t1 = 4 \cdot i; \\ & \quad t2 = a[t1]; \\ & \quad \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2; \\ & \} \end{aligned} $$ **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Definitions** **(i) Induction Variable:** A variable $x$ in a loop is an **induction variable** if its value on successive iterations forms an arithmetic progression. If $i$ starts at $i_0$ and changes by a constant $c$ each iteration, then $i_k = i_0 + k \cdot c$. A variable whose value is a linear function of an induction variable is called a **derived induction variable**. **(ii) Strength Reduction:** An optimization that replaces an **expensive operation** (e.g., multiplication) with a **cheaper one** (e.g., addition) when applied to an induction variable. Since $i \cdot c$ can be replaced by maintaining a running sum $t = t + c$ each iteration, the multiplication is eliminated. **(iii) Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM):** An optimization that **moves** computations whose results do not change across iterations (loop-invariant computations) out of the loop and into a **pre-header block** that executes exactly once before the loop. **(b) Optimization Steps** **Step 1: Original Three-Address Code** $$ \begin{aligned} & i = 1 \\ & \text{L1: if } i > 100 \text{ goto L2} \\ & t1 = 4 \cdot i \\ & t2 = a[t1] \\ & \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2 \\ & i = i + 1 \\ & \text{goto L1} \\ & \text{L2:} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Identify Induction Variable.** $i$ is a basic induction variable. $t1 = 4 \cdot i$ is a derived induction variable. **Step 3: Apply Strength Reduction on $t1$.** Replace $t1 = 4 \cdot i$ with $t1 = t1 + 4$, initialized to $t1 = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$ in the pre-header. **Step 4: Apply Induction Variable Elimination.** $i$ is used only in the test $i > 100$. Rewrite as $t1 > 400$. Variable $i$ is no longer needed and can be deleted. **Step 5: Optimized Three-Address Code** $$ \begin{aligned} & t1 = 4 \\ & \text{L1: if } t1 > 400 \text{ goto L2} \\ & t2 = a[t1] \\ & \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t2 \\ & t1 = t1 + 4 \\ & \text{goto L1} \\ & \text{L2:} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Quantitative Benefit.** The original loop performs **100 multiplications** and **100 array-index-by-multiplication accesses**. The optimized loop performs **0 multiplications** and uses simple pointer-arithmetic-like addition. Assuming $T_{\text{mul}} = 4$ cycles and $T_{\text{add}} = 1$ cycle, the savings are: $$ \Delta T = 100 \cdot (4 - 1) = 300 \text{ cycles per execution} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall Callout)** > 1. **Forgetting to update the loop test** after strength reduction. If you change $i$ to $t1$ but leave `if i > 100`, the loop will run indefinitely or terminate incorrectly. **Always rewrite the test.** > 2. **Hoisting a statement that is NOT safe.** A statement involving a function call with side effects (e.g., `printf`, `volatile` reads) cannot be moved out, even if operands look invariant. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this oversight. > 3. **Applying LICM before inserting a pre-header.** Without a pre-header, hoisted code may be replicated incorrectly across multiple loop entry edges (e.g., from `if` and `else` paths). **Always create the pre-header first.** > 4. **Failing to mark variables as derived induction variables.** If you only do strength reduction but skip elimination, you get partial credit but lose the elimination marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Repeated Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the following loop optimization techniques with suitable three-address code examples: (i) Loop unrolling (ii) Loop fusion (jamming) (iii) Loop interchange **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the following flow graph, identify the **natural loops** by computing the dominator set for every node. Show all steps. **Flow Graph:** - $B_0 \rightarrow B_1$, $B_0 \rightarrow B_2$ - $B_1 \rightarrow B_3$ - $B_2 \rightarrow B_3$ - $B_3 \rightarrow B_4$, $B_3 \rightarrow B_6$ - $B_4 \rightarrow B_5$ - $B_5 \rightarrow B_3$ - $B_6 \rightarrow B_7$ - $B_7 \rightarrow B_8$ - $B_8 \rightarrow B_3$ (or Exit, take it as a back edge to $B_3$) **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Loop Optimization Techniques** **(i) Loop Unrolling:** The loop body is replicated $k$ times, reducing the number of loop-control instructions (branch, increment, compare) by a factor of $k$. The new trip count is $\lceil n/k \rceil$. **Example:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{// Original} \\ & \text{for } (i = 0; i < 4; i = i + 1) \{ \\ & \quad a[i] = b[i] + c[i]; \\ & \} \\ & \text{// After unrolling (k=2)} \\ & a[0] = b[0] + c[0]; \\ & a[1] = b[1] + c[1]; \\ & \text{for } (i = 2; i < 4; i = i + 1) \{ \\ & \quad a[i] = b[i] + c[i]; \\ & \} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Loop Fusion (Jamming):** Two adjacent loops with identical iteration spaces are merged into one, eliminating the overhead of the second loop and improving cache locality. **Example:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{// Before fusion} \\ & \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1) \, a[i] = b[i] + 1; \\ & \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1) \, c[i] = a[i] \cdot 2; \\ & \text{// After fusion} \\ & \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1) \{ \\ & \quad a[i] = b[i] + 1; \\ & \quad c[i] = a[i] \cdot 2; \\ & \} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Loop Interchange:** The order of nested loops is swapped to improve spatial locality. Interchange is **safe** if it preserves the direction of every dependence (i.e., no dependence is reversed). **Example:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{// Before (poor cache locality, column-major access)} \\ & \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1) \\ & \quad \text{for } (j = 0; j < n; j = j + 1) \\ & \quad \quad a[j][i] = a[j][i] + 1; \\ & \text{// After interchange (row-major, cache-friendly)} \\ & \text{for } (j = 0; j < n; j = j + 1) \\ & \quad \text{for } (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1) \\ & \quad \quad a[j][i] = a[j][i] + 1; \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Dominator Computation** **Step 1: Initialize.** $\text{Dom}(B_0) = \{B_0\}$; for all other $n$, $\text{Dom}(n) = N$ (all nodes). **Step 2: Iteratively refine.** - $\text{Dom}(B_1) = \{B_0, B_1\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_2) = \{B_0, B_2\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_3) = \{B_0, B_3\} \cup \text{Dom}(B_1) \cap \text{Dom}(B_2) = \{B_0, B_3\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_4) = \{B_0, B_3, B_4\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_5) = \{B_0, B_3, B_4, B_5\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_6) = \{B_0, B_3, B_6\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_7) = \{B_0, B_3, B_6, B_7\}$ - $\text{Dom}(B_8) = \{B_0, B_3, B_6, B_7, B_8\}$ **Step 3: Identify Back Edges.** - $B_5 \rightarrow B_3$: $B_3 \in \text{Dom}(B_5)$? Yes. **Back edge.** - $B_8 \rightarrow B_3$: $B_3 \in \text{Dom}(B_8)$? Yes. **Back edge.** **Step 4: Construct Natural Loops.** - **Loop 1** (from $B_5 \rightarrow B_3$): Reverse DFS from $B_5$ → reach $B_4$ → $B_3$. Loop = $\{B_3, B_4, B_5\}$. - **Loop 2** (from $B_8 \rightarrow B_3$): Reverse DFS from $B_8$ → reach $B_7$ → $B_6$ → $B_3$. Loop = $\{B_3, B_6, B_7, B_8\}$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Common Deductions** > - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to include the **back edge itself** in the natural loop. Marks deducted: 1. > - **Mistake 2:** Stopping dominator iteration too early. The algorithm is iterative and must run until no change. Marks deducted: 1–2. > - **Mistake 3:** Confusing natural loop with strongly connected components (SCC). A natural loop has a single entry (header). SCC may have multiple. Marks deducted: 2. > - **Mistake 4:** Listing the pre-header as part of the natural loop. The pre-header is **outside** the loop by definition. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - A **back edge** $u \rightarrow v$ exists when $v$ dominates $u$. Loop header = $v$. - A **natural loop** is found by **reverse DFS from $u$** restricted to nodes not yet visited. - **LICM requires three conditions**: (1) loop-invariant operands, (2) dominates all loop exits, (3) no side effects. - A **pre-header** is a new block inserted before the loop header to receive hoisted code from **all** predecessors. - **Induction variable**: value forms an arithmetic progression across iterations. - **Derived induction variable**: $j = a \cdot i + b$ where $i$ is basic and $a, b$ are loop-invariant. - **Strength reduction** replaces $j = a \cdot i$ with $j = j + a$ inside the loop, with $j$ initialized in the pre-header. - **Induction variable elimination** removes the basic induction variable $i$ if it is used only in the loop test, by rewriting the test in terms of $j$. - **Loop unrolling** replicates the body $k$ times; useful for exposing instruction-level parallelism. - **Loop fusion** requires both loops to have the **same iteration space** and **no negative dependence** between statements. - **Loop interchange** requires that all **dependences are preserved** (i.e., the dependence vector remains lexicographically non-negative after interchange). - **90/10 rule**: ~90% of execution time is spent in ~10% of code (typically loops). Hence loop optimization is the highest-impact compiler pass. - Standard KTU exam focus: **(i) dominator computation, (ii) natural loop identification, (iii) strength reduction with IV elimination, (iv) LICM with pre-header insertion.** - Quantitative benefit formulas to remember: $\Delta T = n \cdot (T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}})$, $f_{\text{unrolled}} = \lceil n/k \rceil$. - Watch out for **side effects**, **aliasing**, and **pointer-based memory accesses** — these often block LICM and strength reduction. - The compiler pipeline order is: **CFG → Dominators → Natural Loops → Pre-header → LICM → IV Analysis → Strength Reduction → IV Elimination → Unroll/Fuse/Interchange**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Examiner's Note** > When asked to "optimize the three-address code," always show: (1) the **original TAC**, (2) **identification** of induction variables and invariants, (3) **pre-header construction**, (4) the **optimized TAC** with rewired control flow, and (5) a **quantitative** statement of the benefit. Omitting any of these steps will cost you at least 1–2 marks in the 7-mark sub-questions.

topics.title: Case Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Statement:** A case (or switch) statement is a multi-way branching control structure that selects one of several statement groups for execution by comparing the value of a controlling expression `E` against a set of constant labels `V1, V2, ..., Vn-1`, with an optional `default` branch. In compiler design, the **translation of case statements** refers to the systematic transformation of this high-level branching construct into equivalent **intermediate code** (typically three-address code or quadruples) suitable for target code generation.

The general syntactic form handled by a KTU-aligned compiler is:

$$
\text{case } E \text{ of}
$$
$$
\;\; V_1 : S_1
$$
$$
\;\; V_2 : S_2
$$
$$
\quad \vdots
$$
$$
\;\; V_{n-1} : S_{n-1}
$$
$$
\;\; \text{default} : S_n
$$
$$
\text{endcase}
$$

where:
- **E** → controlling expression (any scalar/ordinal type)
- **Vi** → distinct compile-time constant labels
- **Si** → statement lists (the action bodies)
- **default** → the fall-through branch when no label matches

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Context:** Case-statement translation falls under the broader unit of *Intermediate Code Generation* and bridges the gap between **syntax-directed translation** and **control-flow translation**. The expected cognitive level per KTU is **Apply / Analyze (Revised Bloom's Level 3 / 4)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Indian Railway Station Inquiry Counter:**
>
> Imagine you walk up to a station inquiry counter and ask: *"What is the fare from Ernakulam to {destination city}?"* The clerk does **not** search a giant if-else ladder of "If destination == Delhi, then …; else if destination == Chennai, then …". Instead, the clerk consults a **pre-printed fare table** indexed by the destination's serial number, jumps directly to the row, and reads out the fare.
>
> The **case statement works identically**:
> - The **destination** = controlling expression `E`
> - The **fare table** = **jump table** built at compile time
> - The **clerk's lookup** = one direct indexed jump
> - The **fallback "destination not found" reply** = the `default` case
>
> Just as the clerk's table is only efficient when destinations are *densely numbered* (1, 2, 3, …) and the table becomes wasteful when destinations are *sparse* (1, 7, 1000, …), the compiler must **choose between a jump-table strategy and an if-else strategy** based on how tightly packed the case labels are.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Translation

| Metric | Notation | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Number of case labels | $n$ | dimensionless integer $\geq 1$ |
| Minimum case value | $L = \min(V_i)$ | same type as $E$ |
| Maximum case value | $H = \max(V_i)$ | same type as $E$ |
| Range (spread) | $H - L$ | same type as $E$ |
| Density ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{n}{H - L + 1}$ | $0 < \rho \le 1$ |
| Jump-table threshold | $\rho_{th}$ | often **0.5** to **0.7** |
| Size of jump table | $H - L + 1$ entries | memory words |

> [!NOTE]
> **Density ratio** $\rho$ is the *most important metric* the KTU examiner expects you to compute before selecting a translation strategy. A high $\rho$ (close to 1) → **jump table**; a low $\rho$ (close to 0) → **if-then-else chain**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Canonical Translation Strategies

A KTU-level compiler is expected to know **two principal schemes** for lowering a case statement into three-address code (TAC). The choice between them is a classical engineering trade-off: **time** vs. **space**.

#### 2.1.1 Strategy A — Sequential If-Then-Else (Sparse-Case Strategy)

When the case labels are *sparse* ($\rho$ is small), building a jump table wastes memory. Instead, the compiler generates a linear chain of conditional jumps — one per label.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Evaluate the controlling expression `E` once and store it in a temporary `t`.
2. For each label $V_i$ (in any order, but typically ascending):
   - Generate the test: **if t = $V_i$ then goto $L_i$**.
3. After all labels are exhausted, generate: **goto $L_{default}$**.
4. Each target label $L_i$ is emitted immediately before the code for $S_i$.
5. After the body $S_i$, generate: **goto $L_{next}$** (to skip over remaining cases).

**Resulting Three-Address Code Skeleton:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = E \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 = V_1 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 = V_2 \text{ goto } L_2 \\
&\quad \vdots \\
&\text{4. } \text{if } t_1 = V_{n-1} \text{ goto } L_{n-1} \\
&\text{5. } \text{goto } L_{default} \\
&L_1: \;\; \text{code for } S_1 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \;\; \text{code for } S_2 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&\quad \vdots \\
&L_{n-1}: \;\; \text{code for } S_{n-1} \\
&\quad\;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_{default}: \;\; \text{code for } S_n \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works** *(the 'How' behind the 'Why')*:
- It performs at most $n$ comparisons in the worst case.
- It is **order-independent** in principle but **time-optimal** if labels are arranged to match the statistical probability of each $V_i$ (i.e., the most frequent case is tested first).

---

#### 2.1.2 Strategy B — Jump Table (Dense-Case Strategy)

When the case labels are *dense* ($\rho \ge \rho_{th}$), the compiler builds a **jump table** at compile time. The table is an array of $H - L + 1$ code labels, where the entry at index $k$ is the label of the case matching $V = L + k$. Unfilled entries point to the default label.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Evaluate the controlling expression `E` once and store it in a temporary `t`.
2. Check whether `t` lies within $[L, H]$:
   - If `t < L` or `t > H`, jump directly to the default label.
3. Compute the table index: `i = t − L`.
4. Indirect jump: **goto table[i]**, where `table[i]` is a code address.

**Resulting Three-Address Code Skeleton:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = E \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 < L \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 > H \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{4. } t_2 = t_1 - L \\
&\text{5. } \text{if } t_2 > H - L \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{6. } \text{goto table}[t_2] \\
&L_1: \;\; \text{code for } S_1 \;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \;\; \text{code for } S_2 \;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&\quad \vdots \\
&L_{n-1}: \;\; \text{code for } S_{n-1} \;\; \text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_{default}: \;\; \text{code for } S_n \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**The Jump Table `table[]` is constructed as:**

$$
\text{table}[V_i - L] = L_i \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n-1\}
$$
$$
\text{table}[k] = L_{default} \quad \text{for all other } k \in [0, H-L]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** On most modern architectures, the indirect jump (`goto table[i]`) is implemented as a single indexed memory load + jump. This makes the **time complexity constant $O(1)$** for dispatch, regardless of the number of cases. This is why GCC, Clang, and the HotSpot JVM use jump tables whenever the density threshold is met.

---

#### 2.1.3 Strategy C — Hybrid / Two-Level Approach

A practical KTU-examined approach (used by GCC's *switch conversion pass*) is:

1. Generate a small **binary-search tree of comparisons** when $n$ is moderate and density is moderate.
2. Fall back to a **jump table** when the density is high.
3. Fall back to a **linear if-else** when the density is very low.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This same density-based strategy is implemented in the GCC compiler's `gimple_switch_conversion` pass and in LLVM's `SelectionDAGBuilder` for `switch` lowering. Software engineers writing interpreters (e.g., Python's `match/case`, JavaScript's V8 engine) use the identical heuristic.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of case labels | $n$ | Defines search space size |
| 2 | Range span | $H - L$ | Worst-case jump-table size |
| 3 | Density ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{n}{H - L + 1}$ | Strategy selector |
| 4 | Jump-table size (words) | $H - L + 1$ | Memory cost of Strategy B |
| 5 | Worst-case comparisons (Strategy A) | $n$ | Time cost of sparse path |
| 6 | Dispatch time (Strategy B) | $O(1)$ | Time cost of dense path |
| 7 | Strategy threshold | $\rho \ge \rho_{th}$ (typical $\rho_{th} = 0.5$) | Compile-time decision |
| 8 | Table index | $i = V_i - L$ | Array offset calculation |
| 9 | Out-of-range check | $t < L \lor t > H$ | Bounds safety before indirect jump |
| 10 | Default fall-through | $\text{table}[k] = L_{default}$ for unused $k$ | Guarantees defined behaviour |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When asked "which strategy to use?", **always compute $\rho$ first**, then state the threshold rule, then justify your decision. Examiners award marks for the *reasoning chain*, not just the final answer.

---

### 2.3 Why Case-Statement Translation is Non-Trivial

A case statement is *syntactically simple* but *semantically rich*. Several subtle engineering considerations affect translation:

1. **Duplicate labels** → must be flagged as a *semantic error* during type checking, before code generation.
2. **Fall-through** (in C) vs. **implicit break** (in Java) → the front-end semantic analyser must insert `goto L_{next}` at the end of each $S_i$ unless the user wrote a `break`.
3. **Range of E** → if the type's range is huge (e.g., a 32-bit int), a pure jump table is infeasible; the compiler must fall back to Strategy A.
4. **Constant folding** → the labels $V_i$ are *compile-time constants* by language spec, so all metric computations ($\rho$, $L$, $H$) happen at compile time — **zero run-time overhead** in choosing the strategy.
5. **Optimization interaction** → after translation, the standard optimization passes (constant propagation, dead-code elimination, branch prediction) can simplify the resulting TAC.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sparse Case (Strategy A)

**Source Code (C-style):**
```c
switch (x + 1) {
    case 1:  a = b;  break;
    case 2:  a = c;  break;
    case 5:  a = d;  break;
    case 10: a = e;  break;
    default: a = f;
}
```

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

- Controlling expression: $E = x + 1$
- Label set: $V = \{1, 2, 5, 10\}$, so $n = 4$
- $L = \min(V) = 1$, $H = \max(V) = 10$
- Range span: $H - L = 10 - 1 = 9$
- Density: $\rho = \dfrac{n}{H - L + 1} = \dfrac{4}{10} = 0.4$

**Step 2 — Apply Decision Rule:**

$$
\rho = 0.4 < \rho_{th} = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Use Strategy A (If-Then-Else Chain)}
$$

**Step 3 — Generate Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = x + 1 \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 = 1 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 = 2 \text{ goto } L_2 \\
&\text{4. } \text{if } t_1 = 5 \text{ goto } L_3 \\
&\text{5. } \text{if } t_1 = 10 \text{ goto } L_4 \\
&\text{6. } \text{goto } L_5 \\
&L_1: \; a = b \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \; a = c \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_3: \; a = d \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_4: \; a = e \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_5: \; a = f \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Quadruple Representation (for each `if` instruction):**

| # | Op | Arg1 | Arg2 | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `+` | `x` | `1` | `t1` |
| 2 | `==` | `t1` | `1` | `L1` |
| 3 | `==` | `t1` | `2` | `L2` |
| 4 | `==` | `t1` | `5` | `L3` |
| 5 | `==` | `t1` | `10` | `L4` |
| 6 | `goto` | — | — | `L5` |
| 7 | `=` | `b` | — | `a` |
| 8 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 9 | `=` | `c` | — | `a` |
| 10 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 11 | `=` | `d` | — | `a` |
| 12 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 13 | `=` | `e` | — | `a` |
| 14 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 15 | `=` | `f` | — | `a` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** Quadruple is a 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. For unary ops, `arg2` is left blank. For control-flow (`goto`), `arg1` and `arg2` are blank, and `result` holds the target label. The conditional `==` jumps to the label stored in `result` if the comparison is true; otherwise, execution falls through to the next quadruple.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Dense Case (Strategy B)

**Source Code (C-style):**
```c
switch (k) {
    case 0:  a = b;  break;
    case 1:  a = c;  break;
    case 2:  a = d;  break;
    case 3:  a = e;  break;
    case 4:  a = f;  break;
    default: a = g;
}
```

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

- $E = k$
- $V = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$, so $n = 5$
- $L = 0$, $H = 4$
- Range span: $H - L = 4$
- Density: $\rho = \dfrac{5}{0 - 4 + 1} \cdot \ldots = \dfrac{5}{5} = 1.0$

**Step 2 — Apply Decision Rule:**

$$
\rho = 1.0 \ge 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Use Strategy B (Jump Table)}
$$

**Step 3 — Build the Jump Table:**

$$
\text{table}[0] = L_1, \;\; \text{table}[1] = L_2, \;\; \text{table}[2] = L_3, \;\; \text{table}[3] = L_4, \;\; \text{table}[4] = L_5
$$

**Step 4 — Generate Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = k \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 < 0 \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 > 4 \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{4. } t_2 = t_1 - 0 \;\;\;\; \text{(i.e., } t_2 = t_1\text{)} \\
&\text{5. } \text{goto table}[t_2] \\
&L_1: \; a = b \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \; a = c \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_3: \; a = d \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_4: \; a = e \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_5: \; a = f \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_{default}: \; a = g \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Analytical Summary Table:**

| Property | Example 1 (Sparse) | Example 2 (Dense) |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | 4 | 5 |
| $L$ | 1 | 0 |
| $H$ | 10 | 4 |
| $\rho$ | 0.4 | 1.0 |
| Strategy | If-Then-Else | Jump Table |
| Worst-case comparisons | 4 | 1 (after bounds check) |
| Auxiliary memory | 0 words | 5 words |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection Decision Tree

| Given State of Case Statement | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho \ge 0.5$ AND range $H - L$ is bounded (e.g., $\le 256$) | **Jump Table** | $O(1)$ dispatch, table fits in cache |
| $\rho < 0.5$ AND labels are scattered | **Sequential If-Then-Else** | Wastes no memory on sparse entries |
| $\rho \ge 0.5$ but range is huge (e.g., $> 10^6$) | **Binary Search Tree** | Avoids gigantic table, $O(\log n)$ dispatch |
| $n \le 3$ | **Inline If-Then-Else** | Table setup overhead exceeds comparison cost |
| Labels are character literals | **Jump Table (ASCII-range)** | Natural density in $[0, 127]$ |
| Source has explicit `break` after every case | Either strategy | No fall-through complications |
| Source *omits* `break` (C-style fall-through) | **If-Then-Else with explicit gotos** | Preserves user's intended control flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** When answering a 14-mark question on case-statement translation, you must (a) compute $\rho$, (b) state the decision rule, (c) generate the TAC, and (d) draw the corresponding flowchart or quadruples. Missing *any one* of these four parts typically costs 3–4 marks.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Source Code: switch statement"] --> B["Lexical + Syntax Analysis"]
    B --> C["Parse Tree Constructed"]
    C --> D["Semantic Analysis: Validate Labels"]
    D --> E["Compute Metrics: L, H, n, rho"]
    E --> F{"rho greater than or equal threshold?"}
    F -- "Yes (Dense)" --> G["Strategy B: Jump Table Translation"]
    F -- "No (Sparse)" --> H["Strategy A: If-Then-Else Translation"]
    G --> I["Three-Address Code Generation"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Quadruple / Triple Emission"]
    J --> K["Optimizer Passes"]
    K --> L["Target Code Generation"]
```

### 4.2 Sparse-Case Control-Flow Diagram (Strategy A)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Entry: evaluate E into t1"] --> c1{"t1 equals V1?"}
    c1 -- "Yes" --> s1["Execute S1"]
    c1 -- "No" --> c2{"t1 equals V2?"}
    c2 -- "Yes" --> s2["Execute S2"]
    c2 -- "No" --> c3{"t1 equals Vn-1?"}
    c3 -- "Yes" --> sN1["Execute Sn-1"]
    c3 -- "No" --> defA["Execute default Sn"]
    s1 --> nxt["goto L_next"]
    s2 --> nxt
    sN1 --> nxt
    defA --> nxt
    nxt --> exitA["Exit case statement"]
```

### 4.3 Dense-Case Control-Flow Diagram (Strategy B)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startB["Entry: evaluate E into t1"] --> lo{"t1 less than L?"}
    lo -- "Yes" --> defB["Execute default Sn"]
    lo -- "No" --> hi{"t1 greater than H?"}
    hi -- "Yes" --> defB
    hi -- "No" --> idx["t2 = t1 minus L"]
    idx --> jmp["Indirect goto table at t2"]
    jmp --> jt["Jump Table lookup: O of 1"]
    jt --> s1b["Execute S1 if table hit"]
    jt --> s2b["Execute S2 if table hit"]
    jt --> sNb["Execute Sn-1 if table hit"]
    jt --> defB
    s1b --> nxtb["goto L_next"]
    s2b --> nxtb
    sNb --> nxtb
    defB --> nxtb
    nxtb --> exitB["Exit case statement"]
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Strategy Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    q["Input: case statement with n labels, range H minus L"] --> cRho{"Compute rho equals n divided by H minus L plus 1"}
    cRho -->|rho less than 0.5| sparse["Use Strategy A: If-Then-Else Chain"]
    cRho -->|rho greater than or equal 0.5 and range less than 256| dense["Use Strategy B: Jump Table"]
    cRho -->|rho greater than or equal 0.5 but range huge| hybrid["Use Strategy C: Binary Search Tree"]
    sparse --> ta["Output: Linear TAC of n comparisons"]
    dense --> tb["Output: Bounds check plus indirect jump TAC"]
    hybrid --> tc["Output: Logarithmic depth comparison tree"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Architecture of a Compiler's Case-Handling Front-End

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Source Program"] --> lex["Lexer: tokenize switch, case, default"]
    lex --> par["Parser: build switch CST node"]
    par --> sem["Semantic Checker"]
    sem --> met["Metrics Computer: L, H, n, rho"]
    met --> sel["Strategy Selector"]
    sel --> transA["Translator A: If-Else TAC Gen"]
    sel --> transB["Translator B: Jump-Table TAC Gen"]
    sel --> transC["Translator C: BST TAC Gen"]
    transA --> out["Three-Address Code Output"]
    transB --> out
    transC --> out
    out --> opt["Optimizer"]
    opt --> cg["Code Generator"]
    cg --> tgt["Target Assembly / Bytecode"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When the question says "explain the translation of case statements with a neat diagram", examiners expect at least the **control-flow graph** (Section 4.2 or 4.3) and the **strategy-decision flow** (Section 4.4). Drawing the parse tree is *not* required at this stage — it is covered in Module 2 (Syntax Analysis).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a case statement and list the two principal strategies used by a compiler to translate it into intermediate code.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **case statement** is a multi-way branching construct in a programming language that selects one of several statement groups for execution by comparing the value of a controlling expression `E` against a set of distinct compile-time constant labels $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_{n-1}$, with an optional `default` branch.

The two principal translation strategies are:
1. **Sequential If-Then-Else Chain (Strategy A):** Used when labels are *sparse*; generates $n$ conditional comparisons, one per label. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Jump-Table Method (Strategy B):** Used when labels are *dense*; computes a table index `i = t − L` and performs a single indirect jump via `goto table[i]`, achieving $O(1)$ dispatch. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the density ratio of a case statement? How is it used to choose a translation strategy?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **density ratio** $\rho$ of a case statement is defined as:

$$
\rho = \frac{n}{H - L + 1}
$$

where $n$ is the number of case labels, $L = \min(V_i)$, and $H = \max(V_i)$.

It is used as follows **(2 marks):**
- If $\rho \ge \rho_{th}$ (typically **0.5**), the labels are **dense**, and the compiler uses a **jump table** for $O(1)$ dispatch.
- If $\rho < \rho_{th}$, the labels are **sparse**, and the compiler generates an **if-then-else chain** to avoid wasting memory on a mostly-empty table.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Strategy Selection and Full TAC Generation*

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **two strategies** used for translating a case (switch) statement into three-address code. Clearly state the **decision criterion** that a compiler uses to choose between them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C-style code segment:
```c
switch (m + 2) {
    case 1:  x = a;  break;
    case 2:  x = b;  break;
    case 4:  x = c;  break;
    case 7:  x = d;  break;
    default: x = e;
}
```
**(i)** Compute the **density ratio** $\rho$ and decide which translation strategy to apply. **(3 Marks)**
**(ii)** Generate the complete **three-address code** for the chosen strategy. **(4 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of the Two Strategies (7 Marks):**

**Strategy 1 — Sequential If-Then-Else Chain (Sparse Case):** **(3.5 Marks)**

When the case labels are *sparse* (low $\rho$), the compiler translates the case statement into a chain of conditional jumps. The controlling expression is evaluated once and stored in a temporary `t1`. For each label $V_i$, a comparison `if t1 = Vi goto Li` is emitted. If none match, control flows to the default label. This avoids building a large memory-resident table with mostly empty entries. Its time complexity is $O(n)$.

**Strategy 2 — Jump-Table Method (Dense Case):** **(3.5 Marks)**

When the case labels are *dense* (high $\rho$, typically $\rho \ge 0.5$), the compiler constructs a **jump table** of size $H - L + 1$ words at compile time. At run time, the controlling expression is evaluated; an out-of-range check is performed; the table index is computed as `i = t1 − L`; and a single **indirect jump** `goto table[i]` dispatches in $O(1)$ time. This is the preferred strategy for dense, contiguous labels.

**Decision Criterion:** The compiler computes the **density ratio** $\rho = \dfrac{n}{H - L + 1}$. If $\rho \ge \rho_{th}$ (commonly 0.5), Strategy 2 is chosen; otherwise, Strategy 1 is used.

---

**(b) Worked Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Compute density ratio (3 Marks):**

$$
n = 4, \quad L = \min\{1, 2, 4, 7\} = 1, \quad H = \max\{1, 2, 4, 7\} = 7
$$

$$
H - L + 1 = 7 - 1 + 1 = 7
$$

$$
\rho = \frac{n}{H - L + 1} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.571
$$

**Decision:** Since $\rho \approx 0.571 \ge 0.5$, the labels are **dense enough** → **Use Strategy 2 (Jump Table)**.

**(ii) Three-Address Code (4 Marks):**

Build the jump table:

$$
\text{table}[0] = L_1, \;\; \text{table}[1] = L_2, \;\; \text{table}[2] = L_{default}, \;\; \text{table}[3] = L_3, \;\; \text{table}[4..5] = L_{default}, \;\; \text{table}[6] = L_4
$$

(The $m$-th entry corresponds to case value $V = L + m = 1 + m$.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = m + 2 \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 < 1 \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 > 7 \text{ goto } L_{default} \\
&\text{4. } t_2 = t_1 - 1 \\
&\text{5. } \text{goto table}[t_2] \\
&L_1: \; x = a \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \; x = b \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_3: \; x = c \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_4: \; x = d \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_{default}: \; x = e \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice: Sparse Strategy Worked Example*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** What is a **case statement**? With a neat sketch, explain how a compiler translates a case statement when the case labels are **sparse** (low density). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Translate the following case statement into **three-address code** using the **sparse-case strategy**:
```c
switch (y * 3) {
    case 0:  p = 1;  break;
    case 5:  p = 2;  break;
    case 10: p = 3;  break;
    case 15: p = 4;  break;
    default: p = 0;
}
```
Also represent the same as a **sequence of quadruples**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Case Statement and Sparse Translation (7 Marks):**

A case statement selects one of several statement groups for execution by comparing a controlling expression against a set of constant labels. **(1 Mark)**

For **sparse** labels (i.e., density ratio $\rho < 0.5$), the compiler uses the **if-then-else chain** strategy to avoid wasting memory. The steps are: **(4 Marks)**

1. Evaluate $E$ into a temporary `t1`.
2. For each label $V_i$, emit `if t1 = Vi goto Li`.
3. After all labels, emit `goto L_default`.
4. Each $L_i$ is placed before the code for $S_i$, which ends with `goto L_next` to skip subsequent cases.

*Sketch (control-flow graph already shown in Section 4.2 of these notes — student should redraw it on paper).* **(2 Marks)**

---

**(b) Three-Address Code and Quadruples (7 Marks):**

**Compute metrics:** $E = y \cdot 3$, $V = \{0, 5, 10, 15\}$, $n = 4$, $L = 0$, $H = 15$.

$$
\rho = \frac{4}{15 - 0 + 1} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25 < 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Strategy A}
$$

**Three-Address Code (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{1. } t_1 = y \cdot 3 \\
&\text{2. } \text{if } t_1 = 0 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&\text{3. } \text{if } t_1 = 5 \text{ goto } L_2 \\
&\text{4. } \text{if } t_1 = 10 \text{ goto } L_3 \\
&\text{5. } \text{if } t_1 = 15 \text{ goto } L_4 \\
&\text{6. } \text{goto } L_5 \\
&L_1: \; p = 1 \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_2: \; p = 2 \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_3: \; p = 3 \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_4: \; p = 4 \;\;\text{goto } L_{next} \\
&L_5: \; p = 0 \\
&L_{next}:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quadruple Table (3 Marks):**

| # | Op | Arg1 | Arg2 | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `*` | `y` | `3` | `t1` |
| 2 | `==` | `t1` | `0` | `L1` |
| 3 | `==` | `t1` | `5` | `L2` |
| 4 | `==` | `t1` | `10` | `L3` |
| 5 | `==` | `t1` | `15` | `L4` |
| 6 | `goto` | — | — | `L5` |
| 7 | `=` | `1` | — | `p` |
| 8 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 9 | `=` | `2` | — | `p` |
| 10 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 11 | `=` | `3` | — | `p` |
| 12 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 13 | `=` | `4` | — | `p` |
| 14 | `goto` | — | — | `Lnext` |
| 15 | `=` | `0` | — | `p` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate `E` once.** Many students emit `y * 3` inside every comparison, repeating the multiplication. The temporary `t1 = y * 3` must be computed *exactly once*. *(−2 marks penalty.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the `goto L_next` after each case body.** Without it, control *falls through* into the next case's body, producing incorrect semantics. *(−2 marks.)*
> 3. **Computing $\rho$ incorrectly.** Students often use $H - L$ instead of $H - L + 1$ in the denominator. The `+1` is essential — it is the *count* of integers in the closed interval $[L, H]$. *(−1 mark.)*
> 4. **Not handling the `default` case.** Every translation must include the fallback path — either `goto L_default` (Strategy A) or table entries pointing to `L_default` (Strategy B). *(−2 marks.)*
> 5. **Confusing labels and addresses.** A label `L1` is a *symbolic name*; the jump table stores these labels as code addresses. Quadruples list them in the `Result` field. *(−1 mark.)*
> 6. **Skipping the bounds check in Strategy B.** Always include `if t1 < L goto L_default` and `if t1 > H goto L_default` before the indirect jump. *(−2 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Case statement** = multi-way branch on a controlling expression `E` against constant labels $V_i$.
- [x] **Two main strategies**: *(A)* If-Then-Else chain for **sparse** labels; *(B)* Jump Table for **dense** labels.
- [x] **Density ratio formula**: $\rho = \dfrac{n}{H - L + 1}$, where $n$ = #labels, $L$ = min label, $H$ = max label.
- [x] **Decision rule**: $\rho \ge 0.5$ → Strategy B; $\rho < 0.5$ → Strategy A.
- [x] **Strategy A complexity**: $O(n)$ comparisons in the worst case, zero auxiliary memory.
- [x] **Strategy B complexity**: $O(1)$ dispatch after a constant-time bounds check, but uses $H - L + 1$ words of memory.
- [x] **Three-address code invariant**: Evaluate `E` *once* into a temporary; test the temporary against labels.
- [x] **Fall-through** must be prevented by emitting `goto L_next` at the end of each case body.
- [x] **Bounds check** is mandatory before the indirect jump in Strategy B.
- [x] **Quadruple format** for case translation: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` with labels stored in `result`.
- [x] **Compile-time metrics**: $L$, $H$, $n$, $\rho$ are all computed by the front-end *at compile time* — zero run-time cost for choosing the strategy.
- [x] **Hybrid Strategy C** uses a binary-search tree when $\rho$ is moderate but the range is too large for a jump table.
- [x] **Real-world use**: GCC's `gimple_switch_conversion`, LLVM's `SelectionDAGBuilder`, and HotSpot JVM's `tableswitch` / `lookupswitch` bytecode opcodes.
- [x] **KTU 2024 cognitive target**: Apply / Analyze (Bloom's L3–L4) — be ready to *generate* TAC and *justify* the strategy choice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure Calls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure Calls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In compiler design, **Procedure Calls** refer to the mechanism by which a compiler translates and manages the invocation, execution, and return of subprograms (functions, procedures, or methods) during runtime. The procedure call implementation involves the construction of **Activation Records (ARs)** on the runtime stack, management of **parameter passing protocols**, resolution of **non-local variable references** through static and dynamic links, and execution of the standardized **Calling Sequence (prologue)** and **Return Sequence (epilogue)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCST601 Module 4):**
> *Procedure Calls* in the KTU 2024 framework encompasses the translation of procedure activations, storage allocation strategies for activation records, parameter passing mechanisms, and the runtime stack management that supports nested and recursive subprogram invocations.

## 1.2 The Three Core Sub-Systems of Procedure Calls

A procedure call is decomposed into three logically distinct phases that the compiler must correctly implement:

| Sub-System | Compiler Responsibility | Runtime Artifact |
|------------|------------------------|------------------|
| **Calling Sequence** | Generate code to evaluate arguments and set up the new AR | Prologue instructions |
| **Body Execution** | Resolve local/global variable addresses and parameter accesses | Active AR on stack |
| **Return Sequence** | Copy back results, restore previous AR, transfer control | Epilogue instructions |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Office Meeting" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: A Manager Calling a Subordinate for a Report**

Imagine a senior manager (the *calling procedure*) who needs information from a junior analyst (the *called procedure*).

1. **Saving State**: Before leaving his desk, the manager **pins a sticky note on his chair** indicating exactly what he was doing (saving the return address and current registers). This is the **dynamic link** and **return address** in the AR.

2. **Handing Over Inputs**: The manager hands the analyst a **sealed envelope** containing the data needed (parameters) — this represents **parameter passing** (by value, reference, etc.).

3. **Creating a Workspace**: The analyst sits at a **new desk with a fresh notepad** (a new activation record) where all his temporary working papers (local variables) are stored.

4. **Knowing His Boss**: The analyst places a **reference card** on his desk pointing to his manager's desk number (this is the **static link / access link**), so he can fetch shared files (non-local variables) if needed.

5. **Returning the Result**: Upon completion, the analyst places the **report in a designated tray** (return value), tears up his sticky notes (destroys the AR), and signals the manager.

6. **Resuming Work**: The manager **unpins his note**, picks up the report, and continues from where he left off.

This entire choreography — performed flawlessly thousands of times per second — is what a compiler's procedure call machinery must implement.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Activation**: The execution of a procedure is termed an *activation*. Each execution of a procedure body creates a *new activation*.
> - **Activation Record (AR) / Stack Frame**: A contiguous block of memory on the runtime stack holding all information (parameters, return address, links, locals) needed for one activation.
> - **Activation Tree**: A tree depicting the dynamic control flow among procedure activations.
> - **Control Stack**: The runtime stack holding the ARs of currently active procedures.
> - **Frame Pointer (FP) / Base Pointer (BP)**: A register pointing to a fixed location within the current AR (typically the start), enabling stable addressing of fields regardless of stack growth.
> - **Stack Pointer (SP)**: A register pointing to the *top* of the control stack, dynamically moving as the stack grows and shrinks.
> - **Static Link / Access Link**: A pointer to the AR of the lexically enclosing scope, used to access non-local variables in languages with nested scopes (Pascal, Ada, PL/I).
> - **Dynamic Link / Control Link**: A pointer to the AR of the *caller* (the immediately preceding activation on the stack), used to restore the previous AR on return.
> - **Display**: An array of pointers, one per nesting depth, providing O(1) access to the AR of any enclosing scope.
> - **Calling Sequence**: The code executed by the caller just before transferring control to the callee.
> - **Return Sequence**: The code executed by the callee just before transferring control back to the caller.
> - **Parameter Passing Mechanism**: The protocol for transmitting data between caller and callee (by value, by reference, by value-result, by name).
> - **Thunk**: A small closure used in *call-by-name* to compute the address of an argument at the moment of its actual use.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Activation Record (Stack Frame)

A standard KTU-aligned activation record is laid out as a contiguous block of memory. The exact field order may vary by compiler, but the *logical* layout typically follows:

| Field | Purpose | Set By |
|-------|---------|--------|
| **Actual Parameters** | Values/references passed by the caller | Caller |
| **Return Address** | Address of instruction to resume in caller | Caller |
| **Saved Frame Pointer (Dynamic Link)** | Previous FP value, used to restore on return | Callee (prologue) |
| **Static Link / Access Link** | Pointer to AR of lexically enclosing scope | Callee (prologue) |
| **Saved Registers** | State of caller-saved and callee-saved registers | Callee (prologue) |
| **Local Variables** | Storage for procedure's local data | Callee (prologue) |
| **Temporaries** | Scratch space for expression evaluation | Callee |

> [!NOTE]
> The frame pointer (FP) typically points to the *first word* of the activation record (often where the saved dynamic link is stored), giving the compiler a stable base for offset-based addressing of all AR fields.

## 2.2 Parameter Passing Mechanisms

The KTU syllabus explicitly requires the student to distinguish the following parameter passing techniques. Each has a distinct runtime semantic and memory model.

### 2.2.1 Call-by-Value
- The **actual value** of the argument is copied into the callee's parameter slot.
- Modifications inside the callee **do not affect** the caller's variable.
- Implementation: simple `MOV` or `STORE` instruction in the calling sequence.

### 2.2.2 Call-by-Reference (Call-by-Address)
- The **address** of the caller's argument is passed to the callee.
- The callee accesses the argument *indirectly* through this pointer.
- Modifications inside the callee **do affect** the caller's variable.
- Implementation: pass a pointer; dereference it for both reads and writes.

### 2.2.3 Call-by-Value-Result (Copy-Restore)
- A hybrid: at call time, the **value** is copied in (like by-value). At return time, the **final value** of the formal parameter is copied back to the actual argument.
- Useful for parallel/async contexts but can produce non-intuitive results with aliasing.

### 2.2.4 Call-by-Name
- A **textual substitution** is performed: every occurrence of the formal parameter in the callee is replaced by the *actual argument expression* (delayed evaluation via **thunks**).
- Re-evaluated *each time* the formal is referenced, with the callee's scope.
- Used historically in Algol 60; rarely used in modern languages.

### 2.2.5 Call-by-Need (Lazy Evaluation)
- A memoized variant of call-by-name: the argument expression is evaluated **only on first use**, and the result is cached for subsequent uses.
- Used in Haskell, lazy functional languages.

## 2.3 The Calling Sequence and Return Sequence

A clean division of labor between the caller and the callee is essential. The standard KTU-examined convention is:

### **Calling Sequence (executed by the caller)**
1. Evaluate each actual parameter and store it in the new AR (or in registers per ABI).
2. Store the **return address** in the AR.
3. Push a pointer to the **static link** (if applicable) — or set it in the callee.
4. **Jump** to the callee's entry point.

### **Prologue (executed by the callee upon entry)**
1. Push the **old FP** (saving the dynamic link).
2. Set **FP = SP** (frame pointer now anchors the new AR).
3. Allocate space for **local variables** by adjusting SP downward.
4. Save any **callee-saved registers** that will be modified.

### **Epilogue (executed by the callee before return)**
1. Place the **return value** in a designated register (e.g., `EAX` / `x0`).
2. Restore **callee-saved registers**.
3. Set **SP = FP** (deallocate locals).
4. Pop the **old FP** into FP register.
5. **Return** to the saved return address (POP or RET instruction).

### **Return Sequence (executed by the caller after return)**
1. Copy the return value from the agreed register.
2. Pop the callee's AR (adjust SP past the parameter area).
3. Resume normal execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In many ABIs (System V AMD64, ARM AAPCS), a hybrid strategy is used: the caller allocates the *entire* AR including parameters, sets up its own dynamic link, and the callee only handles local allocation and register saves. This minimizes prologue/epilogue code and enables variable-arity calls.

## 2.4 Static vs. Dynamic Links

### **Dynamic Link (Control Link)**
- Points to the AR of the **caller** — the immediately preceding activation on the control stack.
- Used for **returning control** to the correct caller and for debugging/traceback.
- Depends only on *dynamic* call sequence.

### **Static Link (Access Link)**
- Points to the AR of the **lexically enclosing** procedure — i.e., the most recent activation of the procedure that textually encloses the callee.
- Used to access **non-local variables** following lexical (static) scoping rules.
- Depends on the **syntactic structure** of the program, not the call sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
