<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Sethi-Ullman Labeling

### Problem Statement
> Evaluate the expression `((a + b) - c) * ((d * e) + f)` and determine the **minimum number of registers** required using the Sethi-Ullman algorithm. Then **rebalance** the tree (using associativity/commutativity where allowed) to show whether registers can be reduced.

### Step 1: Construct the Original Syntax Tree

The expression has two main subtrees joined by `*`:
- **Left subtree L1:** `((a + b) - c)`
- **Right subtree R1:** `((d * e) + f)`

### Step 2: Apply Sethi-Ullman Labeling (Bottom-Up)

**Subtree `((a + b) - c)`:**

| Node | Children Labels | Rule Applied | Label |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `b` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `(a + b)` | 1, 1 | Equal → 1 + 1 | 2 |
| `c` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `((a+b) - c)` | 2, 1 | Unequal → max | 2 |

**Subtree `((d * e) + f)`:**

| Node | Children Labels | Rule Applied | Label |
|---|---|---|---|
| `d` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `e` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `(d * e)` | 1, 1 | Equal → 1 + 1 | 2 |
| `f` (leaf) | — | Leaf rule | 1 |
| `((d*e) + f)` | 2, 1 | Unequal → max | 2 |

**Root `*` node:**

| Node | Children Labels | Rule Applied | Label |
|---|---|---|---|
| `((a+b)-c) * ((d*e)+f)` | 2, 2 | Equal → 2 + 1 | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The original tree requires a **minimum of 3 registers** for evaluation.

### Step 3: Generate the Code (Sequential Evaluation)

Since both subtrees have label 2 (equal), we must evaluate one **first** and store its result, then evaluate the other. Let's evaluate left first:

```
t1 = a + b      // uses 1 register, leaves 2 free
t1 = t1 - c     // overwrites t1
t2 = d * e      // uses t2 (since t1 holds left result)
t2 = t2 + f     // overwrites t2
t1 = t1 * t2    // final result
```

**Register trace:** Peak usage = 2 (when `t1` and `t2` are both alive).

Wait — that uses only 2 registers in practice, but the Sethi-Ullman label says 3. Why?

> [!NOTE]
> The Sethi-Ullman label is a **theoretical minimum in the worst case**. With careful instruction ordering, we can often do better. The label is **conservative** and provides an **upper bound on the optimal register count**.

### Step 4: Tree-Height Balancing Attempt

For `*` operator, we can **freely swap the left and right children** (multiplication is commutative). This doesn't change the labels, so register count stays the same.

For `+` operators, we can also swap. The `-` operator is **not commutative**, so we **cannot** swap `(a+b) - c` to `c - (a+b)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion for this example:** Tree-height balancing through child reordering **does not reduce the register count below 3** in this specific expression because the structure is already near-optimal.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Example — Where Balancing ACTUALLY Helps

### Problem: `a + b + c + d + e + f + g + h`

The naive **left-associative** tree is highly **lopsided**:

```
         (+)
        /   \
      (+)    h
     /   \
   (+)    g
  /   \
 (+)   f
...
```

**Sethi-Ullman labeling of this chain:** Each level adds 1, so $L(\text{root}) = 8$ registers.

### Balanced Reformulation

Group as a **balanced binary tree** of additions:
```
        (+)
      /    \
    (+)    (+)
   /  \    /  \
  (+) (+) (+)  (+)
  /\  /\  /\   /\
 a b  c d  e f  g h
```

**Sethi-Ullman labeling of balanced tree:**

- Each leaf: 1
- Each bottom `+`: children both 1 → label = 2
- Each middle `+`: children both 2 → label = 3
- Root `+`: children both 3 → label = 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Register count drops from 8 to 4 — a 50% reduction!**

### Step-by-Step Code Generation for the Balanced Tree

Let $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ denote registers. We evaluate the **heavier child first** (both have label 3, so we go left first):

```
R1 = a + b       // R1 holds result
R2 = c + d       // R2 holds result (R1 still holds 'left' for later)
R1 = R1 + R2     // R1 holds (a+b+c+d) — R2 now free
R2 = e + f       // R2 holds result
R3 = g + h       // R3 holds result
R2 = R2 + R3     // R2 holds (e+f+g+h) — R3 now free
R1 = R1 + R2     // R1 holds final result
```

**Peak register usage = 3** (during the line `R3 = g + h`).

> [!NOTE]
> Theoretical minimum by Sethi-Ullman was 4, but **instruction reordering** brings it down to 3. The actual minimum is bounded by $\lceil \log_2(\text{leaves}) \rceil + 1$ for a balanced tree.

---

## 3.3 Algorithmic Derivation of Time Complexity

The Sethi-Ullman algorithm is a **single bottom-up traversal** of the tree.

For a tree with $n$ nodes:
- Each node is visited **exactly once**
- Constant work per node (label computation)

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n)}$$

**Space complexity:** $O(h)$ where $h$ is the tree height, due to recursion stack. For a balanced tree, $h = O(\log n)$, so space = $O(\log n)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Lopsided Tree | Balanced Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Height** $h$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Registers (Sethi-Ullman)** | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Sequential evaluation time** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Parallel evaluation time ($P$ procs)** | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Memory accesses (temporaries)** | High | Low |
| **Register spills** | Frequent | Rare |

This matrix is a **favorite KTU exam table** — be prepared to fill it in for any expression.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Tree-Height Balancing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Expression] --> B[Parse into Syntax Tree]
    B --> C[Apply Sethi-Ullman Labeling]
    C --> D{For each internal node}
    D -->|Leaves| E[Assign label = 1]
    D -->|Internal| F{Children labels equal?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Node label = child label + 1]
    F -->|No| H[Node label = max of child labels]
    E --> I[Compute Root Label]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Root label <= Available Registers}
    J -->|Yes| K[Generate Optimal Code]
    J -->|No| L[Apply Tree Rebalancing]
    L --> M{Operator Commutative?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Swap child subtrees]
    M -->|No| O[Spill to Memory]
    N --> C
    O --> K
    K --> P[Output Optimized Machine Code]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Before & After Tree Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE: Lopsided Tree — 8 registers"]
        R1[Root +] --> A1[+]
        A1 --> B1[+]
        B1 --> C1[+]
        C1 --> D1[+]
        D1 --> E1[+]
        E1 --> F1[a]
        E1 --> G1[b]
        D1 --> H1[c]
        C1 --> I1[d]
        B1 --> J1[e]
        A1 --> K1[f]
        R1 --> L1[g]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER: Balanced Tree — 4 registers"]
        R2[Root +] --> X1[+]
        R2 --> X2[+]
        X1 --> Y1[+]
        X1 --> Y2[+]
        X2 --> Y3[+]
        X2 --> Y4[+]
        Y1 --> P1[a]
        Y1 --> P2[b]
        Y2 --> P3[c]
        Y2 --> P4[d]
        Y3 --> P5[e]
        Y3 --> P6[f]
        Y4 --> P7[g]
        Y4 --> P8[h]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Sethi-Ullman Code Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Expression Tree Input] --> B[Labeling Pass]
    B --> C[Symbol Table]
    C --> D[Register Pool]
    B --> E[Instruction Selector]
    E --> F[Code Emitter]
    F --> G[Three-Address Code]
    G --> H[Final Machine Code]
    D --> E
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (For Drawing in Exam)

If a Mermaid diagram is not possible in your answer sheet, use this **ASCII-style schematic** of a balanced expression tree for `((a+b) + (c+d)) * ((e+f) + (g+h))`:

```
                [ * ]
              /       \
          [ + ]       [ + ]
         /     \      /    \
      [ + ]  [ + ] [ + ]  [ + ]
      /  \   /  \  /  \    /  \
     a   b  c   d e   f   g   h
```

- **Leaves** (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h): label = 1
- **First-level `+` nodes**: label = 2 (equal children)
- **Second-level `+` nodes**: label = 3 (equal children)
- **Root `*` node**: label = 4 (equal children, both 3)

**Register requirement = 4.** With commutativity, this is the **theoretical minimum** for 8 operands.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Tree-Height Balancing. Why is it important in code generation?

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 (Model)]** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Tree-Height Balancing is a code optimization technique applied on syntax trees or DAGs during the code generation phase of a compiler. The technique restructures an expression tree so that the heights of the subtrees of every internal node are as close to possible (typically differing by at most 1), thereby **minimizing the number of registers/temporaries** required for evaluation.

**Importance:**
1. **Reduces register pressure** — fewer temporaries mean less spilling to memory.
2. **Enables parallel evaluation** — balanced trees can be split across multiple processors with uniform workload.
3. **Shortens the critical path** — useful in hardware synthesis (FPGAs).
4. **Optimal code generation** — combined with the Sethi-Ullman algorithm, it produces the minimum-register code for a given expression.

---

### Question 2: State the Sethi-Ullman labeling rules for a node with two children.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Sethi-Ullman labeling rules for a node with two children labeled $L_1$ (left) and $L_2$ (right) are:

- **If $L_1 = L_2$:** The label of the node is $L_1 + 1$.
- **If $L_1 \neq L_2$:** The label of the node is $\max(L_1, L_2)$.

For **leaf nodes** (operands), the label is always **1**.

The label of the **root node** gives the **minimum number of registers** required to evaluate the expression.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 4 (Model Paper Pattern)]**

### Question A

**For the expression tree shown below, apply the Sethi-Ullman algorithm to determine the minimum number of registers required. Then generate optimal three-address code. Show step-by-step evaluation order.**

```
              ( + )
            /       \
         ( * )      ( + )
        /     \    /     \
      (a)    (b) (c)    ( - )
                       /     \
                     (d)     (e)
```

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Sethi-Ullman Labeling**

**Step 1: Label all leaves** → $a, b, c, d, e$ all get label = **1**.

**Step 2: Label the `-` node** (children: $d=1, e=1$) → Equal, so $L(-) = 1 + 1 = $ **2**.

**Step 3: Label the right `+` node** (children: $c=1, (-)=2$) → Unequal, so $L(+) = \max(1, 2) = $ **2**.

**Step 4: Label the `*` node** (children: $a=1, b=1$) → Equal, so $L(*) = 1 + 1 = $ **2**.

**Step 5: Label the root `+` node** (children: $(*)=2, (+)=2$) → Equal, so $L(+) = 2 + 1 = $ **3**.

> **Answer: Minimum 3 registers required.**

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Optimal Three-Address Code**

Since both children of the root have label 2 (equal), we evaluate the **right** child first (arbitrary choice; left is also valid):

```
t1 = d - e      // (right) — uses 1 register
t1 = c + t1     // (right) — still 1 register (overwrite)
t2 = a * b      // (left) — needs new register since t1 holds 'right' result
t1 = t2 + t1    // root
```

**Register trace:**
- After `t1 = d - e` → R1 alive
- After `t1 = c + t1` → R1 alive (right result)
- After `t2 = a * b` → R1 + R2 alive (peak = 2 registers)
- After `t1 = t2 + t1` → R1 alive (final result)

**Actual peak = 2 registers**, but theoretical minimum from Sethi-Ullman = 3 (the algorithm provides an upper bound; practical reordering can be better).

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Apply the Sethi-Ullman algorithm to evaluate the expression `a + b * c - d / e + f`. Then discuss how tree-height balancing through commutativity of `+` and `*` could affect the register requirement. Generate the optimal three-address code.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Labeling**

Standard left-associative parse of `a + b * c - d / e + f`:

| Subexpression | Parse Tree | Label |
|---|---|---|
| `b * c` | `*` with children 1, 1 | 2 |
| `a + (b*c)` | `+` with children 1, 2 | 2 |
| `d / e` | `/` with children 1, 1 | 2 |
| `(a+b*c) - (d/e)` | `-` with children 2, 2 | **3** |
| `((a+b*c)-(d/e)) + f` | `+` with children 3, 1 | 3 |

**Minimum registers = 3** (from root label).

> **Observation:** Because `*` and `/` have higher precedence, the `b*c` and `d/e` subtrees naturally have label 2. The combined tree is **already near-balanced** in the precedence sense.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Code Generation with Balancing**

Since `+` and `*` are **commutative**, we can swap children to ensure heavier subtrees are evaluated first:

```
// Evaluate the (d/e) sub-subtree first, since at the '-' level
// both sides have label 2, and we want to keep the heavier side's
// result in a register
t1 = d / e       // t1 = (d/e)
t2 = b * c       // t2 = (b*c)
t1 = t1 - t2     // WAIT — subtraction is not commutative!
// Correct order:
t1 = b * c       // left-heavy part of '-'
t1 = a + t1      // t1 = (a + b*c)
t2 = d / e       // t2 = (d/e)
t1 = t1 - t2     // t1 = (a + b*c - d/e)
t1 = t1 + f      // final result
```

**Register peak = 2** (during line `t2 = d / e`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** The Sethi-Ullman upper bound was 3, but instruction reordering (within commutativity constraints) reduces the actual usage to 2. Tree-height balancing provides the **structural** improvement, while instruction scheduling provides the **temporal** improvement. Both work together for optimal code.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the `+1` rule for equal children.** A very common error is to write $L = \max(L_1, L_2)$ for all nodes. Remember: if children are **equal**, you must **add 1**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing tree height with Sethi-Ullman label.** They are **different**! Tree height is a structural measure (longest root-to-leaf path). Sethi-Ullman label is the minimum registers. A tree can be tall but still have a small label.
> 
> 3. **Swapping operands of non-commutative operators.** You **cannot** swap children of `-`, `/`, `%`, or `>` etc. Doing so changes the expression's meaning. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 
> 4. **Not showing the code generation order.** Sethi-Ullman without code generation is **only half the answer** for a 14-mark question. Always produce three-address code.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring common subexpressions.** If a DAG has shared nodes, the basic Sethi-Ullman algorithm must be extended. Mention this as a limitation in your answer.
> 
> 6. **Missing the `L1 = L2` case evaluation order.** When labels are equal, the order doesn't matter for register count, but you must still state this explicitly in your solution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tree-Height Balancing**
> 
> - **Goal:** Minimize the number of registers/temporaries needed to evaluate an expression.
> - **Core Tool:** Sethi-Ullman labeling algorithm.
> - **Leaf Rule:** $L(\text{leaf}) = 1$.
> - **Equal-Children Rule:** $L = L_1 + 1$.
> - **Unequal-Children Rule:** $L = \max(L_1, L_2)$.
> - **Root Label** = Minimum number of registers required.
> - **Tree-Height Balancing** = Reorganizing the tree (using commutativity) so heavier subtrees are evaluated first.
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ for labeling a tree of $n$ nodes.
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(h)$ recursion stack, where $h$ is tree height.
> - **Balanced Tree Registers:** $O(\log n)$ registers for $n$ operands.
> - **Lopsided Tree Registers:** $O(n)$ registers for $n$ operands.
> - **Non-Commutative Operators** (`-`, `/`, `%`, comparisons) **cannot** have children swapped.
> - **Commutative Operators** (`+`, `*`, `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`) **can** have children swapped to balance.
> - **Output Format in KTU:** Three-address code with explicit `t1, t2, ...` temporaries.
> - **DAG Extension:** Shared nodes with multiple parents require a label adjustment of `+ (parents - 1)`.
> - **Industry Use:** Register allocation, JIT compilation, GPU scheduling, hardware synthesis.
> - **Related Concepts:** Peephole optimization, instruction scheduling, register allocation graph coloring (Chaitin's algorithm).
> - **Mnemonic:** "**One, Plus One, or Max**" — remember the three rules as: *1 for leaves, +1 for equals, max for unequals*.

---

**End of Module 4 — Tree-Height Balancing Notes**
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PCCST601 — Compiler Design*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regional Optimization: Superlocal Value Numbering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regional Optimization: Superlocal Value Numbering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Regional Optimization: Superlocal Value Numbering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Value Numbering (VN)** is a classical data-flow analysis technique used by an optimizing compiler to perform **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** and **Constant Folding**. Each distinct *value* (the result of an expression) computed in a program is assigned a small, abstract integer — a **Value Number (VN)**. Two expressions that are syntactically different but compute the same value receive the *same* value number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *Value Numbering is a method of encoding the equivalence of expressions by mapping each newly computed value to a unique abstract value number. If the same value number is reused, the compiler concludes that the corresponding expression has already been computed and the earlier result can be reused.*

**Local Value Numbering (LVN)** is the simplest form — it is restricted to a **single basic block** (sequence of straight-line instructions with a single entry and a single exit).

**Superlocal Value Numbering (SVN)** is the natural generalization. It operates over an **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** — a maximal set of basic blocks that share a *single* common header (entry) block, but may have *multiple* exit blocks. The header dominates every block in the EBB, and the control flow inside the EBB forms a directed acyclic graph (a tree of basic blocks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Superlocal"?**
> It is *more than local* (spans multiple basic blocks) but *less than global* (restricted to a single EBB). This trade-off gives excellent speed-up with very low algorithmic complexity — a property the KTU 2024 scheme examiners frequently test.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of value numbering as a **finger-printing system** used in a chemistry lab:

- Every chemical compound (an *expression*) is given a unique **barcode number (value number)**.
- If two compounds ever get the same barcode, the lab technician knows they are *the same chemical* — produced by the *same reaction*.
- The technician then **reuses the existing vial** rather than manufacturing a fresh one.

In a compiler:
- Every computed value receives a **value number**.
- If the same value number appears twice, the second computation is **redundant** — the earlier result is simply **reused** (Common Subexpression Eliminated).
- **Superlocal** value numbering is like a *department-wide* fingerprinting system inside one lab wing (the EBB) — multiple rooms (basic blocks) cooperate, but the system does not yet extend to the whole building (entire function).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Think **L**ocal = **L**ab (1 room), **S**uperlocal = **S**ection (multiple rooms, one common door), **G**lobal = **G**round floor (whole building).

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Value Number (VN)** | A small integer abstractly identifying a value | Tracked in a **VN Table** |
| **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** | Set of basic blocks sharing a single entry | The unit of SVN |
| **Hash Function** $h(\text{op}, v_1, v_2)$ | Maps a rhs into a bucket in the table | Enables $O(1)$ lookup |
| **Scope-Restricted CSE** | CSE confined to an EBB | Safer than global CSE |
| **VN Numbering Instruction** | An instruction whose result is given a VN | Heart of the algorithm |
| **Copy Instruction** | $x = y$ — reuses y's VN for x | Eliminates trivial copies |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Superlocal Value Numbering Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The classic algorithm (Briggs, Cooper & Torczon) operates on an **Extended Basic Block** which is processed in **reverse postorder** (so that a block is always processed *after* all its predecessors within the EBB).

### 2.1.1 Data Structures

1. **VN Table (VNT):** A hash table mapping each distinct *right-hand-side expression* to a value number.

   $$ \text{VNT} : \text{Expr} \rightarrow \mathbb{N} $$

2. **Symbol Table (SYM):** A mapping from variable names to the value number of the variable's *current* contents.

   $$ \text{SYM} : \text{VarName} \rightarrow \mathbb{N} $$

3. **Constant Table (CONST):** A small associative table mapping constant literals to value numbers (so that `2` and `2.0` and the literal `2` always fold to the same VN).

4. **Work-List (WL):** A LIFO/Push-Down stack used to process blocks in the EBB.

### 2.1.2 The Algorithm in Pseudocode

For each **extended basic block** $E$ rooted at header $h$:

```
SVN(EBB E):
  Initialize VNT, SYM, CONST to empty.
  Push(h) on Work-List.
  While Work-List not empty:
    B = Pop(Work-List)
    For each predecessor P of B inside E:
        Merge(VNT, P.VNT)         // union of dominator-chain VNTs
        For each variable v redefined in P:
            Delete v from SYM
    For each instruction i in B (top-to-bottom):
        Apply VN rules to i (see below)
  End-While
```

### 2.1.3 The Five Value-Numbering Rules (per instruction)

Let the instruction be of the form `x = y op z` (binary), `x = op y` (unary), or `x = y` (copy).

| Rule # | Trigger | Action | Rationale |
|:------:|---------|--------|-----------|
| **1** | `x = y` (copy) | `VN(x) ← VN(y)` | Pure renaming, no new value |
| **2** | `x = c` (constant) | `VN(x) ← VN(c)` from CONST | Constant folding pre-step |
| **3** | `x = y op z` and `VN(y), VN(z)` *both already in SYM* | If $(op, VN(y), VN(z)) \in \text{VNT}$, then $x \leftarrow$ that earlier temp; else assign a new VN, store in VNT | Core CSE check |
| **4** | An operand is missing from SYM | Treat as a fresh symbolic value; assign new VN | Defensive — operands that flow from outside the EBB are considered fresh |
| **5** | Redefinition of a name (`x = ...` when `x` already in SYM) | **Delete** $x$ from SYM before processing rhs | Old VN of $x$ is killed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 5 is the most commonly missed rule in KTU exams.** A variable that is *killed* by a new definition loses its old value number — failing to delete it would cause an *incorrect* CSE across a real redefinition (a *value-number aliasing bug*).

### 2.1.4 The Merge Operation (Critical for SVN)

When a block has $k$ predecessors inside the EBB, the VN tables of the *immediate* predecessors (those on the dominator frontier of the block) are **unioned** into a fresh VNT:

$$ \text{VNT}(B) = \bigcup_{P \in \text{preds}(B) \cap E} \text{VNT}(P) \Big|_{\text{killed vars removed}} $$

In the union, if the *same expression* is mapped to the *same VN* in multiple predecessors, that mapping survives; if the same expression maps to *different* VNs (because the predecessors disagree about its value), the expression is **invalidated** and removed from the merged table.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why must we union, not just overwrite?**
> Because two different control-flow paths into block $B$ may have computed *different* values for the same expression. The merge step is what makes SVN *safe* — it never replaces an expression with a value that is not guaranteed to flow into the use point.

### 2.2 Why Superlocal and Not Just Local?

- **Strength of LVN:** Trivially fast ($O(n)$ per block), always correct.
- **Weakness of LVN:** Misses CSEs that span across block boundaries (e.g., a computation at the end of one basic block and a use at the start of a successor).
- **Strength of SVN:** Catches those *cross-block* CSEs in the same EBB, while keeping the analysis confined to a structured region.
- **Weakness of SVN:** Does not catch CSEs that need to traverse loops or join at points outside the EBB.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used in the **GCC** and **LLVM** optimization pipelines (in the form of *GVN — Global Value Numbering*, which generalizes SVN to the whole function with hashing and RPO traversal).
- In **embedded compilers** (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V) superlocal-style VN is preferred because it is *deterministic* in code-size — a key KTU viva question.
- Forms the theoretical basis of *Sparse Conditional Constant Propagation* and *Redundant Load/Store Elimination*.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Definition | Constraint / Unit |
|:-:|---------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hash key for $x = y \text{ op } z$ | $k = h(\text{op}, \text{VN}(y), \text{VN}(z))$ | $h: \{op\} \times \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ |
| 2 | CSE Detection Predicate | $\exists \, (op, v_1, v_2) \in \text{VNT} : v_1 = \text{VN}(y) \land v_2 = \text{VN}(z)$ | Boolean |
| 3 | Block VNT (after merge) | $\text{VNT}(B) = \bigcup_{P \in \text{preds}(B)} \text{VNT}(P) \setminus K_P$ | $K_P$ = killed set |
| 4 | Constant folding | $\text{VN}(c) = \text{CONST}(c)$ | $c \in \mathbb{Z} \cup \mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Copy rule | $\text{VN}(x) \leftarrow \text{VN}(y)$ for $x = y$ | Renaming only |
| 6 | Kill rule | $\text{SYM} \leftarrow \text{SYM} \setminus \{x\}$ when $x$ redefined | Defensive |
| 7 | Number of VNs in a block | $\vert \text{VNT} \vert \leq n$ where $n$ is # instructions in block | Finite |
| 8 | Time complexity (per EBB) | $O(E \cdot \alpha)$ using union-find with path compression | $E$ = # edges in EBB |
| 9 | Space complexity | $O(\vert \text{VNT} \vert + \vert \text{SYM} \vert)$ | Hash tables |
| 10 | Postorder Traversal | $\text{DFS}(B)$ processed after all descendants in dominator tree | RPO order |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Notice that the hash function $h$ uses *value numbers of the operands*, not the operand names. This is the central insight that makes value numbering work — *names* can be re-bound, but *value numbers* represent mathematical equivalence classes of computed quantities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Superlocal Value Numbering on an EBB

### 3.1.1 The Source Program (Three-address code)

Consider the following C-like code fragment, which forms an **Extended Basic Block** (header = $B_0$, three branches into leaves $B_1, B_2, B_3$):

```c
// Block B0 (header)
a = 1;
b = 2;
c = a + b;          // c = 3

if (x) goto L1 else goto L2;

// Block B1
d = c + a;          // d = 4     (since a=1, c=3)
e = a + b;          // e = 3     (= c, CSE candidate)
f = d + e;          // f = 7
goto L9;

// Block B2
g = a + b;          // g = 3     (yet another copy of a+b)
h = c + a;          // h = 4     (= d, CSE candidate)
i = g + h;          // i = 7
goto L9;

// Block B3
j = a + b;          // j = 3
k = c * a;          // k = 3
goto L9;

L9: return;
```

The EBB has **header $B_0$** and **three exit blocks $B_1, B_2, B_3$** — a classic diamond+tail structure.

### 3.1.2 Trace Table — Superlocal VN over the EBB

We number the VNs as they are first encountered. The working state after **each** instruction is shown.

| Step | Block | Instruction | Rule Used | VNT State (Expression → VN) | SYM State (Var → VN) | CSE Found? |
|:----:|:-----:|:-----------:|:---------:|:---------------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------:|
| 0 | start | *(init)* | — | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 1 | $B_0$ | `a = 1` | 2 | $\{1 \to 1\}$ | $\{a \to 1\}$ | — |
| 2 | $B_0$ | `b = 2` | 2 | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2\}$ | $\{a \to 1,\; b \to 2\}$ | — |
| 3 | $B_0$ | `c = a + b` | 3 (new) | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3\}$ | $\{a \to 1,\; b \to 2,\; c \to 3\}$ | — |
| 4 | $B_1$ | `d = c + a` | 3 (new) | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3,\; (+,3,1) \to 4\}$ | $\{c \to 3,\; a \to 1,\; d \to 4\}$ | — |
| 5 | $B_1$ | `e = a + b` | 3 (HIT!) | unchanged | $\{e \to 3\}$ (reuse $c$'s VN) | **Yes** → `e = c` |
| 6 | $B_1$ | `f = d + e` | 3 (new) | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3,\; (+,3,1) \to 4,\; (+,4,3) \to 5\}$ | $\{d \to 4,\; e \to 3,\; f \to 5\}$ | — |
| 7 | $B_2$ | `g = a + b` | 3 (HIT!) | unchanged | $\{g \to 3\}$ | **Yes** → `g = c` |
| 8 | $B_2$ | `h = c + a` | 3 (HIT!) | unchanged | $\{g \to 3,\; h \to 4\}$ (reuses $d$'s VN) | **Yes** → `h = d` |
| 9 | $B_2$ | `i = g + h` | 3 (new) | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3,\; (+,3,1) \to 4,\; (+,4,3) \to 5,\; (+,3,4) \to 6\}$ | $\{i \to 6\}$ | — |
| 10 | $B_3$ | `j = a + b` | 3 (HIT!) | unchanged | $\{j \to 3\}$ | **Yes** → `j = c` |
| 11 | $B_3$ | `k = c * a` | 3 (new) | $\{1 \to 1,\; 2 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3,\; (+,3,1) \to 4,\; (+,4,3) \to 5,\; (+,3,4) \to 6,\; (*,3,1) \to 7\}$ | $\{k \to 7\}$ | — |

### 3.1.3 The Merge Step (When Two Predecessors Feed a Block)

Now suppose a new block $B_4$ is reached by both $B_1$ and $B_2$ (a *join* point inside the EBB):

```c
// Block B4
m = d + e;          // in path from B1
n = g + h;          // in path from B2
```

The merge of $\text{VNT}(B_1)$ and $\text{VNT}(B_2)$ at the head of $B_4$ is performed as:

$$
\text{VNT}_{\text{merge}}(B_4) = \text{VNT}(B_1) \,\cup\, \text{VNT}(B_2) \,\setminus\, \text{conflicts}
$$

Concretely:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VNT}(B_1) &= \{1 \mapsto 1,\; 2 \mapsto 2,\; (+,1,2) \mapsto 3,\; (+,3,1) \mapsto 4,\; (+,4,3) \mapsto 5\} \\[4pt]
\text{VNT}(B_2) &= \{1 \mapsto 1,\; 2 \mapsto 2,\; (+,1,2) \mapsto 3,\; (+,3,1) \mapsto 4,\; (+,4,3) \mapsto 5,\; (+,3,4) \mapsto 6\} \\[4pt]
\text{VNT}_{\text{merge}}(B_4) &= \big\{ 1 \mapsto 1,\; 2 \mapsto 2,\; (+,1,2) \mapsto 3,\; (+,3,1) \mapsto 4,\; (+,4,3) \mapsto 5 \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The expression $(+,3,4) \mapsto 6$ survives the merge because $B_1$ never computed it; we *additively* include it. Conflicts — entries where both parents agree on the expression but *disagree* on the VN — are *removed*; in this example, no conflicts exist.)

### 3.1.4 Algorithmic Correctness Argument (Sketch Proof)

The algorithm is correct because it maintains the following **invariant** at every point:

> **Invariant (I):** At any point $p$ in a block, for every variable $v$ in $\text{SYM}(p)$, $\text{VN}(v)$ is the value number of the *most recent* definition of $v$ that *reaches* $p$ along the path from the EBB header to $p$.

*Proof Sketch (by induction on instruction index):*
- **Base case:** At the start of the EBB header, $\text{SYM} = \emptyset$ and $\text{VNT} = \emptyset$. The invariant is trivially satisfied (no values have been computed).
- **Inductive step — Rule 3:** When processing `x = y op z`, both $\text{VN}(y)$ and $\text{VN}(z)$ are, by the inductive hypothesis, the *most recent reaching values*. Hence any earlier computation in the EBB that produced the *same* value is necessarily in $\text{VNT}$ with the *same* $(op, v_1, v_2)$ tuple — making the CSE safe.
- **Inductive step — Rule 5:** When $x$ is redefined, the previous binding of $x$ in $\text{SYM}$ is *killed*. Any subsequent use of $x$ therefore receives the *new* VN, satisfying the invariant for the post-definition region.
- **Merge step:** The union takes the *intersection* of guarantees from all predecessors. If both paths agree an expression equals VN $k$, then it equals $k$ on the joined path. If they *disagree*, the expression is conservatively invalidated. ∎

### 3.1.5 Complexity Derivation

For an EBB with $B$ blocks and a total of $N$ instructions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{hash per instruction}} &= O(1) \quad (\text{amortized with good hash}) \\
T_{\text{total}} &= O(N) \quad \text{(one pass per instruction)} \\
T_{\text{merge per join}} &= O(\vert \text{VNT}_1 \vert + \vert \text{VNT}_2 \vert) \quad \text{(union of two hash tables)} \\
T_{\text{total over EBB}} &= O\!\left(N + \sum_{\text{joins}} \vert \text{VNT}_i \vert\right) \leq O(N \cdot B) \\
S_{\text{space}} &= O(\vert \text{VNT} \vert_{\max}) \leq O(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favourite Result:** SVN runs in *linear* time per EBB in the common case. This is the answer to the typical "what is the complexity of SVN?" 3-mark question.

### 3.2 Comparison Matrix: LVN vs SVN vs GVN (Frequent Exam Question)

| Property | Local VN | Superlocal VN | Global VN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Region | Single basic block | Extended basic block | Entire function |
| Handles loops | No | No (if not header of EBB) | Yes |
| Cross-block CSE | No | Yes (within EBB) | Yes |
| Complexity | $O(n)$ per block | $O(n + m)$ per EBB | $O(n \log n)$ amortized |
| Memory | Constant per block | Proportional to EBB size | Proportional to function size |
| Determinism | High | High | Lower (due to phi-functions) |
| Standard Reference | Aho-Sethi-Ullman | Briggs-Cooper-Torczon 1994 | Alpern-Wegman-Zadeck 1988 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Superlocal Value Numbering Pass

The diagram below shows the **modular flow** of the SVN pass inside a typical KTU-modeled compiler. Each block is a logical unit; arrows show data flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source IR in SSA or 3 Address Code] --> B[Identify Extended Basic Block EBB using Dominator Tree]
    B --> C[Initialize VNT SYM CONST to Empty]
    C --> D[Push Header Block onto WorkList]
    D --> E{WorkList Empty}
    E -- No --> F[Pop Block B from WorkList]
    F --> G{B has predecessor inside EBB}
    G -- Yes --> H[Merge VNT of predecessors]
    H --> I[Delete killed variables from SYM]
    G -- No --> I
    I --> J[Process next instruction i in B]
    J --> K{Instruction type}
    K -- Copy x equals y --> L[Rule 1 SYM x gets SYM y]
    K -- Constant x equals c --> M[Rule 2 SYM x gets CONST c]
    K -- Binary x equals y op z --> N[Rule 3 Hash key h op VN y VN z]
    N --> O{Key present in VNT}
    O -- Yes --> P[Apply CSE x gets previous temp]
    O -- No --> Q[Allocate new VN store in VNT]
    L --> R{Variable x being redefined}
    M --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R -- Yes --> S[Delete x from SYM kill step]
    R -- No --> T{More instructions in B}
    S --> T
    T -- Yes --> J
    T -- No --> U[Push successor blocks inside EBB onto WorkList]
    U --> E
    E -- Yes --> V[Emit Optimized 3 Address Code with replacements]
    V --> W[Next Pass in Compiler Pipeline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The merge step (block H) is what makes this algorithm **superlocal** rather than local. A purely local VN pass would skip H entirely and reset VNT at every block boundary.

## 4.2 Data-Flow Diagram — VNT and SYM Evolution During Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HeaderBlock[Header Block B0]
        H1[a equals 1] --> H2[b equals 2]
        H2 --> H3[c equals a plus b]
    end
    subgraph B1[Block B1]
        L1[d equals c plus a] --> L2[e equals a plus b CSE hit]
        L2 --> L3[f equals d plus e]
    end
    subgraph B2[Block B2]
        M1[g equals a plus b CSE hit] --> M2[h equals c plus a CSE hit]
        M2 --> M3[i equals g plus h]
    end
    subgraph B3[Block B3]
        N1[j equals a plus b CSE hit] --> N2[k equals c times a new VN]
    end
    H3 --> L1
    H3 --> M1
    H3 --> N1
    L3 --> J[Join Point L9]
    M3 --> J
    N2 --> J
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison — Local vs Superlocal Scope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Value Numbering Variants] --> B[Local VN scope single BB]
    A --> C[Superlocal VN scope EBB]
    A --> D[Global VN scope whole function]
    B --> B1[Pros O n simple safe]
    B --> B2[Cons no cross block CSE]
    C --> C1[Pros catches cross block CSE linear in EBB]
    C --> C2[Cons no loop handling]
    D --> D1[Pros handles loops and merges]
    D --> D2[Cons needs SSA and phi handling]
```
