# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Value Numbering. How is it different from Local Value Numbering?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCST601 Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Value Numbering (VN):** A compiler optimization technique that assigns a unique abstract integer (a *value number*) to each distinct value computed in a program. Two expressions that compute the same value receive the *same* value number, enabling **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** and **Constant Folding**.

**Local Value Numbering (LVN):** A restricted form of value numbering that operates *only within a single basic block*. It scans the instructions of one block in linear order, maintaining a small hash table (VNT) of expressions seen so far. Whenever an expression is repeated, it is replaced by the earlier temporary.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Value Numbering | Local Value Numbering |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Any region (block, EBB, function) | Single basic block |
| Cross-block CSE | Possible (in SVN/GVN) | Not possible |
| Complexity | $O(N)$ to $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ always |
| Tables | VNT + SYM (per region) | VNT + SYM (per block, reset) |

---

### Q2. What is an Extended Basic Block? Why is it central to Superlocal Value Numbering?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCCST601 Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** is a maximal set of basic blocks in a control-flow graph that share a *single common entry block* (the *header*) and have a tree-like (acyclic) internal control-flow structure with one or more *exit* blocks. The header dominates every block in the EBB.

**Centrality to SVN:** SVN requires a structured region where each block (except the header) has a *single* predecessor *inside* the EBB. This guarantees:
1. A simple merge step (union of value tables along the dominator chain).
2. Linear-time processing in reverse postorder.
3. Soundness — the header's value environment flows unambiguously into every descendant block.

Without the EBB structure, the merge step becomes the complex *phi-function* problem of global value numbering.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCST601, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the algorithm for **Superlocal Value Numbering** with the data structures used. Discuss the role of the **hash function** and the **merge step**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Consider the following three-address code forming an extended basic block. Apply Superlocal Value Numbering and detect all **Common Subexpressions**. Show the optimized code. **(7 marks)**

```c
// Block B0
x = 2;
y = 3;
t1 = x + y;
t2 = y * x;
if (p) goto B1 else goto B2;

// Block B1
t3 = x + y;        // same as t1
t4 = t1 + t2;
t5 = x + y;        // same as t1
t6 = t3 * t2;
goto L;

// Block B2
t7 = y * x;        // same as t2
t8 = t1 + t2;      // same as t4
t9 = x + y;        // same as t1
goto L;
```

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Data Structures (1 mark):** VNT (hash table mapping expression → VN), SYM (variable → VN), CONST (literal → VN), Work-List (stack of blocks in the EBB).

2. **Algorithm (3 marks):**
   - Identify the EBB (header + descendants in the dominator tree).
   - Initialize all tables to empty.
   - Process the EBB in *reverse postorder* (header first, leaves last).
   - At each join point, **merge** the VNTs of all in-EOB predecessors via set union (with conflict resolution).
   - At each instruction, apply the **five VN rules** (copy, constant, binary with lookup, missing operand, kill).
   - When a key $h(op, VN(y), VN(z))$ is *already* in VNT, perform CSE — replace the rhs with the earlier temp.

3. **Role of the hash function (1 mark):** The hash key is $h(op, VN(y), VN(z))$. It uses the *value numbers* of the operands (not their names) so that expressions like `a+b` and `c+d` can be recognized as identical when $a$ and $c$ hold the *same value* (i.e., share the same VN).

4. **Role of the merge step (1 mark):** Combines the VNTs of multiple predecessors at a join point. Keeps expressions agreed upon by *all* predecessors; *invalidates* expressions with conflicting VNs. This is what makes SVN safe across control flow.

5. **Complexity (1 mark):** $O(N)$ per EBB in the linear case; $O(N \cdot B)$ worst case where $B$ is the number of blocks in the EBB.

**Part (b) — Worked Example (7 marks):**

*Initialize:* VNT = $\emptyset$, SYM = $\emptyset$, CONST = $\emptyset$.

| Step | Block | Instruction | Rule | VNT Updated | SYM Updated | CSE? |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $B_0$ | `x = 2` | 2 | $\{2 \to 1\}$ | $\{x \to 1\}$ | — |
| 2 | $B_0$ | `y = 3` | 2 | $\{2 \to 1,\; 3 \to 2\}$ | $\{x \to 1,\; y \to 2\}$ | — |
| 3 | $B_0$ | `t1 = x + y` | 3 new | $\{2 \to 1,\; 3 \to 2,\; (+,1,2) \to 3\}$ | $\{t1 \to 3\}$ | — |
| 4 | $B_0$ | `t2 = y * x` | 3 new | $\{..., (*,2,1) \to 4\}$ | $\{t1 \to 3,\; t2 \to 4\}$ | — |
| 5 | $B_1$ | `t3 = x + y` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t3 \to 3\}$ | **Yes** → `t3 = t1` |
| 6 | $B_1$ | `t4 = t1 + t2` | 3 new | $\{..., (+,3,4) \to 5\}$ | $\{t4 \to 5\}$ | — |
| 7 | $B_1$ | `t5 = x + y` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t5 \to 3\}$ (replaces `t5`) | **Yes** → `t5 = t1` |
| 8 | $B_1$ | `t6 = t3 * t2` | 3 new | $\{..., (*,3,4) \to 6\}$ | $\{t6 \to 6\}$ | — |
| 9 | $B_2$ | `t7 = y * x` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t7 \to 4\}$ | **Yes** → `t7 = t2` |
| 10 | $B_2$ | `t8 = t1 + t2` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t8 \to 5\}$ | **Yes** → `t8 = t4` |
| 11 | $B_2$ | `t9 = x + y` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t9 \to 3\}$ | **Yes** → `t9 = t1` |

**Optimized Code (after SVN):**

```c
// Block B0 (header) — unchanged
x = 2;
y = 3;
t1 = x + y;
t2 = y * x;

// Block B1 — three CSE replacements
t3 = t1;           // CSE
t4 = t1 + t2;
t5 = t1;           // CSE
t6 = t3 * t2;      // = t1 * t2

// Block B2 — three CSE replacements
t7 = t2;           // CSE
t8 = t4;           // CSE
t9 = t1;           // CSE
```

**CSE Eliminated: 6 redundant computations** (3 in $B_1$, 3 in $B_2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):** Students often forget to **delete the redefined variable from SYM** (Rule 5). If a variable `x` is assigned twice in the same block, the second assignment *must* invalidate `x`'s previous VN entry. Skipping this causes an illegal CSE across a real redefinition and costs up to 2 marks. Also, examiners deduct 1 mark if the student writes CSE replacements as `t3 = x + y` *with the new expression* instead of writing `t3 = t1`.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCST601, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of an **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **dominance property** and how it enables safe value numbering across blocks. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For the following EBB, perform **Superlocal Value Numbering** and write the optimized code. Identify all CSEs and constant-folding opportunities. **(7 marks)**

```c
// Block B0
p = 4;
q = 5;
r = p * q;
s = p + q;

// Block B1
t = p + q;         // same as s
u = r * 2;
v = p * q;         // same as r
w = t + u;

// Block B2
x = r * 2;         // same as u
y = p + q;         // same as s
z = y + x;
```

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Extended Basic Block (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** An EBB is a set of basic blocks $E = \{B_0, B_1, \ldots, B_k\}$ such that (i) they share a single *header* $B_0$, (ii) every block $B_i$ ($i > 0$) has *exactly one* predecessor inside $E$, and (iii) the subgraph induced by $E$ is a directed tree rooted at $B_0$.

2. **Diagram (2 marks):** A dominator tree fragment — header $B_0$ branching to $B_1, B_2, B_3$ with arrows showing tree edges; exits shown dangling.

3. **Dominance property (2 marks):** $B_0$ dominates every block in $E$. Any definition in $B_0$ is guaranteed to reach every descendant block. This guarantees that a value computed in the header can be safely reused in any successor — the cornerstone of SVN's correctness.

4. **Why EBB is essential (1 mark):** It restricts the analysis to a structured region where the merge step is a simple set union — no phi-functions, no iterative fixed-point, no convergence loop.

**Part (b) — Worked Example (7 marks):**

| Step | Block | Instruction | Rule | VNT | SYM | CSE? |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $B_0$ | `p = 4` | 2 | $\{4 \to 1\}$ | $\{p \to 1\}$ | — |
| 2 | $B_0$ | `q = 5` | 2 | $\{4 \to 1,\; 5 \to 2\}$ | $\{p \to 1,\; q \to 2\}$ | — |
| 3 | $B_0$ | `r = p * q` | 3 new | $\{..., (*,1,2) \to 3\}$ | $\{r \to 3\}$ | — |
| 4 | $B_0$ | `s = p + q` | 3 new | $\{..., (*,1,2) \to 3,\; (+,1,2) \to 4\}$ | $\{s \to 4\}$ | — |
| 5 | $B_1$ | `t = p + q` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{t \to 4\}$ | **Yes** → `t = s` |
| 6 | $B_1$ | `u = r * 2` | 3 new | $\{..., (*,3,1) \to 5\}$ (since $2 \to 1$) | $\{u \to 5\}$ | — |
| 7 | $B_1$ | `v = p * q` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{v \to 3\}$ | **Yes** → `v = r` |
| 8 | $B_1$ | `w = t + u` | 3 new | $\{..., (+,4,5) \to 6\}$ | $\{w \to 6\}$ | — |
| 9 | $B_2$ | `x = r * 2` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{x \to 5\}$ | **Yes** → `x = u` |
| 10 | $B_2$ | `y = p + q` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{y \to 4\}$ | **Yes** → `y = s` |
| 11 | $B_2$ | `z = y + x` | 3 HIT | unchanged | $\{z \to 6\}$ | **Yes** → `z = w` |

**Constant Folding Observation (bonus 1 mark):** `p * q` and `p + q` could also be constant-folded because `p = 4`, `q = 5` are constants. After folding, `r = 20`, `s = 9`. However, classic SVN does *not* perform constant propagation across blocks; only a separate constant-propagation pass (or a GVN-with-SCC pass) achieves that.

**Optimized Code:**

```c
// Block B0 (unchanged)
p = 4;
q = 5;
r = p * q;
s = p + q;

// Block B1
t = s;          // CSE
u = r * 2;
v = r;          // CSE
w = t + u;

// Block B2
x = u;          // CSE
y = s;          // CSE
z = w;          // CSE
```

**Total CSEs Eliminated: 6** (3 in $B_1$, 3 in $B_2$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B):**
> (i) Students frequently *forget the kill rule* when `p` or `q` is redefined inside the EBB — losing 1 mark.
> (ii) Some students incorrectly apply constant folding *inside* SVN — note that SVN's constant-folding is restricted to *literal constants on the RHS*, not propagated constants across blocks. Mixing up these two passes loses 1 mark.
> (iii) Marks are deducted if the optimized code retains the redundant RHS expression (e.g., writing `t = p + q` instead of `t = s`).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Value Numbering = assigning abstract integers to values; SVN = value numbering over an Extended Basic Block.
- [ ] **EBB:** A set of basic blocks sharing a single header (one entry, possibly many exits), forming a tree in the dominator tree.
- [ ] **Three core data structures:** VNT (expression → VN), SYM (variable → VN), CONST (literal → VN).
- [ ] **Hash key formula:** $h(op, VN(y), VN(z))$ — uses value numbers, *not* variable names.
- [ ] **Five processing rules:** Copy, Constant, Binary (with CSE check), Missing operand (treat as fresh), Kill (delete from SYM on redefinition).
- [ ] **CSE detection:** Hash key already present in VNT → replace RHS with the earlier temporary.
- [ ] **Merge step at joins:** Union of predecessor VNTs; remove conflicting entries.
- [ ] **Processing order:** Reverse Postorder (RPO) of the EBB dominator tree.
- [ ] **Time complexity:** $O(N)$ per EBB in the common case.
- [ ] **Space complexity:** $O(\vert \text{VNT} \vert) \leq O(N)$.
- [ ] **Limitations:** Does *not* handle loops that do *not* enter via the EBB header; does *not* perform inter-EBB CSE; does *not* propagate constants across blocks.
- [ ] **Classic Reference:** Briggs, Cooper & Torczon, *"Practical improvements to the construction and destruction of static single assignment form"*, Software — Practice & Experience, 1998 (the SVN algorithm is the *predecessor* to GVN).
- [ ] **Comparison hooks for viva:**
  - LVN ⟶ SVN ⟶ GVN (scales from one block to whole function).
  - SVN is the *deterministic, fast, EBB-scoped* middle option — preferred in embedded compilers.

> [!TIP]
> **Two-Second Recall Mnemonic:** **"CSE = Same Hash, Same Number."** If `h(op, VN(y), VN(z))` is already in VNT, you have a Common Sub-Expression and you reuse the earlier temp.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loop Unrolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loop Unrolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Loop Unrolling** is a well-known **loop transformation optimization** technique employed by compilers (and sometimes by assembly programmers) in which the body of a loop is **replicated multiple times** within a single iteration of the transformed loop, with the intention of reducing the frequency of branch instructions executed, decreasing the relative cost of the loop's overhead, and exposing more **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** to the underlying hardware pipeline.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, loop unrolling is categorized under the umbrella of **Local and Global Optimizations** (Module 4) and forms a foundational building block for more advanced transformations such as **loop pipelining**, **software pipelining**, and **vectorization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Tag (KTU PCCST601 Module 4):** *Local and Global Optimizations — Loop Optimization techniques: Loop Unrolling, Loop Jamming, Loop Fission, Loop Peeling, and Loop Interchange.*

### 1.2 The Unroll Factor (k)

The **unroll factor** $k$ is the integer constant that specifies how many copies of the original loop body are fused into one iteration of the new loop.

$$k \ge 1, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$

* If $k = 1$, no unrolling occurs (identity transformation).
* If $k$ equals the **trip count** $N$ of the loop, the result is **complete (full) unrolling**, leaving zero branches.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Assembly Line"

Imagine a factory with **4 workers** and a **supervisor** who, at the end of every 1 hour, must ring a bell to signal "move to the next item." The bell-ringing is a tiny cost in itself, but over a **40-hour week**, the supervisor has rung the bell 40 times for *each worker*, totaling 160 rings.

**Loop Unrolling**, in plain English, is like combining the work of 4 workers into 1 super-worker who handles 4 items in one go — but the supervisor only rings the bell **once for the 4 items**. The "ringing of the bell" represents the **branch-and-jump overhead** of a loop; unrolling amortizes that overhead across more useful work.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Definition

* **Trip Count (N):** The total number of original iterations.
* **Unroll Factor (k):** Number of body copies merged per new iteration.
* **New Trip Count:** $N' = \lceil N / k \rceil$.
* **Code-Size Expansion Factor:** Approximately $k \times$ the original body size.
* **Branch Reduction Factor:** Branch count is divided by $k$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do Compilers Unroll Loops? — The Operational Motivation

Loop unrolling is fundamentally a **trade-off optimization**. The compiler accepts a higher memory footprint in exchange for:

1. **Reduced Branch Penalty** — Fewer back-edges to the loop header, fewer mispredicted branches.
2. **Increased Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)** — More independent operations per cycle become visible to the scheduler, allowing superscalar/VLIW pipelines to fill more issue slots.
3. **Better Register Allocation** — With multiple instances of the same operation, the allocator can schedule loads and stores further apart, hiding memory latency.
4. **Improved Prefetching Behavior** — Memory streams are detected and prefetched more aggressively by the hardware.

### 2.2 The Three Variants of Loop Unrolling

| Variant | Description | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Unrolling** | The entire loop is replaced by straight-line code. Branch is removed entirely. | When $N$ is a compile-time constant and small. |
| **Partial (Static) Unrolling** | The body is replicated $k$ times; the residual $(N \bmod k)$ iterations are peeled off into a prologue/epilogue. | Most common case in production compilers. |
| **Dynamic Unrolling** | The decision of how much to unroll is deferred using runtime tests on the trip count. | When $N$ is not statically known. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Unrolling** assumes the compiler can resolve the trip count at compile time. **Dynamic Unrolling** handles variable trip counts by emitting guarded residue handling blocks.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N$ | Original trip count | iterations (compile-time constant or symbolic) |
| $k$ | Unroll factor | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+, k \ge 2$ |
| $N' = \lceil N/k \rceil$ | New trip count | iterations |
| $r = N \bmod k$ | Residual iterations (peel) | $0 \le r < k$ |
| $B' = \lfloor N/k \rfloor$ | Branches saved | dimensionless |
| $S_{\text{exp}} = k$ | Code-size expansion factor | dimensionless |
| $T_{\text{orig}} = N \cdot (t_{\text{body}} + t_{\text{branch}})$ | Original loop time | seconds/cycles |
| $T_{\text{unrolled}} \approx (N/k) \cdot (k \cdot t_{\text{body}} + t_{\text{branch}})$ | Unrolled loop time | seconds/cycles |
| $\text{Speedup} \le k$ | Theoretical max speedup | bounded by $k$ |

> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** For absolute-value expressions inside markdown tables, we use the LaTeX command `\vert` instead of the keyboard pipe `|` to prevent breaking the table parser — e.g., $\vert N - k \cdot N' \vert$ rather than $\lvert N - k \cdot N' \rvert$ typed as raw pipes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **DSP & Embedded Kernels:** FIR/IIR filters and FFT butterflies are routinely unrolled by hand or by `gcc -funroll-loops`.
* **GPU Shaders:** HLSL/GLSL compilers unroll small loops (e.g., matrix multiply) to expose SIMD lanes.
* **JIT Compilers:** HotSpot (Java), LuaJIT, and V8 use tiered unrolling at higher optimization levels.
* **Scientific Computing:** BLAS routines (e.g., DGEMM) unroll $4\times$ or $8\times$ to maximize FMA throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full Unrolling of a Constant-Bound Loop

Consider the following high-level loop (assuming $N = 4$ is known at compile time):

**Original Loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < 4; i = i + 1) \\
&\qquad a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Symbolic expansion** of each iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i=0 &: \quad a[0] = b[0] + c[0]; \\
i=1 &: \quad a[1] = b[1] + c[1]; \\
i=2 &: \quad a[2] = b[2] + c[2]; \\
i=3 &: \quad a[3] = b[3] + c[3];
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reassemble as straight-line code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a[0] &= b[0] + c[0]; \\
a[1] &= b[1] + c[1]; \\
a[2] &= b[2] + c[2]; \\
a[3] &= b[3] + c[3];
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The branch instruction at the loop header is **completely eliminated**, and four independent additions can now be scheduled in parallel by a superscalar pipeline.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Partial Unrolling with $k = 2$, $N$ Symbolic

**Original Loop:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < N; i = i + 1) \\
&\qquad a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Choose the unroll factor.** Let $k = 2$.

**Step 2 — Compute residual.** $r = N \bmod 2$.

**Step 3 — Peel the residue into a prologue** (handles the case $N$ is odd):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } (N \bmod 2 = 1) \quad a[N-1] = b[N-1] + c[N-1]; \\
&N_{\text{new}} = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor ;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Replace the loop body with two fused copies** (the "new body"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < N_{\text{new}}; i = i + 1) \\
&\qquad a[2i]   = b[2i]   + c[2i]; \\
&\qquad a[2i+1] = b[2i+1] + c[2i+1];
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Count the savings.** Original branch count = $N+1$. New branch count = $N_{\text{new}}+1 = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$. The branch reduction is $\lceil N/2 \rceil$ — a saving of nearly **50%**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Execution Cost

We model the per-iteration cost as $t_{\text{body}} + t_{\text{branch}}$, where $t_{\text{branch}}$ includes the compare, conditional jump, and pipeline flush on misprediction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{orig}}  &= N \cdot (t_{\text{body}} + t_{\text{branch}}) \\
T_{\text{unrolled}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{N}{k} \right\rfloor \cdot (k \cdot t_{\text{body}} + t_{\text{branch}}) + r \cdot t_{\text{body}} \\
T_{\text{orig}} - T_{\text{unrolled}} &= N \cdot t_{\text{branch}} - \left\lfloor \frac{N}{k} \right\rfloor \cdot t_{\text{branch}} - \text{residue penalty}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The unrolled version saves approximately $t_{\text{branch}} \cdot (N - N/k) = t_{\text{branch}} \cdot N \cdot (1 - 1/k)$ cycles of branch overhead.

### 3.4 Critical Pitfalls of Aggressive Unrolling

1. **I-Cache Pollution** — The instruction cache may thrash if the unrolled code exceeds the cache line budget.
2. **Register Pressure** — More live values compete for the limited register file; the allocator may spill to memory, *erasing* all gains.
3. **Loss of Vectorization** — Some auto-vectorizers refuse to vectorize already-unrolled loops. Best practice: vectorize first, then unroll lightly.
4. **Code Bloat for Branch Predictors** — Modern CPUs with very good BPTs (branch target predictors) already predict the back-edge with $\ge 95\%$ accuracy, eroding the marginal benefit of unrolling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compiler Pipeline Position of Loop Unrolling

The following Mermaid block depicts where loop unrolling fits within the broader KTU compiler backend. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `phase1`, `phaseA`) and labels contain **only plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no markdown bold, no embedded HTML tables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1[FRONT END] --> phase2[INTERMEDIATE REPRESENTATION IR]
    phase2 --> phase3[LOCAL OPTIMIZATION]
    phase3 --> phase4[LOOP OPTIMIZATION MODULE 4]
    phase4 --> phase5[LOOP UNROLLING]
    phase4 --> phase6[LOOP JAMMING]
    phase4 --> phase7[LOOP FISSION]
    phase4 --> phase8[LOOP INTERCHANGE]
    phase5 --> phase9[REGISTER ALLOCATION]
    phase6 --> phase9
    phase7 --> phase9
    phase8 --> phase9
    phase9 --> phase10[INSTRUCTION SCHEDULING]
    phase10 --> phase11[CODE GENERATION]
    phase11 --> phase12[TARGET ASSEMBLY]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Mechanics of a $k = 4$ Unroll

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[ORIGINAL LOOP HEADER] --> bodyA[BODY COPY 1]
    bodyA --> bodyB[BODY COPY 2]
    bodyB --> bodyC[BODY COPY 3]
    bodyC --> bodyD[BODY COPY 4]
    bodyD --> incrA[INCREMENT I BY K]
    incrA --> condA[BRANCH BACK TO HEADER]
    condA -- TRIP COUNT NOT EXHAUSTED --> startA
    condA -- EXIT --> endNode[LOOP EXIT]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cost vs Benefit Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[ORIGINAL LOOP] --> boxA[UNROLL BY K]
    boxA --> out1[REDUCED BRANCH OVERHEAD]
    boxA --> out2[INCREASED ILP]
    boxA --> out3[CODE SIZE EXPANSION]
    boxA --> out4[HIGHER REGISTER PRESSURE]
    out1 --> sumA[NET GAIN DOMINATES]
    out2 --> sumA
    out3 --> sumB[NET LOSS DOMINATES]
    out4 --> sumB
    sumA --> finalA[KEEP UNROLLING]
    sumB --> finalB[REVERT TO ORIGINAL LOOP]
```

> **Why this fallback design:** Loop unrolling is fundamentally a *physical* transformation of the IR. Because students may not always have a drawing tool at hand, the diagrams above render the **functional architecture** of the optimization — showing what feeds what — rather than attempting schematic hardware drawings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**  
*Define loop unrolling. Mention any two advantages and one disadvantage of the technique.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Loop unrolling is a loop transformation that replicates the body of a loop $k$ times, reducing the number of branch instructions executed.
> *Advantages:* (i) Reduced branch overhead, (ii) Increased instruction-level parallelism.
> *Disadvantage:* Increased code size, which may cause instruction cache misses. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]`**  
*What is the role of the unroll factor $k$ in loop unrolling? What happens when $k = N$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The unroll factor $k$ specifies the number of copies of the loop body merged into a single iteration of the new loop. When $k = N$ (the original trip count), the result is **complete (full) unrolling** — the entire loop is replaced by straight-line code with no branch instruction. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently *lose 2–3 marks* on loop-unrolling 14-markers by (a) drawing control-flow graphs inconsistently, (b) forgetting to peel the residual iterations when $N$ is not divisible by $k$, and (c) confusing the new trip count formula. Always write the residue-handling prologue **explicitly**, and clearly separate the *unrolled body* from the *peeled iteration block*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **loop unrolling** in detail. Discuss the unroll factor and describe **partial** and **full** unrolling with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Loop unrolling is a transformation that replicates the body of a loop $k$ times to reduce the number of branch-and-jump cycles and expose more instruction-level parallelism to the scheduler.

2. **Unroll factor (1 mark):** The integer $k$ specifies the number of body copies. The new trip count is $N' = \lceil N/k \rceil$, and the residual $r = N \bmod k$ iterations must be peeled off.

3. **Partial unrolling — example (3 marks):** For the loop

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i=0; i < N; i = i+1) \\
&\qquad a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
\end{aligned}
$$

with $k = 2$, the compiler emits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } (N \bmod 2 = 1) \; a[N-1] = b[N-1] + c[N-1]; \\
&\text{for } (i=0; i < \lfloor N/2 \rfloor; i = i+1) \; \{ \\
&\qquad a[2i]   = b[2i]   + c[2i]; \\
&\qquad a[2i+1] = b[2i+1] + c[2i+1]; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Full unrolling — example (2 marks):** If $N = 4$ is known at compile time, the loop is fully unrolled into four straight-line statements with **no branch**, allowing the scheduler to issue all four additions in parallel.

**(b)** Consider the following loop with $N = 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < 100; i = i+1) \\
&\qquad x[i] = y[i] + z[i];
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply **loop unrolling with $k = 4$** and write the resulting code. Compute (i) the new trip count, (ii) the residual, and (iii) the percentage reduction in the number of back-edge branches. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Residual computation (1 mark):** $r = N \bmod k = 100 \bmod 4 = 0$. Since the residual is zero, **no peel is required**.

2. **New trip count (1 mark):** $N' = \lfloor 100/4 \rfloor = 25$ iterations.

3. **Transformed loop (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < 25; i = i+1) \; \{ \\
&\qquad x[4i]   = y[4i]   + z[4i]; \\
&\qquad x[4i+1] = y[4i+1] + z[4i+1]; \\
&\qquad x[4i+2] = y[4i+2] + z[4i+2]; \\
&\qquad x[4i+3] = y[4i+3] + z[4i+3]; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Branch reduction (2 marks):** Original branch count = $N+1 = 101$. New branch count = $N'+1 = 26$. Reduction = $101 - 26 = 75$ branches. Percentage reduction = $(75/101) \times 100 \approx 74.26\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **functional flow of the loop unrolling pass** within a compiler backend. List the **prerequisites** that must hold for safe unrolling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Functional flow (4 marks):**
    * The IR walker reaches a natural loop $L$ whose header dominates all back-edges.
    * The **trip count analyzer** resolves (or bounds) $N$ to a symbolic or constant form.
    * The **dependence tester** verifies that no two iterations carry a dependence whose distance is less than $k$ (otherwise reordering operations across iterations would violate semantics).
    * The **body cloner** produces $k$ copies of the loop body, substituting the induction variable $i$ with $i, i+1, \dots, i+k-1$.
    * The **residue peeler** generates a prologue (and possibly an epilogue) to handle $r = N \bmod k$.
    * The **back-edge rewriter** patches the branch target to the new header and rewires induction increments by $k$.

2. **Prerequisites (3 marks):**
    * The trip count $N$ must be bounded (or known).
    * The loop must be in **normalized form** (single induction variable, increment of 1).
    * The dependences must be **legal to reorder** across iterations (Legality Test).
    * The available registers must suffice; otherwise, the allocator may degrade performance.

**(b)** Given the loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 0; i < N; i = i+1) \\
&\qquad a[i] = a[i-1] + b[i];
\end{aligned}
$$

with $N = 10$ and $k = 3$, perform partial unrolling. Show all three transformed code regions explicitly (prologue, new loop, epilogue). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Residual (1 mark):** $r = N \bmod k = 10 \bmod 3 = 1$. So **one iteration must be peeled**.

2. **New trip count (1 mark):** $N' = \lfloor 10/3 \rfloor = 3$ iterations.

3. **Prologue — handle the residue iteration $i = 0$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a[0] &= a[-1] + b[0];
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **New loop — body of $k = 3$ fused copies (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i = 1; i \le 9; i = i + 3) \; \{ \\
&\qquad a[i]   = a[i-1]   + b[i]; \\
&\qquad a[i+1] = a[i]     + b[i+1]; \\
&\qquad a[i+2] = a[i+1]   + b[i+2]; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. **Verification of the loop-carried dependence (1 mark):** Because each statement reads $a[i-1]$ written by the previous one, the dependence is preserved by the **exact textual order** of the fused body. Hence the transformation is **legal**.

> **Note for students:** In a real dependence test, the compiler would identify a *loop-carried true dependence* of distance 1 and only allow unrolling that respects the original statement order — exactly as we did above.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Loop Unrolling** replicates the loop body $k$ times to amortize branch overhead and increase ILP.
* The **unroll factor** $k$ controls the trade-off: larger $k$ ⇒ more parallelism but more code size and register pressure.
* **Full unrolling** removes the branch entirely and is feasible only for small, constant trip counts.
* **Partial unrolling** handles symbolic or large $N$ via a peeled **prologue/epilogue** for the residual $r = N \bmod k$.
* **New trip count:** $N' = \lfloor N/k \rfloor$.
* **Legality** depends on the dependence structure — reordering across iterations must not violate any true (flow) dependence.
* **Modern pitfalls:** aggressive unrolling can *hurt* performance on out-of-order CPUs with large I-caches, deep BTBs, and good branch predictors.
* **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write (i) the unrolled loop, (ii) the peeled residual block, (iii) the new trip count, and (iv) the branch-count reduction — all four items carry easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Global Optimization** within a compiler's intermediate code generation phase, an **Uninitialized Variable** is a named program variable whose storage location has been allocated but whose value has never been explicitly defined (assigned) along any execution path leading to its current use. Detection of such variables is critical because referencing a variable before definition yields **undefined behaviour**, a violation of program correctness.

**Live Variable Analysis** is a classic **backward-flow data-flow analysis** technique used to determine, for every program point, which variables may be *live* (i.e., their current values may be read subsequently before being overwritten). The intersection of "live at a use point" and "no reaching definition" precisely characterises an uninitialized-variable hazard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Global Optimization):**
> The detection of uninitialized variables is classified as a **must-be-done global data-flow problem** because the *Use* of a variable is observed, but no *Definition* reaches that use on some path. This falls under the broader umbrella of "Global Optimisation" techniques that operate over the entire Control Flow Graph (CFG).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Empty Water Glass:**
> Imagine a buffet table where a customer walks up with a **brand new water glass** that has never been filled. A waiter offers to pour from the jug. The waiter has two questions:
> 1. **Is this glass still being used later?** (i.e., *is the variable live?*)
> 2. **Has any jug actually filled it before now?** (i.e., *does a definition reach the use?*)
>
> If the answer to (1) is YES and to (2) is NO, the customer is about to *drink from an empty glass* — that is exactly the compiler's warning: **"variable may be used uninitialized"**.

A more programmer-friendly view: a variable is *uninitialized at a use* if it is *live at that use* and the use is *not reached by any definition* of the variable.

## 1.3 The Role of Live Sets in This Detection

The **Live-In Set** $L_{IN}(B)$ of a basic block $B$ is the set of variables whose values flow into $B$ and may be used in $B$ or its successors *before* being redefined. The **Live-Out Set** $L_{OUT}(B)$ is the set of variables that are live immediately *after* the last statement of $B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:**
> A variable $v$ is *uninitialized at a use site* if and only if:
> $$\text{Uninit}(v) \iff (v \in L_{IN}(\text{use-block})) \land (\text{no reaching definition of } v \text{ reaches that use})$$

This combines **Liveness Analysis (backward)** with **Reaching Definitions Analysis (forward)** — two canonical data-flow frameworks operating in tandem.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **Lattice:** $(\mathcal{P}(Vars), \subseteq)$ — the powerset lattice of program variables, with $\bot = \emptyset$ and $\top = Vars$.
- **Meet Operator ($\wedge$):** Set **union** ($\cup$) for may-analysis (liveness is a *may* problem).
- **Transfer Function:** $L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))$.
- **Boundary Condition:** $L_{OUT}(\text{EXIT}) = \emptyset$ (after program termination, nothing is live).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the Algorithm Works

The detection of uninitialized variables proceeds in **two interlocking phases**:

### Phase A — Liveness Analysis (Backward Data-Flow)

1. **Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)** $G = (N, E)$ with $N$ = basic blocks and $E$ = edges representing possible flow of control.
2. For each basic block $B$, compute two auxiliary sets:
   - $\text{Use}(B)$: variables used in $B$ *before* any definition of the same variable in $B$.
   - $\text{Def}(B)$: variables defined (assigned) in $B$ *before* any use of the same variable in $B$.
3. Iteratively solve the system:
   - $L_{OUT}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} L_{IN}(S)$
   - $L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))$
4. Repeat until no $L_{IN}$ or $L_{OUT}$ set changes (fixed point reached).

### Phase B — Reaching Definitions (Forward Data-Flow)

1. For each block $B$, compute:
   - $\text{Gen}(B)$: definitions generated within $B$ that reach the *end* of $B$.
   - $\text{Kill}(B)$: definitions killed (overwritten) within $B$.
2. Iteratively solve:
   - $R_{IN}(B) = \bigcup_{P \in pred(B)} R_{OUT}(P)$
   - $R_{OUT}(B) = \text{Gen}(B) \cup (R_{IN}(B) - \text{Kill}(B))$
3. Boundary: $R_{OUT}(\text{ENTRY}) = \emptyset$ and $R_{IN}(\text{ENTRY}) = \emptyset$ (no definitions reach the start).

### Phase C — Uninitialized Variable Detection

1. For every use site $u$ of variable $v$ in block $B$:
   - If $v \in L_{IN}(B)$ (i.e., $v$ is live entering the use path), **and**
   - No definition $d$ of $v$ appears in $R_{IN}(B)$ *on every path* to $u$,
2. Then $v$ is **possibly uninitialized** at $u$. The compiler emits a warning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Logic:**
> Liveness answers *"does the value matter?"*, while reaching definitions answers *"where did the value come from?"*. Their conjunction captures the precise condition under which a programmer's expectation of a defined value is violated.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Live-Out of a block** | $L_{OUT}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} L_{IN}(S)$ | Iterated until fixed point |
| **Live-In of a block** | $L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))$ | Backward transfer function |
| **Reaching In** | $R_{IN}(B) = \bigcup_{P \in pred(B)} R_{OUT}(P)$ | Forward confluence |
| **Reaching Out** | $R_{OUT}(B) = \text{Gen}(B) \cup (R_{IN}(B) - \text{Kill}(B))$ | Forward transfer function |
| **Boundary (Liveness)** | $L_{OUT}(EXIT) = \emptyset$ | Nothing is live after program |
| **Boundary (Reaching)** | $R_{IN}(ENTRY) = \emptyset$ | No defs reach program start |
| **Uninit Condition** | $v \in L_{IN}(u) \land R_{IN}(u) \cap \text{Def}(v) = \emptyset$ | Warning emitted |
| **Use(B)** | vars used in $B$ **before** redefinition in $B$ | Local analysis |
| **Def(B)** | vars defined in $B$ **before** use in $B$ | Local analysis |
| **Lattice Meet** | $\wedge = \cup$ (for may-analyses) | Liveness, Reaching |
| **Lattice Order** | $\sqsubseteq = \subseteq$ (set inclusion) | Monotone framework |
| **Termination** | $2^{\vert Vars \vert}$ iterations max | Bounded by finite lattice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> This analysis is built into every modern compiler. `gcc -Wuninitialized`, `clang -Wsometimes-uninitialized`, and Microsoft MSVC's `/w4` all implement variants of this exact data-flow framework to issue compile-time warnings — a direct industrial application of KTU Module 4 content.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Solution (KTU Exam Style)

Consider the following three-address code fragment:

```c
1:  i = 0;
2:  j = 1;
3:  if (i < 10) goto 9;
4:  i = i + 1;
5:  j = k + 2;     // use of k
6:  goto 3;
7:  i = 0;
8:  k = i + j;
9:  print i, j, k;
```

We partition into basic blocks:

| Block | Statements | Successor(s) |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ (ENTRY) | $i = 0;\ j = 1;\ \text{if}(i<10)\ \text{goto } 9;$ | $B_2$, $B_3$ |
| $B_2$ | $i = i + 1;\ j = k + 2;\ \text{goto } B_1$ | $B_1$ |
| $B_3$ (EXIT) | $i = 0;\ k = i + j;\ \text{print } i,j,k;$ | — |

### Step 1 — Compute Local Use and Def Sets

For $B_1$: the only uses in $B_1$ are $i$ (in `i<10`) — but $i$ is also defined earlier in $B_1$, so **before** that definition there is no use. Thus $\text{Use}(B_1) = \emptyset$. $\text{Def}(B_1) = \{i, j\}$.

For $B_2$: the uses are $i$ (in `i+1`) and $k$ (in `k+2`). $i$ is also defined in $B_2$ *after* its use, so it counts as a use. $j$ is only defined (not used). So $\text{Use}(B_2) = \{i, k\}$, $\text{Def}(B_2) = \{i, j\}$.

For $B_3$: the uses are $i, j$ in `i+j`, and $i, j, k$ in `print`. $i$ is also defined in $B_3$ — only the **use before redefinition** counts, which is the use in `i+j`. So $\text{Use}(B_3) = \{i, j\}$. $\text{Def}(B_3) = \{i, k\}$.

| Block | $\text{Use}$ | $\text{Def}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{i, j\}$ |
| $B_2$ | $\{i, k\}$ | $\{i, j\}$ |
| $B_3$ | $\{i, j\}$ | $\{i, k\}$ |

### Step 2 — Solve Liveness Equations (Backward, Iterative)

Initialise: $L_{OUT}^{(0)}(B_i) = \emptyset$ for all $i$, $L_{IN}^{(0)}(B_i) = \text{Use}(B_i)$.

Equations:

$$
L_{OUT}(B_1) = L_{IN}(B_2)
$$
$$
L_{OUT}(B_2) = L_{IN}(B_1)
$$
$$
L_{OUT}(B_3) = \emptyset \quad \text{(EXIT boundary)}
$$
$$
L_{IN}(B_1) = \text{Use}(B_1) \cup (L_{OUT}(B_1) - \text{Def}(B_1))
$$
$$
L_{IN}(B_2) = \text{Use}(B_2) \cup (L_{OUT}(B_2) - \text{Def}(B_2))
$$
$$
L_{IN}(B_3) = \text{Use}(B_3) \cup (L_{OUT}(B_3) - \text{Def}(B_3))
$$

**Iteration 1:**

$L_{OUT}(B_3) = \emptyset$
$L_{IN}(B_3) = \{i, j\} \cup (\emptyset - \{i, k\}) = \{i, j\}$
$L_{IN}(B_2) = \{i, k\} \cup (\emptyset - \{i, j\}) = \{i, k\}$
$L_{OUT}(B_1) = L_{IN}(B_2) = \{i, k\}$
$L_{IN}(B_1) = \emptyset \cup (\{i, k\} - \{i, j\}) = \{k\}$
$L_{OUT}(B_2) = L_{IN}(B_1) = \{k\}$
Updated $L_{IN}(B_2) = \{i, k\} \cup (\{k\} - \{i, j\}) = \{i, k\}$ (no change)

**Iteration 2:**

$L_{OUT}(B_1) = \{i, k\}$ (from $B_2$'s IN)
$L_{IN}(B_1) = \emptyset \cup (\{i,k\} - \{i,j\}) = \{k\}$ (no change)

**Fixed point reached.**

| Block | $L_{IN}$ | $L_{OUT}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $\{k\}$ | $\{i, k\}$ |
| $B_2$ | $\{i, k\}$ | $\{k\}$ |
| $B_3$ | $\{i, j\}$ | $\emptyset$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Variable $k$ is live entering $B_1$. This is the **smoking gun** — $k$ is used in $B_2$ (in `j = k + 2`) but no path from ENTRY defines $k$ before $B_2$. Hence $k$ is *potentially uninitialized*.

### Step 3 — Solve Reaching Definitions (Forward, Iterative)

We label definitions: $d_1 : i = 0$ (in $B_1$), $d_2 : j = 1$ (in $B_1$), $d_3 : i = i+1$ (in $B_2$), $d_4 : j = k+2$ (in $B_2$), $d_5 : i = 0$ (in $B_3$), $d_6 : k = i+j$ (in $B_3$).

| Block | $\text{Gen}$ | $\text{Kill}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ | $\{d_3, d_5\}$ (kills other $i$ defs) |
| $B_2$ | $\{d_3, d_4\}$ | $\{d_1, d_5, d_2\}$ (kills $i$ and $j$ defs) |
| $B_3$ | $\{d_5, d_6\}$ | $\{d_1, d_3\}$ (kills $i$ defs) |

Initialise: $R_{IN}(B_1) = \emptyset$, $R_{OUT}(B_i) = \emptyset$.

**Iteration 1:**

$R_{IN}(B_1) = \emptyset$
$R_{OUT}(B_1) = \{d_1, d_2\} \cup (\emptyset - \{d_3, d_5\}) = \{d_1, d_2\}$
$R_{IN}(B_2) = R_{OUT}(B_1) = \{d_1, d_2\}$
$R_{OUT}(B_2) = \{d_3, d_4\} \cup (\{d_1, d_2\} - \{d_1, d_5, d_2\}) = \{d_3, d_4\}$
$R_{IN}(B_3) = R_{OUT}(B_2) = \{d_3, d_4\}$
$R_{OUT}(B_3) = \{d_5, d_6\} \cup (\{d_3, d_4\} - \{d_1, d_3\}) = \{d_4, d_5, d_6\}$

**Iteration 2:** No further change → **fixed point**.

| Block | $R_{IN}$ | $R_{OUT}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ (ENTRY) | $\emptyset$ | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ |
| $B_2$ | $\{d_1, d_2\}$ | $\{d_3, d_4\}$ |
| $B_3$ | $\{d_3, d_4\}$ | $\{d_4, d_5, d_6\}$ |

### Step 4 — Uninitialized Variable Decision

The use of $k$ is at statement $j = k+2$ in $B_2$. We examine $R_{IN}(B_2) = \{d_1, d_2\}$. Definitions of $k$ are only $\{d_6\}$. Since $d_6 \notin R_{IN}(B_2)$, **no definition of $k$ reaches its use** in $B_2$. Combined with $k \in L_{IN}(B_1)$ (it is live along the path), the compiler correctly flags:

> [!WARNING]
> **Compiler Warning:** `Variable 'k' may be uninitialized when used here.`

### 3.2 Algorithmic Complexity Analysis

The iterative data-flow algorithm for liveness runs in time:
$$
O(N \cdot E \cdot V)
$$
where $N$ is the number of basic blocks, $E$ is the number of edges in the CFG, and $V$ is the number of variables. The factor $V$ arises because each iteration manipulates bit vectors of length $V$, and at most $2^V$ distinct values exist (ensuring termination).

The reverse-CFG topological sort for liveness is **not** available (the graph is processed backward), so the worklist algorithm is the standard efficient approach.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Graph of the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeB1["B1 ENTRY: i=0; j=1; if i<10 goto B3"]
    nodeB2["B2: i=i+1; j=k+2; goto B1"]
    nodeB3["B3 EXIT: i=0; k=i+j; print i,j,k"]

    nodeB1 -- true branch: i>=10 --> nodeB3
    nodeB1 -- false branch: i less 10 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 -- unconditional back edge --> nodeB1

    style nodeB1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    style nodeB2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    style nodeB3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA["Source Program"] --> cfgA["CFG Constructor"]
    cfgA --> bblk["Basic Block Partitioner"]
    bblk --> useA["Compute Use(B) and Def(B) per block"]
    useA --> liveA["Backward Iterative Solver: Liveness"]
    bblk --> genA["Compute Gen(B) and Kill(B) per block"]
    genA --> reachA["Forward Iterative Solver: Reaching Defs"]
    liveA --> combA["Combine L_IN with R_IN"]
    reachA --> combA
    combA --> warnA["Uninitialized Variable Emitter"]
    warnA --> outA["Compile-Time Warning List"]

    style srcA fill:#e2e3e5
    style cfgA fill:#cce5ff
    style bblk fill:#cce5ff
    style useA fill:#fff3cd
    style liveA fill:#f8d7da
    style genA fill:#fff3cd
    style reachA fill:#f8d7da
    style combA fill:#d4edda
    style warnA fill:#d4edda
    style outA fill:#d1ecf1
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Lattice Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    topNode["TOP: All variables V = {i, j, k, ...}"]
    s1["Subset 1: {i, k}"]
    s2["Subset 2: {i, j}"]
    s3["Subset 3: {k}"]
    s4["Subset 4: {i}"]
    s5["Subset 5: {j}"]
    botNode["BOTTOM: Empty Set = Bot"]

    topNode --> s1
    topNode --> s2
    s1 --> s3
    s1 --> s4
    s2 --> s4
    s2 --> s5
    s3 --> botNode
    s4 --> botNode
    s5 --> botNode

    style topNode fill:#f8d7da
    style botNode fill:#d4edda
    style s1 fill:#fff3cd
    style s2 fill:#fff3cd
    style s3 fill:#cce5ff
    style s4 fill:#cce5ff
    style s5 fill:#cce5ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The lattice diagram illustrates the **monotonic descent** during iterative data-flow analysis. Each iteration of the solver produces a value lower (or equal) in the lattice than the previous one, guaranteeing termination at the fixed point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define an uninitialized variable. How does live variable analysis help detect it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **uninitialized variable** is one whose storage is allocated but no definition reaches its use along some execution path. **Liveness analysis** computes, for each program point, the set of variables whose current values may be read before being overwritten. If a variable is *live* at a point but no *reaching definition* of that variable arrives at the same point, the variable is potentially uninitialized. Hence, liveness + reaching definitions together pinpoint uninitialized uses with high precision (3 marks: definition 1, liveness role 1, combination logic 1).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Write the data-flow equations for Live-In and Live-Out of a basic block B. State the boundary condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$
L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))
$$
$$
L_{OUT}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} L_{IN}(S)
$$
**Boundary condition:** $L_{OUT}(B_{EXIT}) = \emptyset$ (after program termination, no variable is live). (1 mark per equation + 1 mark for boundary.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of *Live Variable Analysis* with its data-flow equations. How is it classified — forward or backward, may or must analysis? Justify.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following three-address code, construct the CFG, compute Use/Def sets, and determine the Live-In and Live-Out of every block. Identify any uninitialized variable.

```c
1:  a = 5;
2:  b = 10;
3:  if (a > b) goto 7;
4:  c = a + b;
5:  d = e + 1;     // use of e
6:  goto 3;
7:  print c, d, e;
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Live Variable Analysis — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

Live Variable Analysis is a **backward-flow, may (over-approximating), intra-procedural data-flow analysis**. It determines, for every program point $p$, the set of variables that may be used (read) after $p$ before being redefined.

**Why backward?** A variable's liveness is determined by *future* uses, so we propagate information from program exit backward to entry.

**Why may analysis?** If *any* execution path uses a variable after point $p$, we conservatively declare it live — the join operator is **union**.

**Why data-flow equations?**

The transfer function for a block $B$ is:
$$
L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))
$$
where $\text{Use}(B)$ are variables used in $B$ before any redefinition within $B$, and $\text{Def}(B)$ are variables defined in $B$ before any use within $B$. The intuition: a variable is live entering $B$ if either (i) it is used in $B$ before being killed, or (ii) it is live exiting $B$ and not redefined in $B$.

The confluence (meet) operator over successors is **set union**:
$$
L_{OUT}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} L_{IN}(S)
$$
**Boundary:** $L_{OUT}(EXIT) = \emptyset$ (after exit, nothing is live).

Classification: **Backward (we process blocks in reverse postorder of the reverse CFG), May (union is the join), Intra-procedural (single function scope).** (Full 7 marks: 2 for concept, 2 for equations, 2 for classification with justification, 1 for boundary.)

---

**(b) Worked Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Partition into blocks (1 mark):**

| Block | Statements | Succ |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $a=5;\ b=10;\ \text{if}(a>b)\ \text{goto } 7$ | $B_2, B_3$ |
| $B_2$ | $c=a+b;\ d=e+1;\ \text{goto } B_1$ | $B_1$ |
| $B_3$ | $\text{print } c, d, e$ | EXIT |

**Step 2 — Use/Def sets (1 mark):**

$B_1$: Uses = $\{a\}$ (in `a>b` after definition of $a$ — wait, $a$ is defined first then used, so use of $a$ survives). Uses = $\{a\}$. Defs = $\{a, b\}$.
$B_2$: Uses = $\{a, b, e\}$. Defs = $\{c, d\}$.
$B_3$: Uses = $\{c, d, e\}$. Defs = $\emptyset$.

**Step 3 — Iterative solution (3 marks):**

| Iter | $L_{IN}(B_1)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_1)$ | $L_{IN}(B_2)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_2)$ | $L_{IN}(B_3)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{a\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{c,d,e\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | $\{a\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{a\}$ | $\{c,d,e\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | $\{b,e\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{b,e\}$ | $\{c,d,e\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 3 | $\{b,e\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{a,b,e\}$ | $\{b,e\}$ | $\{c,d,e\}$ | $\emptyset$ |

**Fixed point reached at iteration 3.**

**Step 4 — Uninitialized variable identification (2 marks):**

Reaching definitions for $e$: the only definition of $e$ is *not present* anywhere in the code. So $R_{IN}(B_2) \cap \text{Def}(e) = \emptyset$. Since $e \in L_{IN}(B_2)$ (it is live, used in $B_2$ and $B_3$), **$e$ is potentially uninitialized at its use in $d = e + 1$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\text{Use}(B)$ with all uses in $B$.** Only uses that occur *before* a redefinition of the same variable in $B$ are counted. This is the most common error costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Iterate until fixed point — never stop early.** The lattice is finite, so termination is guaranteed; stopping after one iteration is a frequent KTU mistake.
> 3. **Boundary condition:** Students often forget $L_{OUT}(EXIT) = \emptyset$. This is worth 1 mark.
> 4. **For uninitialized detection:** Both liveness AND reaching definitions must be computed. Reporting only one half earns partial credit only.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between *Reaching Definitions* and *Live Variable Analysis* in terms of direction, confluence operator, transfer function, and lattice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the code below, identify all uninitialized variables. Show the full analysis.

```c
1:  x = read();
2:  if (x > 0) goto 5;
3:  y = x * 2;
4:  goto 6;
5:  z = y + x;     // use of y
6:  print x, y, z;
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Reaching Definitions | Live Variable Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Forward | Backward |
| **Transfer Function** | $R_{OUT}(B) = \text{Gen}(B) \cup (R_{IN}(B) - \text{Kill}(B))$ | $L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))$ |
| **Confluence (Meet)** | $\cup$ (may — any predecessor's contribution) | $\cup$ (may — any successor's demand) |
| **Lattice** | $(\mathcal{P}(Defs), \subseteq)$ | $(\mathcal{P}(Vars), \subseteq)$ |
| **Boundary** | $R_{IN}(ENTRY) = \emptyset$ | $L_{OUT}(EXIT) = \emptyset$ |
| **Information Propagated** | Definitions | Variables (symbols) |
| **May/Must** | May (over-approx) | May (over-approx) |
| **Application** | Constant propagation, uninit detection | Register allocation, dead-store elim |

**Key conceptual point:** Reaching definitions track *origin* of values; liveness tracks *future demand* of values. Their combination is exactly the framework for uninitialized-variable detection. (2 marks for table, 2 marks for direction/transfer, 2 marks for boundary/lattice, 1 mark for synthesis.)

---

**(b) Worked Solution (7 marks):**

**Blocks:**
- $B_1$: `x=read(); if(x>0) goto 5;` → succ: $B_2$, $B_3$
- $B_2$: `y=x*2; goto 6;` → succ: $B_3$ (i.e., $B_3$ is the EXIT block)
- $B_3$ (EXIT): `z=y+x; print x,y,z;`

**Use/Def sets:**

| Block | Use | Def |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $\emptyset$ (x used after def within block — counts as def only) | $\{x\}$ |
| $B_2$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{y\}$ |
| $B_3$ | $\{x, y\}$ (z used after def of z? z is defined, then used in print — but we only count use-before-def; z is defined before use, so z is NOT a use; x and y are not redefined in $B_3$, so all their uses count) | $\{z\}$ |

**Liveness iteration:**

| Iter | $L_{IN}(B_1)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_1)$ | $L_{IN}(B_2)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_2)$ | $L_{IN}(B_3)$ | $L_{OUT}(B_3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{x\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | $\emptyset$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | $\{x, y\}$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 3 | $\{x, y\}$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\{x, y\}$ | $\emptyset$ |

**Reaching definitions for $y$:** Definitions of $y$ exist only as $d_y: y = x*2$ in $B_2$. Path analysis: to reach the use of $y$ in $B_3$ (at `z=y+x`), we must pass through $B_2$. If we take the path $B_1 \to B_2 \to B_3$, $d_y$ reaches. If we take $B_1 \to B_3$ directly, **no definition of $y$ reaches $B_3$**.

So the use of $y$ in $B_3$ (at `z=y+x` and `print y`) is **uninitialized on the path $B_1 \to B_3$** when $x \le 0$. Since this path exists, the compiler must warn: **`y` is potentially uninitialized**.

Variable `z` is defined in $B_3$ before its use in `print` → safe.
Variable `x` is defined in $B_1$ before all uses → safe.
Variable `y` is uninitialized on one path → **WARNING**.

(2 marks for blocks, 1 mark for Use/Def, 2 marks for liveness, 1 mark for reaching, 1 mark for uninit decision.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall for Question B(b):**
> Students often claim `z` is uninitialized because the print statement uses it, missing that `z` is *defined* in the same block before its use. A use-before-definition in the **same block** is the textbook definition of uninitialized access. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU Module 4, Global Optimization**
>
> 1. **Uninitialized variable** = a variable with at least one use not preceded by a definition on some path.
> 2. **Liveness analysis is BACKWARD; reaching definitions is FORWARD.**
> 3. **Confluence (meet) operator for both = set UNION** (both are *may*-analyses, over-approximating).
> 4. **Liveness equations:** $L_{IN}(B) = \text{Use}(B) \cup (L_{OUT}(B) - \text{Def}(B))$; $L_{OUT}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} L_{IN}(S)$.
> 5. **Reaching equations:** $R_{OUT}(B) = \text{Gen}(B) \cup (R_{IN}(B) - \text{Kill}(B))$; $R_{IN}(B) = \bigcup_{P \in pred(B)} R_{OUT}(P)$.
> 6. **Boundary conditions:** $L_{OUT}(EXIT) = \emptyset$; $R_{IN}(ENTRY) = \emptyset$. **Forgetting these costs marks.**
> 7. **Use(B) ≠ all uses in B** — only uses appearing *before* any redefinition of the same variable within B. **Most common KTU mistake.**
> 8. **Def(B) ≠ all definitions in B** — only definitions appearing *before* any use of the same variable within B.
> 9. **Termination:** Maximum $2^{\vert Vars \vert}$ iterations; in practice much fewer.
> 10. **Time complexity:** $O(N \cdot E \cdot V)$ for the worklist algorithm.
> 11. **Lattice:** $(\mathcal{P}(Vars), \subseteq)$ with $\bot = \emptyset$, $\top = Vars$. Monotone transfer functions guarantee fixed point.
> 12. **Industrial relevance:** Implemented in `gcc -Wuninitialized`, `clang -Wsometimes-uninitialized`, MSVC `/w4`.
> 13. **Combines backward + forward analyses** — liveness tells *what is needed*; reaching defs tells *where it came from*.
> 14. **May vs Must:** Liveness is a *may*-analysis (lattice meet = union); uninitialized detection itself is also *may* (report on any path).
> 15. **Reverse Postorder (RPO)** of the reverse CFG is the optimal processing order for liveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Code Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST601 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Code Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Global Code Placement** is a class of advanced code optimization techniques used during the **code generation** phase of a compiler, in which the compiler **reorders, repositions, and restructures basic blocks across the entire procedure (i.e., globally)**, rather than confining itself to a single basic block. The objective is to maximize the utilization of modern processor resources such as **instruction pipelines, branch prediction units, instruction caches (I-cache), and issue slots** by placing frequently executed paths so that they form straight-line, contiguous code sequences with minimal branching and cache miss overhead.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is studied as a refinement of **Basic Block Reordering** and forms a bridge between local instruction scheduling and **Software Pipelining**. Three principal techniques fall under this umbrella:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor — Three Pillars of Global Code Placement**
> 1. **Trace Scheduling** (J. A. Fisher, 1981) — earliest global placement technique.
> 2. **Superblock Scheduling** — extension of trace scheduling eliminating *side entrances*.
> 3. **Hyperblock Scheduling** — uses *if-conversion* to obtain predicated, straight-line code.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a tourist walking through a **large museum** with hundreds of interconnected halls (basic blocks). Some halls are visited by 90% of tourists (e.g., the *Mona Lisa* room), while others are visited only by 5% (a side gallery). The museum director wants to:

1. **Place the most popular rooms next to each other** so the crowd flows naturally without backtracking (this is *trace selection*).
2. **Remove small detours** that cause the crowd to scatter (this is *superblock formation*).
3. **Replace signed side doors** with a unified guide (this is *if-conversion / hyperblock*).

The result? The crowd (the *instruction stream*) moves through a **straight, predictable, low-friction path** that minimizes waiting, detours, and confusion — exactly what the CPU's pipeline, branch predictor, and instruction cache desire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Recap**
> - **Trace** = a *likely-execution path* through the control-flow graph.
> - **Superblock** = a trace with side entrances *removed* (a "cleaner" trace).
> - **Hyperblock** = a *predicated* straight-line code block formed by if-conversion.
> - **Goal** = place such blocks contiguously in memory so the **instruction fetch unit** never stalls.

## 1.3 Why is Global Code Placement Required?

- **Branch penalty**: Each mispredicted branch costs **3–20+ cycles** in modern out-of-order processors.
- **I-cache miss**: Cold instruction misses cost **~50–200 cycles**.
- **Pipeline bubbles**: A delayed instruction fetch leaves the issue slots idle.
- **Profile-guided insight**: With runtime profiling, the compiler can lay out the procedure so that the *hottest* path is the *default* layout in memory, while cold paths (rarely taken) are pushed to out-of-line locations.

## 1.4 Positioning in the KTU 2024 Compiler Pipeline

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Lex} \rightarrow \text{Parse} \rightarrow \text{Semantic} \rightarrow \text{IR} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Local Opt.} \rightarrow \text{Global Opt.} \rightarrow \text{Global Code Placement}}_{\text{Module 4}} \rightarrow \text{Code Gen} \rightarrow \text{Target}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Big Picture: Inputs and Outputs

| Stage | Input Artifact | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| **Trace Selection** | Control Flow Graph (CFG) + edge profile counts | A sequence of basic blocks — a *trace* |
| **Trace Compaction** | Trace (sequence of blocks) | A straight-line instruction schedule (large basic block) |
| **Bookkeeping** | Off-trace edges | *Compensation code* inserted at trace exits |
| **Superblock Formation** | Trace | Superblock (no side entrances) via **tail duplication** |
| **If-Conversion** | Block with conditional branches | Straight-line predicated code (Hyperblock) |
| **Code Positioning** | CFG + block execution frequencies | Linear memory layout of blocks |

## 2.2 Trace Scheduling — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trace Scheduling (Fisher 1981)** is the **founding technique** of global code placement and is a *guaranteed* KTU exam question.

**Operational Steps (the "Why" and "How"):**

1. **Select a trace** $T$ — A trace is a path $b_1 \rightarrow b_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow b_k$ in the CFG where each edge $b_i \rightarrow b_{i+1}$ has a *high probability* of being traversed (obtained via profiling or heuristics).
   - *Why?* A common execution path executes the same operations repeatedly, so compacting it reduces branches and increases ILP.
2. **Trace compaction (scheduling)** — Within trace $T$, treat the entire chain as one giant basic block and schedule instructions using **list scheduling** to maximize IPC.
   - *How?* Dependencies are now across former basic-block boundaries — dataflow analysis is performed globally.
3. **Add bookkeeping / compensation code** — For each *off-trace* edge entering or leaving the trace, the compiler must insert code that *compensates* for the schedule's effect (since the original ordering is changed).
   - *Why?* The off-trace block may depend on a register value *not yet defined* by the rescheduled trace — we must ensure semantics are preserved.

## 2.3 Superblock Scheduling — Solving the Side-Entrance Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of Trace Scheduling:** A trace may have *side entrances* — other blocks may jump *into the middle* of the trace. The scheduler may move an instruction *up* in the trace that is illegal for those side entrances. The compiler would need a compensation block at every side entrance, exploding the code.

**Superblock Solution:**

- A **superblock** is a trace with **no side entrances** — only one entry (the head) and one or more exits.
- **Tail duplication**: Before forming the superblock, *duplicate* the suffix of the trace (and any block that jumps into its middle) so each side entrance has its own copy.
- *Why?* Now the superblock can be scheduled aggressively, since the only entry is the head — bookkeeping is needed only at the exits, not at every side entrance.

## 2.4 Hyperblock Scheduling — If-Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If-Conversion (Allen et al., 1983)** transforms a sequence of conditional branches into **straight-line, predicated (guarded) code**. Each instruction is tagged with a *predicate*; it executes only if the predicate is true.

**Logical Steps:**

1. Identify a set of basic blocks that form a **single-entry, multiple-exit** region (a *hyperblock*).
2. Convert all conditional branches inside the region to *predicated execution* (e.g., `p1 = (a < b); p2 = !p1;`).
3. Merge the blocks into one straight-line block.
4. Schedule the hyperblock using list scheduling.

$$
\text{Result: a region with no internal branches → maximally exposed ILP}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Hyperblocks rely on hardware support for predication** — found in IA-64 (Itanium), ARM with `csel`/`cinc`, and most modern GPUs.

## 2.5 Code Positioning (Layout) Algorithms

**Goal:** Decide the linear order of basic blocks in the executable.

| Heuristic | Rule |
|---|---|
| **Fall-through preference** | Place the *most likely* successor block immediately after the current block. |
| **Pettis-Hansen** | Repeatedly merge the two blocks (or chains) with the **highest connecting edge weight**. |
| **Chain splitting** | Break cycles in the call/branch graph before merging. |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics and formulas are **frequently tested** in the KTU ESE.

| # | Metric / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Branch misprediction penalty** | $P_{\text{branch}} = P_{\text{mispredict}} \times C_{\text{pipeline}}$ | $C_{\text{pipeline}}$ = pipeline refill cycles (typical: **10–20**) |
| 2 | **Trace execution probability** | $P(T) = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} P(\text{edge } b_i \rightarrow b_{i+1})$ | Product of edge probabilities |
| 3 | **Average cycles per instruction (CPI)** | $\text{CPI} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{BranchPenalty} \times \text{BranchFrequency} + \text{CacheMissPenalty} \times \text{MissRate}$ | Used to compare optimization gains |
| 4 | **Speedup of code placement** | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{unoptimized}}}{T_{\text{optimized}}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f_{\text{branch}} \times p_{\text{mispredict}} \times \dfrac{C_{\text{pen}}}{N_{\text{inst}}}}$ | $f_{\text{branch}}$ = branch frequency, $N_{\text{inst}}$ = instructions per loop |
| 5 | **I-cache miss rate** | $\text{MissRate} = 1 - \text{HitRate}$ | Optimized placement increases spatial locality |
| 6 | **Predicated execution guard** | $I_{\text{effective}} = p \cdot I_{\text{original}}$ | Where $p \in \{0, 1\}$ is the predicate |
| 7 | **Compensation code cost** | $C_{\text{book}} = \sum_{e \in E_{\text{off-trace}}} w_e \cdot c_e$ | $w_e$ = edge frequency, $c_e$ = instruction count to emit |
| 8 | **If-conversion predicate count** | $n_{\text{pred}} = \sum_{b \in B_{\text{region}}} \text{branches}(b)$ | One predicate per conditional branch eliminated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit-Conversion Pitfall:** Branch penalty is in **clock cycles**, NOT in nanoseconds. To convert, divide by clock frequency: $T_{\text{ns}} = C_{\text{cycles}} / f_{\text{GHz}}$.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLIW/EPIC processors** (Itanium, DSPs like TI C6000) **depend on global code placement** — they statically schedule instructions, so the compiler must place blocks to expose parallelism.
- **Modern x86 / ARM cores** use code placement to feed the **μop cache (Decoded I-cache)** and **µop queue** without bubbles.
- **GPU shader compilers** (NVIDIA NVCC, AMD GCN) use hyperblock-style if-conversion to vectorize divergent branches.
- **Mobile compilers** (LLVM -O2, -O3) employ profile-guided layout based on **PGO** to reduce branch mispredicts by **15–30%** in practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Trace Selection from a CFG

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE — the compiler is given a CFG with edge probabilities and asked to identify the *best trace*.

**Problem.** Consider the control flow graph with edge probabilities shown below (assume block numbering B1, B2, B3, B4, B5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &\rightarrow B_2 \quad \text{with probability } p_{12} = 0.85 \\
B_1 &\rightarrow B_3 \quad \text{with probability } p_{13} = 0.15 \\
B_2 &\rightarrow B_4 \quad \text{with probability } p_{24} = 0.70 \\
B_2 &\rightarrow B_5 \quad \text{with probability } p_{25} = 0.30 \\
B_3 &\rightarrow B_5 \quad \text{with probability } p_{35} = 1.00 \\
B_4 &\rightarrow B_5 \quad \text{with probability } p_{45} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.** Enumerate all root-to-exit paths and compute their probabilities.

Path A: $B_1 \to B_2 \to B_4 \to B_5$
$$
P(A) = p_{12} \times p_{24} \times p_{45} = 0.85 \times 0.70 \times 1.00
$$
$$
P(A) = 0.595
$$

Path B: $B_1 \to B_2 \to B_5$
$$
P(B) = 0.85 \times 0.30 \times 1.00 = 0.255
$$

Path C: $B_1 \to B_3 \to B_5$
$$
P(C) = 0.15 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 0.150
$$

**Step 2.** Verify total probability.
$$
P(A) + P(B) + P(C) = 0.595 + 0.255 + 0.150 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3.** Select the *best trace*. By Fisher's rule, pick the path with the **highest probability**:
$$
T = B_1 \to B_2 \to B_4 \to B_5
$$

**Step 4.** Identify off-trace edges (these need **bookkeeping code**):
- $B_1 \to B_3$ (leaves trace at $B_1$)
- $B_2 \to B_5$ (leaves trace at $B_2$)

**Step 5.** Bookkeeping: for edge $B_1 \to B_3$, after the scheduled trace, the compiler must emit a jump to $B_3$ (or duplicate the tail of $B_1$ followed by a jump to $B_3$). Similarly for $B_2 \to B_5$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Speedup from Code Placement

> [!TIP]
> This is a **derivation KTU often sets in Part B**.

**Given:**
- $f_{\text{branch}}$ = branch frequency in inner loop = **0.20 branches/inst**
- $p_{\text{mispredict}}$ = misprediction rate before optimization = **0.10**
- $p'_{\text{mispredict}}$ = misprediction rate after global code placement = **0.02**
- $C_{\text{pen}}$ = branch penalty = **12 cycles**
- $N_{\text{inst}}$ = dynamic instructions per loop iteration = **100**

**Step 1.** Compute **unoptimized branch stalls per iteration**:
$$
S_{\text{unopt}} = f_{\text{branch}} \times p_{\text{mispredict}} \times C_{\text{pen}}
$$
$$
S_{\text{unopt}} = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 12 = 0.24 \text{ stall cycles/instruction} \times 100 = 24 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute **optimized branch stalls per iteration**:
$$
S_{\text{opt}} = 0.20 \times 0.02 \times 12 \times 100 = 4.8 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute **total cycles per iteration**:

Assume base CPI = 1.0, so $C_{\text{base}} = 1.0 \times 100 = 100$ cycles.

$$
C_{\text{unopt}} = 100 + 24 = 124 \text{ cycles}
$$
$$
C_{\text{opt}} = 100 + 4.8 = 104.8 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4.** Speedup:
$$
S = \dfrac{C_{\text{unopt}}}{C_{\text{opt}}} = \dfrac{124}{104.8} \approx 1.183
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 1.18 \times \;\;\text{(a 15.5% reduction in total cycle count)}}
$$

## 3.3 Compensation-Code Cost Analysis (Worked)

**Scenario.** Trace $T = B_1 \to B_2 \to B_4$ is scheduled and an instruction $I_x$ that originally was at position 5 in $B_2$ is *moved up* to position 2 of the trace. An off-trace edge enters $B_2$ (or its tail) at position 4, executing $I_x$ *before* its original operands were ready.

**Compiler Action:**

1. Detect the off-trace edge and the moved instruction.
2. Insert a **compensation copy** of $I_x$ (with original semantics) at the off-trace entry.
3. **Cost incurred:** $C_{\text{book}} = w_e \times c_e$ — if the off-trace edge executes 1 in 1000 invocations and the compensation code has 2 instructions, the cost is **0.002 extra instructions per invocation** — usually negligible.

**Conclusion:** Bookkeeping cost $\ll$ savings from better scheduling, *provided* the trace is the dominant path.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix of the Three Techniques

| Aspect | Trace Scheduling | Superblock | Hyperblock |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Entry** | Single | Single (no side entrances) | Single |
| **Exits** | Multiple | Multiple | Multiple |
| **Side entrances handled?** | Compensation at every entry | **Removed via tail duplication** | Eliminated by if-conversion |
| **Code bloat** | Medium | High (duplication) | Medium (predicates) |
| **Branch reduction** | High | Very High | **Maximum (zero internal branches)** |
| **Hardware support needed** | None | None | **Predication** |
| **Used in** | VLIW compilers (Multiflow TRACE) | HP PA-RISC, IA-64 | Itanium, ARM, GPUs |
| **KTU Exam weightage** | **High** (3 marks Q, 14 marks Q) | Medium | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Global Code Placement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CFG + Profile Data] --> B[Trace Selection]
    B --> C{Trace Probability >= Threshold}
    C -- Yes --> D[Trace Compaction via List Scheduling]
    C -- No --> E[Select Next-Highest Trace]
    D --> F[Identify Off-Trace Edges]
    F --> G[Insert Bookkeeping / Compensation Code]
    G --> H[Optional: Tail Duplication]
    H --> I[Superblock Formed]
    I --> J{If-Conversion Applicable?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Predicate the Conditional Branches]
    J -- No --> L[Code Positioning - Linear Layout]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Linearized Procedure]
    E --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start with the CFG and profile; pick a likely trace, compact it, handle off-trace edges, optionally upgrade to superblock or hyperblock, finally do linear placement. **This is the standard "global code placement" pipeline tested in KTU.**

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Trace, Superblock, and Hyperblock Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original_CFG[Original CFG]
        B1A[B1] -- p=0.85 --> B2A[B2]
        B1A -- p=0.15 --> B3A[B3]
        B2A -- p=0.70 --> B4A[B4]
        B2A -- p=0.30 --> B5A[B5]
        B3A --> B5A
        B4A --> B5A
    end

    subgraph Trace_Block[Trace]
        T1[B1] --> T2[B2] --> T3[B4]
    end

    subgraph Super_Block[Superblock after Tail Duplication]
        S1[B1] --> S2[B2] --> S3[B4]
        B1A -.duplicate.-> S1
    end

    subgraph Hyper_Block[Hyperblock after If-Conversion]
        H1[Straight-line Predicated Code] --> H2[No Internal Branches]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace** still has side entrances (the dashed duplicate in the Superblock). **Superblock** duplicates the tail so side entrances are gone. **Hyperblock** is the most aggressive — even the conditional branches inside are converted to predicates.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Compensation / Bookkeeping Code Injection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Scheduled Trace Linearized] --> N{Off-Trace Edge Detected?}
    N -- No --> O[Emit Trace as-is]
    N -- Yes --> P[Locate Moved Instruction in Trace]
    P --> Q[Insert Compensation Copy at Off-Trace Entry]
    Q --> R[Link Off-Trace Block]
    R --> S[Updated Linearized Code]
    O --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall to remember:** Compensation code is *only* needed if an instruction is **moved across** the off-trace edge against the data-dependence direction. Many students wrongly insert compensation code for *every* off-trace edge — a guaranteed mark-deduction in the KTU valuation key.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pettis-Hansen Code Layout (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read CFG + Edge Weights] --> M1[Initialize: each block is its own chain]
    M1 --> M2[Compute edge-weight matrix W]
    M2 --> M3[Pick max-weight edge A-B]
    M3 --> M4{A and B in different chains?}
    M4 -- Yes --> M5[Merge chains, place A adjacent to B]
    M4 -- No --> M6[Skip this edge]
    M5 --> M7{More edges to process?}
    M6 --> M7
    M7 -- Yes --> M3
    M7 -- No --> M8[Break any remaining cycles]
    M8 --> M9[Output final block order]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pettis-Hansen** is the most cited block-level layout algorithm. Note: in the diagram, "edge A-B" is the **strongest** connection in the merged matrix, and the algorithm repeatedly joins chains that share a high-weight edge — a classic greedy agglomerative clustering approach.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define **Trace Scheduling**. Mention its three major phases. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Trace Scheduling** is a global instruction-scheduling technique introduced by **J. A. Fisher (1981)** for VLIW and EPIC architectures, in which a frequently executed path (trace) through a procedure is identified, compacted into straight-line code, and supplemented with bookkeeping (compensation) code to preserve correctness on off-trace paths.

The **three major phases** are:

1. **Trace Selection** — Identify a sequence of basic blocks $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k$ in the CFG with the highest cumulative execution probability.
2. **Trace Compaction** — Treat the entire trace as one large basic block and schedule instructions with list scheduling to exploit maximum ILP.
3. **Bookkeeping (Compensation Code)** — For each off-trace edge entering or leaving the trace, insert code to preserve program semantics after the reordering.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between a **Trace**, a **Superblock**, and a **Hyperblock**. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Trace | Superblock | Hyperblock |
|---|---|---|---|
| Side entrances | **Allowed** | **Eliminated by tail duplication** | Eliminated (single entry) |
| Internal branches | Yes | Yes | **None — converted to predicates** |
| Code bloat | Low | High (duplication) | Medium |
| Hardware needs | None | None | **Predication** |
| Goal | ILP on hot path | ILP + cleaner bookkeeping | Maximum ILP, zero branches |

A **trace** is a likely-execution path; a **superblock** is a trace with side entrances removed; a **hyperblock** goes further by replacing internal branches with predicated (guarded) instructions.
