## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Trace Scheduling Focus `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Trace Scheduling** algorithm in detail, with its three phases. Show how **compensation code** is generated for off-trace edges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following CFG with edge probabilities. Identify the **best trace** and list the **bookkeeping code** required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{B1} \xrightarrow{0.9} \text{B2} \xrightarrow{0.8} \text{B3} \xrightarrow{1.0} \text{B6} \\
&\text{B1} \xrightarrow{0.1} \text{B4} \xrightarrow{1.0} \text{B6} \\
&\text{B2} \xrightarrow{0.2} \text{B5} \xrightarrow{1.0} \text{B6} \\
&\text{B3} \xrightarrow{0.0} \text{B5} \quad (\text{dead edge})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Trace Scheduling — Detailed Working (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Trace Selection (2 marks)**
- The compiler performs profiling (instrumented runs) or uses static heuristics (e.g., loop branches, bias of conditional jumps).
- A trace $T$ is a sequence $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k$ maximizing $P(T) = \prod_{e \in T} p_e$ where $p_e$ is the edge probability.
- Example: in a loop, the *back-edge* and *taken* branches typically form the dominant trace.

**Step 2: Trace Compaction (2 marks)**
- All instructions in the trace are aggregated and dependencies are recomputed across former block boundaries.
- A **list scheduler** assigns cycles to instructions, prioritizing the **critical path** and respecting READ-AFTER-WRITE, WRITE-AFTER-READ, and WRITE-AFTER-WRITE hazards.
- Goal: minimize the **schedule length** (= total cycles of the trace).

**Step 3: Bookkeeping / Compensation (3 marks)**
- For every **off-trace edge** $e$ entering or leaving the trace:
  1. Identify the instructions whose original position is **after** their new position in the schedule, and whose data dependencies are violated along $e$.
  2. Insert **compensation copies** of these instructions at the entry of the off-trace block.
  3. Cost: $C_{\text{book}} = \sum_{e} w_e \cdot c_e$ where $w_e$ is the execution frequency of the edge and $c_e$ is the count of compensation instructions.
- Bookkeeping is **only** inserted if the move crosses a true data-dependence boundary — otherwise it is unnecessary.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Enumerate all source-to-sink paths.**

Path 1: $\text{B1} \to \text{B2} \to \text{B3} \to \text{B6}$
$$
P_1 = 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 = 0.72
$$

Path 2: $\text{B1} \to \text{B2} \to \text{B5} \to \text{B6}$
$$
P_2 = 0.9 \times 0.2 \times 1.0 = 0.18
$$

Path 3: $\text{B1} \to \text{B4} \to \text{B6}$
$$
P_3 = 0.1 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 0.10
$$

Total: $0.72 + 0.18 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark$

**Step 2: Select the trace with maximum probability.**
$$
\boxed{T = \text{B1} \to \text{B2} \to \text{B3} \to \text{B6}} \quad (P = 0.72)
$$

**Step 3: Identify off-trace edges entering/leaving the trace.**
- Off-trace edges *leaving* $T$: $\text{B2} \to \text{B5}$ (probability 0.2).
- Off-trace edges *entering* $T$: $\text{B1} \to \text{B4}$ (probability 0.1) leaves the trace at B1.

**Step 4: Generate bookkeeping code.**
- At the end of the rescheduled B2, the compiler must **emit a branch to B5** (or place a duplicated B2-tail + jump) to handle the B2→B5 edge.
- Similarly, just before B4 (or via duplication of B1's tail), insert a branch to B4 to handle the B1→B4 path.
- The B3→B5 edge has probability 0.0, so it is **dead** and needs no compensation.

**Final linearized code** (skeleton):

```
[ B1 (rescheduled) ][ B2 (rescheduled) ][ B3 (rescheduled) ][ B6 ]
        |                       |
        +-> B4 (bookkeeping)   +-> B5 (bookkeeping)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Computing path probabilities without verifying that they sum to **1.0**. If the student forgets this check, they will likely misidentify the trace. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Inserting compensation code for *every* off-trace edge regardless of whether an instruction was actually moved across a data dependence. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Confusing **Trace Scheduling** with **Basic Block Scheduling**. Trace scheduling operates *across* basic block boundaries — a student who restricts the schedule to a single block loses the core of the answer. **Loss: 3–4 marks.**

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Superblock & Hyperblock Focus `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Superblock Scheduling** technique. How does **tail duplication** eliminate side entrances, and what is its effect on **code size**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **if-conversion** in detail. Show how the following C-like code is converted to **predicated straight-line code** for execution as a **hyperblock**. Assume predicates are evaluated using `cmov`-like instructions. **(7 marks)**

```c
if (a > b)
    x = 1;
else
    x = -1;
y = x + 10;
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Superblock Scheduling — Detailed (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
A **superblock** is a single-entry, **no-side-entrance**, multiple-exit straight-line code region formed by *tail-duplicating* a portion of a trace.

**Step 2: Why Side Entrances Hurt (2 marks)**
- In a plain trace, an instruction may be moved *upward* in the schedule, but a side entrance may still execute the block *after* the move.
- This means the side-entrant edge could observe a value that has not yet been computed by the moved instruction — **semantic violation**.
- Trace scheduling requires a compensation block at *every* side entrance, leading to combinatorial code growth.

**Step 3: Tail Duplication (2 marks)**
- For each block $S$ that jumps into the *middle* of a trace, the suffix of the trace after the jump-in point is **duplicated** so that the side entrance now lands in its own copy of the suffix.
- The original suffix is reserved for the *primary* entry; the new copy serves the side entrance.
- Result: only the **head** of the superblock has incoming edges, and bookkeeping is needed only at the **exits** (not at every side entrance).

**Step 4: Code-Size Trade-off (2 marks)**
- Tail duplication **increases static code size** by the size of the duplicated suffix × number of side entrances.
- Empirical studies (HP PA-RISC, Multiflow TRACE) report **10–40% code bloat**, which is acceptable in I-cache-rich VLIW / EPIC systems where ILP gains far outweigh the size cost.
- For small I-cache systems, the gain may be neutralized by cache misses, so the technique is selectively applied to hot procedures.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**If-Conversion — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Original code (1 mark)**

```c
if (a > b)
    x = 1;
else
    x = -1;
y = x + 10;
```

This contains one conditional branch, which can be **converted to a predicate**.

**Step 2: Identify the predicate (2 marks)**

Define:
$$
p_1 = (a > b), \quad p_2 = \neg p_1 = (a \le b)
$$

**Step 3: Predicated straight-line code (2 marks)**

```asm
; Predicated execution (cmov-style)
p1 = (a > b)            ; compute predicate
p2 = !p1                ; complementary predicate
x = 1       if p1       ; conditional move: x := 1 if a > b
x = -1      if p2       ; conditional move: x := -1 if !(a > b)
y = x + 10             ; unconditional, follows both paths
```

**Step 4: Verify semantics (2 marks)**

- If $a > b$: $p_1 = \text{true}$, so $x = 1$ is committed; $p_2 = \text{false}$, so $x = -1$ is suppressed. $y = 11$. ✓
- If $a \le b$: $p_1 = \text{false}$, $p_2 = \text{true}$, $x = -1$ is committed. $y = 9$. ✓

The two predicated `x =` instructions are **executed unconditionally in the pipeline**, but only one writes to the architectural register — semantic equivalence with the original branch is preserved.

This is the **core of a hyperblock**: a single-entry, multiple-exit, **branch-free** region that the scheduler can treat as one giant straight-line block.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Failing to **negate the predicate** for the `else` branch — students often write `p2 = p1` instead of `p2 = !p1`. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Forgetting that **both** predicated moves execute on the hardware — they are *not* mutually exclusive in the pipeline. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Confusing **predicated execution** with **speculative execution**. Predicates are committed only if true; speculation may be squashed. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Not mentioning the **hardware prerequisite** (predication support in the target ISA). **Loss: 1 mark.**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 4 — Global Code Placement)**

- ☐ **Trace Scheduling = Trace Selection + Compaction + Bookkeeping** (Fisher, 1981).
- ☐ A **trace** is a **high-probability path** in the CFG — path probability is the **product of edge probabilities**.
- ☐ **Compensation code** is needed **only when an instruction crosses a true data-dependence edge** along an off-trace path.
- ☐ **Superblock** = trace + **tail duplication** to remove side entrances — fewer compensation blocks, but more code size.
- ☐ **Hyperblock** = single-entry, multiple-exit region where **if-conversion replaces branches with predicates**.
- ☐ **If-conversion** requires **predication hardware** (IA-64, ARM, GPU).
- ☐ **Code positioning** (e.g., Pettis-Hansen) is a complementary **block-level layout** technique that **maximizes fall-through probability** for hot edges.
- ☐ Global code placement reduces **branch misprediction penalty** and **I-cache miss rate**, improving **CPI** and overall **speedup**.
- ☐ Speedup formula: $S = \dfrac{C_{\text{unopt}}}{C_{\text{opt}}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f_b \cdot p_m \cdot C_{\text{pen}} / N}$.
- ☐ **Bookkeeping cost** is acceptable only if the trace is the **dominant path** — otherwise the duplication/compensation overhead neutralizes the gain.
- ☐ KTU expects: the **3-phase structure of trace scheduling** and the **differences among trace/superblock/hyperblock** in EVERY ESE attempt.
- ☐ Always **verify path probabilities sum to 1.0** — a famous valuation trick in numerical CFG problems.
- ☐ Modern LLVM/ GCC use **PGO + machine-block-layout** to implement the *Pettis-Hansen* algorithm in production.
